| 8-Puzzle reachable states | **181,440** (= $9!/2$) |
| 8-Puzzle avg branching | **~2.67** |
| 8-Puzzle optimal depth (avg) | **22** |
| 8-Puzzle optimal depth (max) | **31** |
| 8-Queens naive states | **4,426,165,368** (= $\binom{64}{8}$) |
| 8-Queens efficient states | **40,320** (= $8!$) |
| 8-Queens total solutions | **92** |
| 8-Queens fundamental solutions | **12** |

### 🔑 Algorithms to Know
- [ ] **BFS** for Vacuum World — finds optimal path with uniform cost.
- [ ] **A\* with Manhattan heuristic** for 8-Puzzle — optimal and efficient.
- [ ] **Backtracking with constraint propagation** for 8-Queens — minimum node visits (~2,057).

### ⚖️ Heuristic Comparison
- [ ] $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) ≤ $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) — **dominance** holds.
- [ ] Both $h_1$ and $h_2$ are **admissible** → A* is **optimal**.
- [ ] Manhattan distance is also **consistent** (monotonic) → no re-expansion in A*.

### 🚫 Common Traps
- [ ] Do NOT confuse state-space size with number of solutions.
- [ ] Do NOT apply heuristics to the Vacuum World (state space too small).
- [ ] Do NOT use uniform-cost search when BFS suffices (same path costs).
- [ ] Do NOT forget to specify the **one-queen-per-row** constraint in 8-Queens formulations.

### 📐 Engineering Real-World Connections
- [ ] Vacuum World → **Autonomous cleaning robots** (Roomba-class devices).
- [ ] 8-Puzzle → **Warehouse robot pathfinding** and **grid-based game AI**.
- [ ] 8-Queens → **VLSI circuit testing**, **parallel memory layouts**, **cryptographic permutations**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 1: Example Problems (Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens)**
> **PECST522 — Artificial Intelligence | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Searching: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Uninformed (Blind) Search — The Foundational Premise Before we analyze the three algorithms, it is critical to recognize that **Depth First Search (DFS)**, **Breadth First Search (BFS)**, and **Iterative Deepening Depth First Search (IDDFS)** belong to the family of **uninformed (blind) search strategies**. An uninformed search operates *without any domain-specific heuristic* — it has no idea how close it is to the goal, and therefore explores the state space in a mechanically predetermined order. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Uninformed search* uses only the information available in the problem definition, namely the branching factor ($b$), the depth of the shallowest goal ($d$), and the maximum depth of the state space ($m$). The choice of *which* node to expand next is governed entirely by the data structure used to store the frontier. The three strategies differ in exactly **one** dimension: *the order in which the frontier (the set of unexpanded leaf nodes) is maintained*. The frontier is a data structure whose access policy dictates the search order. | Strategy | Frontier Data Structure | Access Policy | |---|---|---| | **DFS** | **Stack (LIFO)** | Last-In, First-Out | | **BFS** | **Queue (FIFO)** | First-In, First-Out | | **IDDFS** | Stack with a hard depth cap, repeated for $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ | DFS repeated with growing limits | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogies — Plain English Explanations ### 1.2.1 Depth First Search (DFS) — "The Deep Diver" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine you are exploring a **caves** in total darkness. You enter the first tunnel and walk as far as you can. When the path splits, you pick one and go all the way to its end. Only when you hit a dead end do you back up — but only to the last junction — and try the *other* branch. You keep going deeper before coming back. You are, in effect, leaving a single trail of bread crumbs that you rewind whenever you hit a wall. DFS plunges down one path as deeply as possible before backtracking. It uses a **stack** (or recursion, which is implicit stack allocation). ### 1.2.2 Breadth First Search (BFS) — "The Lake Ripples" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Drop a pebble in a still pond. The ripples expand outward in perfect concentric rings. The first ring touches everything at distance 1, the second touches everything at distance 2, and so on. BFS inspects the start node's *all immediate neighbors* before looking at *any* second-level neighbor. It is exhaustive in width before committing to depth. BFS uses a **queue** to expand nodes in the order they were discovered. Because every edge has equal cost in an unweighted graph, BFS is guaranteed to find the **shortest path** (in terms of number of edges) to any reachable node. ### 1.2.3 Iterative Deepening Search (IDS) — "The Compromise Cave Explorer" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Take the cave explorer who dives deep (DFS), but with a strict rule: *you may only go $L$ meters deep before coming back out*. On your first expedition, $L = 1$ meter. You go in, hit the limit, and return. Next trip, $L = 2$. Then $L = 3$, and so on, until you find what you are looking for. You only ever carry a small map (a single path), yet you have explored layer by layer. IDDFS performs a sequence of **depth-limited DFS** calls with limits $0, 1, 2, \dots$ until the goal is found. It inherits the **completeness and optimality of BFS** while inheriting the **low memory footprint of DFS** — making it, in many textbooks (Russell & Norvig), the *preferred* uninformed search when the search tree is large and the solution depth is unknown. ## 1.3 The Tree Search Loop — Shared Skeleton All three algorithms are special cases of the **general tree search** algorithm. Only the *queueing function* (the rule used to insert successors into the frontier) changes. $$ \text{Frontier} \leftarrow \{\text{InitialState}\} $$ $$ \text{Loop:} \quad \text{If Frontier is empty} \Rightarrow \text{return Failure} $$ $$ \text{Node} \leftarrow \text{Pop from Frontier (per strategy-specific policy)} $$ $$ \text{If } \text{State}(Node) = \text{Goal} \Rightarrow \text{return Solution} $$ $$ \text{Expand Node, push its children to Frontier per strategy} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Graph Search vs. Tree Search:** In *tree search*, repeated states are revisited (wasting work). In *graph search*, we maintain a **closed list** (explored set) and refuse to re-add a node whose state has been seen. KTU questions frequently test whether you understand that **BFS with a closed list** becomes truly optimal in graphs with cycles. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Depth First Search (DFS) ### 2.1.1 Operational Logic DFS expands the **deepest unexpanded node** first. The frontier is implemented as a **LIFO stack** (or via recursive function call frames). When a node is expanded, its children are pushed onto the stack; the *last-pushed* child will be expanded first. > [!NOTE] > **Recursive Pseudocode (Russell & Norvig style):** > Function **DFS(node)**: >   If **node** is a goal, return the path to **node**. >   For each successor **s** of **node**, in some order: >     result $\leftarrow$ DFS(s) >     If result is not failure, return result. >   Return failure. ### 2.1.2 Properties of DFS | Property | Result | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Complete** | Yes (finite state space, cycle-free), or Yes with visited set | Every reachable node is eventually reached because the stack guarantees all branches are eventually popped. | | **Optimal** | **No** | DFS returns the *first* goal found in the stack's order, which can be much deeper than the shallowest goal. | | **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | In the worst case, DFS may explore the *entire* tree of maximum depth $m$ before finding the goal. | | **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ | Only the nodes on the *current path* (from root to current leaf) plus their unexpanded siblings need to be stored. | Where: - $b$ = branching factor (max children per node) - $m$ = maximum depth of the search tree - $d$ = depth of the shallowest solution ### 2.1.3 When to Choose DFS - The search space is **deep** and solutions are believed to lie *deep* in the tree. - The graph has a **high branching factor** but depth $m$ is bounded and modest. - **Memory is the bottleneck** (embedded systems, very deep trees where BFS would explode). - You do **not** need the shortest path. ## 2.2 Breadth First Search (BFS) ### 2.2.1 Operational Logic BFS expands the **shallowest unexpanded node** first, using a **FIFO queue**. It explores the search tree *level by level*: every node at depth $k$ is expanded before any node at depth $k+1$. > [!NOTE] > **Why BFS is Optimal for Unweighted Graphs:** Every edge has uniform cost. A node discovered at depth $k$ must be reached via a path of exactly $k$ edges. Since the algorithm expands by ascending depth, the *first* time a goal is dequeued, the path used to reach it is provably the shortest. Any other goal at depth $d' < d$ would already have been dequeued first. ### 2.2.2 Properties of BFS | Property | Result | |---|---| | **Complete** | **Yes** (if $b$ is finite) — the goal at depth $d$ will eventually be reached. | | **Optimal** | **Yes** (for uniform step cost). | | **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | | **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ — the **dominant** disadvantage; every node at the frontier must be stored. | ### 2.2.3 When to Choose BFS - The solution is expected to be **shallow** (small $d$). - You need the **shortest path** in an unweighted graph (e.g., minimum number of moves in a 15-puzzle, shortest route in a road network with uniform edge weights). - Memory is not a critical constraint. ## 2.3 Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS) ### 2.3.1 Operational Logic IDDFS calls **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** with limit $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ in sequence, until the goal is found. $$ \text{For } l = 0, 1, 2, \dots $$ $$ \quad \text{result} \leftarrow \text{DLS(initial, l)} $$ $$ \quad \text{If result } \neq \text{cutoff}, \text{ return result} $$ A *cutoff* indicates the depth limit was hit without finding a goal or a failure. *Failure* means the entire subtree was exhausted with no goal. ### 2.3.2 Why IDDFS is Clever — The Wastefulness Argument A natural objection is: *"Doesn't IDDFS re-expand upper-level nodes many times?"* Yes — but the re-expansions are geometrically insignificant. - Nodes at depth $k$ are generated **once** by the call with $l = k$. - They are *re-generated* by later calls with $l > k$, but only as overhead. - The total number of nodes generated in the limit-$l$ call is $$ N(l) = b^0 + b^1 + b^2 + \cdots + b^l $$ - The *total* nodes generated across all calls (target depth $d$) is $$ N_{\text{IDS}} = (d+1) \cdot b^0 + d \cdot b^1 + (d-1) \cdot b^2 + \cdots + 1 \cdot b^d $$ The leading term is dominated by $b^d$, so the asymptotic complexity remains $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, identical to BFS. Yet memory usage is $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$, identical to DFS. ### 2.3.3 Properties of IDDFS | Property | Result | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Complete** | **Yes** | Goal at finite depth $d$ will be found when $l = d$. | | **Optimal** | **Yes** (uniform step cost) | Same argument as BFS, layered on top of the depth limit. | | **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | Asymptotically identical to BFS (with constant factor of ~$(b+1)/(b-1)$ overhead). | | **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | Only the current path is stored, like DFS. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table — it is the **single most frequently asked** content in KTU Module 2 questions on uninformed search. | Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | |---|---|---|---|---| | **DFS** | No (cycle risk) / Yes (with visited) | No | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(bm)$ | | **BFS** | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | | **IDDFS** | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(bd)$ | | **Uniform-Cost Search (for context)** | Yes | Yes (general cost) | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** BFS underlies *shortest-path routing* in network protocols, *garbage collection* (mark-and-sweep over object graphs), and *social network friend-of-friend* queries. DFS underlies *cycle detection* in compilers, *topological sorting* in build systems, *maze generation*, and *puzzle solvers* (Sudoku, N-Queens). IDDFS is the *gold standard* for game-tree search when depth is unknown and memory is tight — it is conceptually similar to how iterative deepening is applied in chess engines. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace, and Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Trace on a Canonical Sample Tree Consider the following search tree, where **S** is the start node and **G** is the goal. Numbers in parentheses indicate the *order in which each algorithm expands* the nodes. $$ \text{Tree structure:} $$ $$ S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow H \quad (leaf) $$ $$ A \rightarrow D \quad (leaf) $$ $$ S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow I \rightarrow G \quad (goal) $$ $$ B \rightarrow F \quad (leaf) $$ A more compact notation: $S$ has children $A, B$; $A$ has children $C, D$; $C$ has child $H$; $B$ has children $E, F$; $E$ has child $I$; $I$ has child $G$. ### 3.1.1 DFS Trace (Stack / LIFO) Assume children are pushed in **alphabetical order**, so the *last* child pushed is the *first* popped. | Step | Action | Stack (top = right) | Expanded | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Push root | $[S]$ | — | | 2 | Pop $S$, push children $A, B$ | $[A, B]$ | $S$ | | 3 | Pop $B$ (last pushed), push $E, F$ | $[A, E, F]$ | $S, B$ | | 4 | Pop $F$, no children | $[A, E]$ | $S, B, F$ | | 5 | Pop $E$, push $I$ | $[A, I]$ | $S, B, F, E$ | | 6 | Pop $I$, push $G$ | $[A, G]$ | $S, B, F, E, I$ | | 7 | Pop $G$ — **GOAL FOUND** | — | $S, B, F, E, I, G$ | **Goal found at depth 4, but DFS visited $F$ (a useless leaf) along the way.** This illustrates non-optimality: the path $S \to B \to E \to I \to G$ is the *only* path here, but in a deeper tree, DFS could find a goal at depth 100 when a goal exists at depth 5. ### 3.1.2 BFS Trace (Queue / FIFO) | Step | Action | Queue (front = left) | Visited/Goal | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Enqueue $S$ | $[S]$ | — | | 2 | Dequeue $S$, enqueue children $A, B$ | $[A, B]$ | $S$ | | 3 | Dequeue $A$, enqueue $C, D$ | $[B, C, D]$ | $S, A$ | | 4 | Dequeue $B$, enqueue $E, F$ | $[C, D, E, F]$ | $S, A, B$ | | 5 | Dequeue $C$, enqueue $H$ | $[D, E, F, H]$ | $S, A, B, C$ | | 6 | Dequeue $D$ (no children) | $[E, F, H]$ | $S, A, B, C, D$ | | 7 | Dequeue $E$, enqueue $I$ | $[F, H, I]$ | $S, A, B, C, D, E$ | | 8 | Dequeue $F$ (no children) | $[H, I]$ | $S, A, B, C, D, E, F$ | | 9 | Dequeue $H$ (no children) | $[I]$ | $S, A, B, C, D, E, F, H$ | | 10 | Dequeue $I$, enqueue $G$ | $[G]$ | $S, A, B, C, D, E, F, H, I$ | | 11 | Dequeue $G$ — **GOAL FOUND** | — | $S, A, B, C, D, E, F, H, I, G$ | > [!NOTE] > In BFS, notice that **every node at depth 0, 1, 2 is processed before any node at depth 3** — this is the *level-by-level* signature. If a goal existed at depth 2, BFS would find it in step 5 or 6, not after exhausting deeper levels. ### 3.1.3 IDDFS Trace | Iteration $l$ | DLS visits (in order) | Goal? | |---|---|---| | 0 | $S$ | No | | 1 | $S, A, B$ | No | | 2 | $S, A, C, D, B, E, F$ | No | | 3 | $S, A, C, H, D, B, E, I, F$ | No | | 4 | $S, A, C, H, D, B, E, I, G$ | **Yes** | The upper-level nodes ($S, A, C, D, B, E$) are *revisited* in each iteration, but the total work is dominated by the final iteration at $l = d$. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: IDDFS Overhead Ratio **Goal:** Show that IDDFS's total work is at most a small constant multiple of BFS's work. Let $T_{\text{IDS}}(d)$ denote the total number of nodes generated by IDDFS up to finding a goal at depth $d$. The number generated in the $l$-th iteration is the size of a complete tree of depth $l$: $$ N_l = 1 + b + b^2 + \cdots + b^l = \frac{b^{l+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ The total work is the sum of these geometric series, weighted by how many iterations each node participates in: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) = \sum_{l=0}^{d} N_l = \sum_{l=0}^{d} \frac{b^{l+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ Pull the constant $(b-1)^{-1}$ out: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) = \frac{1}{b-1} \sum_{l=0}^{d} \left( b^{l+1} - 1 \right) $$ Split the sum: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) = \frac{1}{b-1} \left[ \sum_{l=0}^{d} b^{l+1} - \sum_{l=0}^{d} 1 \right] $$ Evaluate the second sum: $$ \sum_{l=0}^{d} 1 = d + 1 $$ For the first sum, factor out $b$: $$ \sum_{l=0}^{d} b^{l+1} = b \sum_{l=0}^{d} b^l = b \cdot \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ Substitute: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) = \frac{1}{b-1} \left[ b \cdot \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} - (d+1) \right] $$ Combine into a single fraction: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) = \frac{b \cdot (b^{d+1} - 1) - (d+1)(b-1)}{(b-1)^2} $$ As $d \to \infty$, the dominant term in the numerator is $b^{d+2}$, and the denominator is $(b-1)^2$. Therefore: $$ T_{\text{IDS}}(d) \sim \frac{b^{d+2}}{(b-1)^2} = b^d \cdot \frac{b^2}{(b-1)^2} $$ Compare to BFS, which generates $N_d = (b^{d+1}-1)/(b-1) \sim b^{d+1}/(b-1)$. The **overhead ratio** is: $$ \frac{T_{\text{IDS}}(d)}{N_d} \sim \frac{b^{d+2}/(b-1)^2}{b^{d+1}/(b-1)} = \frac{b}{b-1} $$ For $b = 10$ (a typical branching factor in chess-like games), this ratio is $10/9 \approx 1.11$, i.e., **IDDFS does only 11% more work than BFS in the limit**, but uses $b^d$-times less memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This is *why* iterative deepening is the canonical choice for two-player adversarial game search (e.g., chess, checkers): you get the optimality and completeness of BFS, the memory efficiency of DFS, and the asymptotic cost penalty is negligible. ## 3.3 State-Space Graph vs. Search Tree — Why the Distinction Matters > [!CAUTION] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** A *state-space graph* may have cycles. A *search tree* does not (each state is a fresh node). When asked about **completeness** in a graph, the answer depends on whether you use a **closed list** (graph search) or not (tree search). - **Tree search** with DFS on a graph with cycles → **may loop forever**, hence *incomplete*. - **Graph search** with DFS (closed list maintained) → *complete on finite graphs*. - **BFS with a closed list** in a *uniform-cost graph* → complete and optimal. - **IDDFS with a closed list** (or with iterative limit) → complete and optimal for finite uniform-cost graphs. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sample Search Tree (Used for Trace) The following Mermaid diagram shows the canonical tree used in Section 3, with traversal-order annotations for DFS, BFS, and IDDFS. ```mermaid graph TD S((S
Start)) --> A1((A)) S --> B1((B)) A1 --> C1((C)) A1 --> D1((D)) C1 --> H1((H)) B1 --> E1((E)) B1 --> F1((F)) E1 --> I1((I)) I1 --> G1((G
GOAL)) style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style A1 fill:#E0F0FF style B1 fill:#E0F0FF style C1 fill:#FFE0E0 style D1 fill:#FFE0E0 style E1 fill:#E0FFE0 style F1 fill:#E0FFE0 style H1 fill:#F0E0FF style I1 fill:#F0E0FF ``` ## 4.2 DFS vs. BFS — Architectural Flow Comparison The two strategies differ only in the *type* of frontier data structure. This block diagram shows the unified control flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Init[Initialization Phase] I1[Initialize FRONTIER] I2[FRONTIER = Stack or Queue] I3[Push S into FRONTIER] I1 --> I2 --> I3 end subgraph Loop[Main Search Loop] L1{Is FRONTIER empty?} L2[Pop / Dequeue NODE from FRONTIER] L3{Is NODE state equal to GOAL?} L4[Return SOLUTION path to NODE] L5[Generate successors of NODE] L6{Frontier type} L7[Push successors to STACK LIFO] L8[Enqueue successors to QUEUE FIFO] L9[Mark NODE as EXPLORED closed list] L1 -- No --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 -- Yes --> L4 L3 -- No --> L9 L9 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 -- DFS --> L7 L6 -- BFS --> L8 L7 --> L1 L8 --> L1 L1 -- Yes --> L10[Return FAILURE] end Init --> Loop ``` ## 4.3 IDDFS — Outer Loop Around Depth-Limited DFS ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start IDDFS]) --> Init[depth = 0] Init --> Call[Call DLS Root depth] Call --> DLSExec[Depth Limited Search with limit L] DLSExec --> Check{Result type} Check -- Goal Found --> Success([Return SOLUTION]) Check -- Failure no goal anywhere --> Fail([Return FAILURE]) Check -- Cutoff limit reached --> Inc[Increment depth by 1] Inc --> Call style Start fill:#FFD700 style Success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style Fail fill:#FF6B6B style DLSExec fill:#E0F0FF ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Legend[Notation] N1[b = branching factor] N2[d = depth of shallowest goal] N3[m = maximum depth of tree] end subgraph Comparison[Algorithm Property Map] A1[DFS] A2[BFS] A3[IDDFS] A1 --- P1[Complete No or with closed] A2 --- P2[Complete Yes] A3 --- P3[Complete Yes] A1 --- Q1[Optimal No] A2 --- Q2[Optimal Yes uniform cost] A3 --- Q3[Optimal Yes uniform cost] A1 --- R1[Time Obm] A2 --- R2[Time Obd] A3 --- R3[Time Obd] A1 --- S1[Space Obm] A2 --- S2[Space Obd] A3 --- S3[Space Obd] end style A1 fill:#FFE0E0 style A2 fill:#E0FFE0 style A3 fill:#E0F0FF style P1 fill:#FFF5E0 style Q1 fill:#FFF5E0 style R1 fill:#FFF5E0 style S1 fill:#FFF5E0 style P2 fill:#FFF5E0 style Q2 fill:#FFF5E0 style R2 fill:#FFF5E0 style S2 fill:#FFF5E0 style P3 fill:#FFF5E0 style Q3 fill:#FFF5E0 style R3 fill:#FFF5E0 style S3 fill:#FFF5E0 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Breadth First Search and Depth First Search with respect to the data structure used, completeness, optimality, and space complexity.** **Model Answer:** Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) are uninformed search strategies that differ primarily in the order in which they expand nodes. BFS uses a **FIFO queue** as its frontier, expanding the shallowest unexpanded node first; this leads to a level-by-level exploration of the search tree. DFS uses a **LIFO stack**, expanding the deepest unexpanded node first and backtracking only when a dead end is reached. In terms of **completeness**, BFS is complete in finite state spaces because every node at shallower depths is explored before deeper ones, so any reachable goal is eventually found. DFS, in its basic tree-search form, is **not complete** in graphs that contain cycles — it can loop forever. However, when augmented with a **visited set** (graph search), DFS becomes complete in finite graphs. Regarding **optimality**, BFS is **optimal** in graphs where every step has uniform cost, because the first goal it dequeues must be at the minimum depth. DFS, in contrast, is **not optimal** — it returns the first goal encountered in its depth-first dive, which may be much deeper than the shallowest goal. The **space complexity** of BFS is $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, where $b$ is the branching factor and $d$ is the depth of the shallowest solution, because it must store every node in the frontier. DFS has a much lower space complexity of $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$, where $m$ is the maximum depth of the search tree, because only the nodes on the current path plus their unexpanded siblings are stored. *(3 marks — 1 mark for data structure, 1 mark for completeness + optimality distinction, 1 mark for space complexity.)* ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is Iterative Deepening Search preferred over BFS and DFS in certain problem domains? Justify with its complexity measures.** **Model Answer:** Iterative Deepening Depth First Search (IDDFS) is preferred over BFS and DFS in problem domains where the depth of the solution is unknown a priori and memory is constrained, because it inherits the **strengths of both strategies** while mitigating their weaknesses. IDDFS performs a series of depth-limited DFS calls with limits $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ until the goal is found. It is **complete** (because the depth limit eventually reaches $d$, the depth of the shallowest goal) and **optimal for uniform step costs** (because it expands nodes in order of increasing depth, exactly like BFS). Its time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, which is asymptotically identical to BFS; the constant-factor overhead is bounded by a ratio of $b/(b-1)$, which is negligible (e.g., for $b = 10$, only 11% extra work). Critically, its **space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$**, identical to DFS — many orders of magnitude smaller than BFS's $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$. Therefore, in domains like two-player game-tree search (chess, checkers) where the search tree has a large branching factor but the goal depth is unknown and memory is finite, IDDFS gives the *best of both worlds* — BFS-quality completeness and optimality with DFS-level memory efficiency. *(3 marks — 1 mark for inheritance argument, 1 mark for complexity comparison, 1 mark for application domain.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (Option 1): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the Breadth First Search algorithm with a suitable example. Show the order in which nodes are visited and discuss its completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain Depth First Search with an example. Under what conditions is DFS complete? When is it preferred over BFS? **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution — BFS **Algorithm Description:** Breadth First Search explores the search tree level by level, expanding all nodes at depth $k$ before any node at depth $k+1$. The frontier is maintained as a **FIFO queue**, ensuring that the *oldest* (shallowest) unexpanded node is processed first. We also maintain a *closed list* of visited states to avoid revisiting nodes in graph search. **Worked Example:** Consider the tree from Section 3, with $S$ as start and $G$ as goal, with children alphabetized. Step-by-step BFS trace: | Step | Queue (front→rear) | Node Expanded | |---|---|---| | 1 | $[S]$ | — | | 2 | $[A, B]$ | $S$ | | 3 | $[B, C, D]$ | $A$ | | 4 | $[C, D, E, F]$ | $B$ | | 5 | $[D, E, F, H]$ | $C$ | | 6 | $[E, F, H]$ | $D$ | | 7 | $[F, H, I]$ | $E$ | | 8 | $[H, I]$ | $F$ | | 9 | $[I]$ | $H$ | | 10 | $[G]$ | $I$ | | 11 | $[]$ | $G$ ← **GOAL** | **Order of expansion:** $S, A, B, C, D, E, F, H, I, G$. **Complexity Analysis:** - **Completeness:** BFS is **complete** in any finite state space, provided the branching factor $b$ is finite. Every node at depth $d$ is eventually reached because the queue guarantees all shallower nodes are processed first. - **Optimality:** BFS is **optimal** in unweighted (uniform-cost) graphs. Any goal at depth $d$ is guaranteed to be the shallowest, because all paths to it have length $d$, and BFS expands in order of increasing depth. - **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ — in the worst case, BFS generates all nodes at depths $0$ through $d$. - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ — the entire frontier at depth $d$ (which can be roughly $b^d$ nodes) must be stored simultaneously. --- #### (b) Model Solution — DFS **Algorithm Description:** Depth First Search expands the deepest unexpanded node first, using a **LIFO stack** (or recursion). When a node has multiple successors, the most recently generated is expanded first; when it leads to a dead end, the algorithm backtracks to the most recent node with an unexplored alternative. **Worked Example (using the same tree):** DFS trace with children alphabetized and the *last* child expanded first: | Step | Stack (top→bottom) | Node Expanded | |---|---|---| | 1 | $[S]$ | — | | 2 | $[A, B]$ | $S$ | | 3 | $[A, E, F]$ | $B$ | | 4 | $[A, E]$ | $F$ | | 5 | $[A, I]$ | $E$ | | 6 | $[A, G]$ | $I$ | | 7 | $[A]$ | $G$ ← **GOAL** | **Order of expansion:** $S, B, F, E, I, G$. **Conditions for Completeness:** - DFS is **complete in tree search** if the search tree is finite (no cycles in the underlying state-space graph). - DFS is **complete in graph search** if a *closed list* (visited set) is maintained to prevent re-exploring already-visited states. - Without a closed list, DFS can loop forever in cyclic graphs (e.g., a graph with the edge $A \to B \to A$). **When DFS is Preferred over BFS:** - The solution is expected to lie **deep** in the search tree. - The branching factor is high but the **depth** is moderate. - **Memory is the bottleneck** — DFS uses $\mathcal{O}(bm)$ space vs BFS's $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, which is decisive in embedded systems, large trees, or game-tree search. - The exact path length does not matter — only existence of a solution. - Examples: cycle detection in a graph, topological sorting, solving puzzles like the N-Queens problem, parsing, and maze exploration. --- ### Question B (Option 2): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** What is Iterative Deepening Search? Explain with a depth-limited search algorithm and demonstrate it on a search tree of depth 4. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare BFS, DFS, and IDDFS in terms of completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. Why is IDDFS often called the "best of both worlds"? **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution — Iterative Deepening Search **Definition:** Iterative Deepening Depth First Search (IDDFS) is an uninformed search strategy that repeatedly applies **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** with progressively increasing depth limits: $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$, until the goal is found or failure is confirmed. **Depth-Limited Search Algorithm:** ``` function DLS(node, limit): if node.state == GOAL: return SOLUTION(path to node) if limit == 0: return CUTOFF cutoff_occurred = false for each successor s of node: result = DLS(s, limit - 1) if result == CUTOFF: cutoff_occurred = true elif result != FAILURE: return result if cutoff_occurred: return CUTOFF else: return FAILURE ``` **Iterative Deepening Driver:** ``` function IDDFS(start): for depth = 0, 1, 2, ... : result = DLS(start, depth) if result == CUTOFF: continue else: return result // SOLUTION or FAILURE ``` **Demonstration on the Sample Tree (target depth $d = 4$):** - **Iteration $l = 0$:** DLS expands $S$ only. Cutoff (no goal at depth 0). Sequence: $S$. - **Iteration $l = 1$:** DLS expands $S, A, B$ (children at depth 1). Cutoff. Sequence: $S, A, B$. - **Iteration $l = 2$:** DLS expands $S \to A \to C, D$; then $S \to B \to E, F$. Sequence: $S, A, C, D, B, E, F$. Cutoff. - **Iteration $l = 3$:** DLS expands $S \to A \to C \to H$; $S \to A \to D$; $S \to B \to E \to I$; $S \to B \to F$. Sequence: $S, A, C, H, D, B, E, I, F$. Cutoff. - **Iteration $l = 4$:** DLS expands $S \to A \to C \to H \to \text{(no children)}$; $S \to A \to D \to \text{(no children)}$; $S \to B \to E \to I \to G$. **Goal $G$ found.** Sequence: $S, A, C, H, D, B, E, I, G$. **Return solution.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Re-expansions:** Nodes like $S, A, B$ are *re-expanded* in every iteration. This is the perceived wastefulness, but as proved in Section 3, the overhead ratio is bounded by $b/(b-1)$. --- #### (b) Model Solution — Comparative Analysis **Side-by-Side Comparison Table:** | Criterion | DFS | BFS | IDDFS | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Structure** | Stack (LIFO) | Queue (FIFO) | Stack with depth limit, repeated | | **Completeness** | No (tree search) / Yes (graph search with closed list) | Yes | Yes | | **Optimality** | No | Yes (uniform cost) | Yes (uniform cost) | | **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | | **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(bm)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(bd)$ | Where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = maximum depth of tree. **Why IDDFS is the "Best of Both Worlds":** 1. **Optimality & Completeness from BFS:** Because IDDFS expands nodes in order of increasing depth limit, the first goal it discovers must be at the minimum possible depth, exactly as BFS guarantees. Thus it inherits BFS's completeness and optimality for uniform-cost graphs. 2. **Memory Efficiency from DFS:** IDDFS uses only a stack of depth at most $d$, so its space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(bd)$, identical to plain DFS. This is the critical advantage over BFS, whose $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ space requirement is often infeasible. 3. **Asymptotically Optimal Time:** Despite re-expanding upper-level nodes, IDDFS's time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, the same as BFS. The constant overhead is $b/(b-1)$, which converges rapidly to 1 for moderate $b$. 4. **Solution Depth Independence:** Unlike DFS, IDDFS does not need a predetermined maximum depth. Unlike BFS, it does not need to know $d$ in advance. For these reasons, IDDFS is the **canonical choice for uninformed search** when the search tree is large, the depth of the solution is unknown, and memory is limited — the textbook example being two-player game-tree search in chess-like AI. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Most Frequently Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. **Confusing tree search with graph search:** A common mistake is to assert "DFS is complete" without specifying whether a closed list is maintained. In tree search on a cyclic state-space graph, DFS is *not* complete. Examiners specifically deduct for failing to mention the closed list (visited set) mechanism. > 2. **Wrong space complexity for BFS:** Many students write $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ for BFS *time* but confuse the space complexity with the *height* of the tree. The correct space is dominated by the *frontier at the deepest explored level*, which holds up to $b^d$ nodes. > 3. **Mixing up $d$ and $m$:** $d$ is the depth of the **shallowest** solution; $m$ is the depth of the **deepest** node in the search tree. DFS time is $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ (it may explore the entire tree); BFS time is $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ (it stops at the first goal depth). > 4. **Claiming IDDFS has worse time complexity than BFS:** Examiners expect the student to *prove* that the asymptotic time is identical ($\mathcal{O}(b^d)$), not merely state it. A worked-out geometric series derivation is the hallmark of a top-mark answer. > 5. **Notational carelessness in trace tables:** Showing the *queue* or *stack* state at every step is mandatory for full marks. Skipping intermediate steps is penalized. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Searching Strategies (DFS, BFS, IDDFS)** > - **Uninformed search** uses no heuristic; choices are dictated by the frontier data structure. > - **DFS** = LIFO stack (or recursion), explores depth-first. **Incomplete** in cyclic tree search, **not optimal**, time $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$, space $\mathcal{O}(bm)$. > - **BFS** = FIFO queue, explores level-by-level. **Complete** and **optimal** for uniform cost, time $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, space $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$. > - **IDDFS** = DFS repeated with limits $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$. **Complete**, **optimal** for uniform cost, time $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, space $\mathcal{O}(bd)$. > - The **closed list** (explored set) is the key distinction between tree search and graph search; without it, DFS may cycle in a graph. > - IDDFS is preferred when **depth of solution is unknown** and **memory is limited** — the textbook example being game-tree search. > - The **overhead ratio** of IDDFS over BFS is bounded by $b/(b-1)$, which approaches 1 for moderate branching factors. > - BFS guarantees the **shortest path** (fewest edges) in an unweighted graph because it expands in increasing order of path length. > - DFS uses a *single* path plus siblings; BFS uses the *entire* frontier; IDDFS uses only a *single* path with a depth bound. > - In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, always **state the data structure**, **show the trace**, and **quote the complexity** in Big-O notation with explicit definition of $b$, $d$, $m$. > - **Mnemonic:** "DFS dives, BFS breathes (expands outward), IDS is the disciplined diver who comes back to the surface after $L$ meters, then dives again deeper."

topics.title: Heuristic Search Strategies: Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heuristic Search Strategies: Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: The Heuristic Function

In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECST522 – Artificial Intelligence)**, a **heuristic function**, denoted **h(n)**, is a function that maps any node **n** in the state-space search graph to a non-negative real number representing an **estimate of the minimum cost required to reach a goal state from node n**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Standard Definition (Russell & Norvig, AIMA)**
> A heuristic function **h(n)** is an *informed estimate* of the cost of the cheapest path from the current state **n** to the nearest goal state. The "informed" nature of the function — derived from domain-specific knowledge — is what distinguishes heuristic search from uninformed (blind) search strategies like Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Uniform-Cost Search (UCS).

The three cost functions that govern the **A\*** and **Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)** algorithms are:

$$g(n) = \text{actual cost from the start state to node } n$$

$$h(n) = \text{estimated cost from node } n \text{ to the goal state}$$

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) = \text{estimated total cost of the cheapest solution through } n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Role of h(n) in Informed Search**
> - In **Greedy Best-First Search**, the expansion order is governed *purely* by h(n). It tries to move the search "toward" the goal as quickly as possible, but it is neither optimal nor complete in the worst case.
> - In **A\* Search**, both the path cost g(n) and the heuristic h(n) jointly determine the expansion order, leading to the celebrated property of *admissibility* — A* is guaranteed to return an optimal solution if h(n) is admissible.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Map-and-Ruler Analogy"

Imagine you are a tourist in an unfamiliar city, standing at the town square, and you need to reach a famous cathedral whose exact address you do not know.

- The **cathedral** is the **goal state**.
- The **streets and intersections** are the **state-space graph**.
- The **actual walking distance** along the streets (following one-way rules, no-crossing signs) is the **true cost h*(n)**.
- The **straight-line distance** measured "as the crow flies" using a ruler on the map is the **heuristic h(n)**.

The straight-line distance is *always less than or equal to* the actual road distance (you cannot walk through a building in a straight line on a city map). This single property — "the estimate never overshoots reality" — is precisely the definition of an **admissible heuristic**.

A good heuristic is therefore like having a *trustworthy ruler*: the more accurately the ruler measures (the closer h(n) is to the true h*(n)), the fewer intersections you will explore, the less backtracking you will do, and the faster you will find the cathedral.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students**
> Think of **h(n)** as the *H*onest guide: it tells the search algorithm how far the goal "looks like" it is, even though the exact terrain is unknown. An *inadmissible* heuristic is a *liar* — it over-promises and misleads the search, sometimes leading to non-optimal solutions.

---

## 1.3 Why Heuristic Accuracy Matters — A High-Level Preview

The central thesis of this lecture topic is:

> **The accuracy of the heuristic function h(n) is the single most important design lever in informed search. A more accurate heuristic expands fewer nodes, runs faster, and consumes less memory, without sacrificing the optimality guarantees of the algorithm (provided admissibility is preserved).**

This is quantified by the **Effective Branching Factor (b\*)**, a metric borrowed from information-theoretic analysis of search trees. We will develop this metric rigorously in Sections 2 and 3.

---

## 1.4 Heuristic Function — Quick Reference Table

| Property | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Importance in KTU Exams |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heuristic estimate | $h(n)$ | Estimated cost from n to goal | Fundamental |
| Path cost so far | $g(n)$ | Actual cost from start to n | Used in A* |
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via n | Order of expansion in A* |
| True optimal cost | $h^{*}(n)$ | True minimum cost to goal | Used to define admissibility |
| Admissibility | $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | Never overestimates | Ensures optimality of A* |
| Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality holds | Ensures graph-search optimality |
| Dominance | $h_{2}(n) \ge h_{1}(n) \; \forall n$ | h₂ is more accurate | h₂ expands ≤ nodes than h₁ |
| Effective branching factor | $b^{*}$ | Empirical quality measure | Lower is better |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of a "Good" Heuristic Function

A heuristic function is not merely *any* estimate. For the search algorithm to behave correctly, the heuristic must satisfy mathematical properties. The two most critical properties in the KTU 2024 syllabus are **admissibility** and **consistency**.

### 2.1.1 Admissibility (Optimism)

A heuristic **h(n)** is called **admissible** if, for every node **n**, the estimated cost is bounded above by the true minimum cost to a goal:

$$0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

In words: **h(n) never overestimates** the true remaining cost. It is said to be *optimistic* because it always believes the goal is at most as far as the real cost suggests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility ⇒ Optimality of A\***
> If h(n) is admissible, then A* search (using TREE-SEARCH) is guaranteed to expand nodes in non-decreasing order of f(n) = g(n) + h(n), and the first goal node it pops from the frontier is guaranteed to be an **optimal solution**.

### 2.1.2 Consistency (Monotonicity)

A heuristic **h(n)** is called **consistent** (or **monotonic**) if, for every node **n** and every successor **n'** of **n** reached by action **a** with step-cost **c(n, a, n')**, the following triangle inequality holds:

$$h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

This is the discrete analogue of the triangle inequality from geometry: "the estimated cost from n to the goal is at most the cost of one step to n' plus the estimated cost from n' to the goal."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Consistency Matters**
> Consistency is a *stronger* condition than admissibility. Every consistent heuristic is admissible, but the converse is not necessarily true. For **GRAPH-SEARCH** (where repeated states are detected), consistency is the property that guarantees A* will not re-expand a node after it has been closed, preserving optimality and efficiency. This distinction between TREE-SEARCH and GRAPH-SEARCH is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

### 2.1.3 Dominance

Given two admissible heuristics **h₁(n)** and **h₂(n)** defined on the same problem, we say **h₂ dominates h₁** if:

$$h_{2}(n) \ge h_{1}(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

When h₂ dominates h₁, then for every node n, the A* algorithm using h₂ will expand a **subset** (or equal set) of the nodes expanded by A* using h₁. Hence, h₂ is always at least as efficient as h₁, and strictly more efficient when the inequality is strict for some n.

---

## 2.2 The Effective Branching Factor (b*) — The Quality Metric

The **Effective Branching Factor** is the single most important empirical measure of heuristic quality in the KTU syllabus. It abstracts the complexity of an irregular search tree into a single number that is comparable across heuristics.

### 2.2.1 Definition

The effective branching factor **b\*** is defined as the branching factor of a *uniform* tree (every non-leaf node has exactly b* children) that would expand the same total number of nodes **N** to reach a solution at depth **d**:

$$N + 1 \;=\; 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{3} + \dots + (b^{*})^{d}$$

The "+1" on the left accounts for the root node; the right side is a finite geometric series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Heuristic Accuracy ↔ b*)**
> - $b^{*} = 1$ ⇒ the heuristic is *perfect* (no wasted search, the algorithm walks straight to the goal).
> - $b^{*} \approx$ branching factor of the underlying graph ⇒ the heuristic provides *no useful information*; search degrades to uninformed search.
> - The **closer b* is to 1**, the **more accurate** the heuristic.
> - A well-designed heuristic typically achieves $1 \le b^{*} \le 1.5$ on hard problems; reductions of even 0.1 in b* can translate to **orders of magnitude** fewer node expansions.

### 2.2.2 Interpretation of b*

A useful comparative rule of thumb for KTU exam purposes:

| Effective Branching Factor $b^{*}$ | Interpretation | Node Expansions to Depth 10 |
|---|---|---|
| $1.0$ | Perfect heuristic | $\approx 11$ |
| $1.2$ | Excellent heuristic | $\approx 26$ |
| $1.5$ | Very good heuristic | $\approx 173$ |
| $2.0$ | Fair heuristic | $\approx 2047$ |
| $3.0$ | Poor heuristic (close to uninformed) | $\approx 88,573$ |

This table makes the *exponential* payoff of even modest improvements in heuristic accuracy starkly visible.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Constraints / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* evaluation function | Costs in problem-specific units (km, steps, etc.) |
| 2 | $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | Admissibility condition | Non-negative real numbers |
| 3 | $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ | Consistency condition | Triangle inequality on costs |
| 4 | $N + 1 = \dfrac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$ | Closed-form of effective branching factor | $b^{*} > 1$, depth $d$, total nodes $N$ |
| 5 | $(b^{*})^{d+1} \approx N \cdot (b^{*} - 1) + 1$ | Iterative approximation for $b^{*}$ | Used in practice; $b^*$ solved by bisection |
| 6 | $h_{2}(n) \ge h_{1}(n) \Rightarrow N_{2} \le N_{1}$ | Dominance → fewer nodes | Equality iff $h_{1} \equiv h_{2}$ |
| 7 | $f\text{-contour} = \{n : f(n) \le C\}$ | A* expands nodes within f-contour of optimal cost | Used in proof of A* optimality |
| 8 | $h_{\text{relaxed}}(n) = h^{*}(n)$ | Perfect heuristic from relaxed problem | Theorem: every relaxed problem gives an admissible heuristic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall: The Pipe Symbol Trap**
> In KTU answer scripts, when you write the consistency condition, **always use** $h(n) \le c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ **with the explicit "less than or equal to" symbol**. Avoid abbreviating the absolute value with a vertical bar in a way that can be misread as the markdown table separator.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heuristic search is not a theoretical curiosity — it is the engine behind:

- **GPS Navigation Systems (Google Maps, HERE Maps)**: Heuristic = straight-line or precomputed geodesic distance; search = A* on road networks.
- **Robotics Path Planning (ROS, autonomous vehicles)**: Heuristic = Euclidean distance in occupancy grids; search = A* or D* Lite.
- **Game Playing Engines (Chess, Go)**: Heuristic = static board evaluation function; search = minimax with α–β pruning (h(n) here is the leaf-node estimate).
- **VLSI Chip Layout and Routing**: Heuristic = Manhattan distance to nearest pin; search = A* for wire routing.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain (FedEx, Amazon)**: Heuristic = great-circle distance between distribution centers; search = A* for vehicle routing.
- **AI Planning (PDDL planners)**: Heuristic = delete-relaxation heuristics ($h_{max}$, $h_{add}$, $h_{FF}$); search = enforced hill-climbing or A*.

The accuracy of h(n) in each of these domains directly determines **time-to-solution, memory footprint, and quality of the plan returned** — a fact that KTU examiners love to test as an application-oriented question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Branching Factor Equation

We start from the combinatorial structure of a **uniform tree** of depth **d** with branching factor **b\***. A uniform tree is one in which every non-leaf node has exactly the same number of children — this is the *idealized* shape that a perfectly focused search would follow.

**Step 1 — Count the total nodes level-by-level.**

- Level 0 (the root): $1$ node
- Level 1: $b^{*}$ nodes
- Level 2: $(b^{*})^{2}$ nodes
- ⋯
- Level $k$: $(b^{*})^{k}$ nodes
- ⋯
- Level $d$: $(b^{*})^{d}$ nodes

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series.**

$$N + 1 \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{d} (b^{*})^{k} \;=\; 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + \dots + (b^{*})^{d}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the closed-form formula for a finite geometric series.**

For any real number $r \ne 1$ and integer $d \ge 0$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{d} r^{k} \;=\; \frac{r^{d+1} - 1}{r - 1}$$

Substituting $r = b^{*}$:

$$N + 1 \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply to obtain the polynomial in $b^*$.**

$$(N + 1)(b^{*} - 1) \;=\; (b^{*})^{d+1} - 1$$

$$(N + 1)\,b^{*} - (N + 1) \;=\; (b^{*})^{d+1} - 1$$

$$(N + 1)\,b^{*} - N \;=\; (b^{*})^{d+1}$$

This is a polynomial equation of degree $d+1$ in $b^*$, which in general has no closed-form solution for $d \ge 4$. The standard practical approach is **bisection search** or **Newton–Raphson iteration** on the equivalent form:

$$f(b^{*}) \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1} - (N + 1) \;=\; 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **How KTU Examiners Test This**
> They will *not* ask you to solve for $b^*$ by hand on a real polynomial of degree 5 or more. Instead, they will give you a *small* $d$ (typically $d = 2$ or $d = 3$) and a numerical $N$, and ask you to compute $b^*$ directly. We do that next.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing b* for a Small Tree

**Problem:** A heuristic search expands a total of $N = 21$ nodes (including the root) to find a solution at depth $d = 3$. Compute the effective branching factor $b^*$.

**Solution.** We use the closed-form:

$$N + 1 \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$$

Plug in $N = 21$, $d = 3$:

$$22 \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{4} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$$

Use the algebraic identity $\dfrac{x^{4} - 1}{x - 1} = 1 + x + x^{2} + x^{3}$:

$$22 \;=\; 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{3}$$

Try $b^{*} = 3$:

$$1 + 3 + 9 + 27 \;=\; 40 \;\ne\; 22$$

Try $b^{*} = 2$:

$$1 + 2 + 4 + 8 \;=\; 15 \;\ne\; 22$$

Try $b^{*} = 2.5$:

$$1 + 2.5 + 6.25 + 15.625 \;=\; 25.375 \;\ne\; 22$$

Try $b^{*} = 2.3$:

$$1 + 2.3 + 5.29 + 12.167 \;=\; 20.757 \;\approx\; 21$$

Try $b^{*} = 2.32$:

$$1 + 2.32 + 5.3824 + 12.487 \;=\; 21.189 \;\approx\; 21$$

Interpolating linearly between 2.3 and 2.32:

$$b^{*} \;\approx\; 2.3 + 0.02 \cdot \frac{22 - 20.757}{21.189 - 20.757} \;\approx\; 2.3 + 0.02 \cdot 2.88 \;\approx\; 2.315$$

So the effective branching factor is $b^{*} \approx 2.32$. This is a "fair" heuristic — between 2.0 (good) and 3.0 (poor).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Comparing Two Heuristics

**Problem.** Two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$ are used with A* to solve the same 8-puzzle instance. A* with $h_1$ expands $N_1 = 2000$ nodes to depth $d = 20$. A* with $h_2$ expands $N_2 = 500$ nodes to the same depth. Which heuristic is more accurate, and by how much?

**Step 1 — Compute $b^*_{1}$.**

We need $b^*$ such that:

$$2001 \;=\; 1 + b^*_{1} + (b^*_{1})^{2} + \dots + (b^*_{1})^{20}$$

Since $2^{20} \approx 10^{6}$ but $2001$ is much smaller, $b^*_{1}$ must be well below 2. Iterating:

- $b^* = 1.4$: $\dfrac{1.4^{21} - 1}{1.4 - 1} = \dfrac{126.0 - 1}{0.4} \approx 312.5$ — too small
- $b^* = 1.6$: $\dfrac{1.6^{21} - 1}{0.6} \approx \dfrac{1478}{0.6} \approx 2463$ — slightly too large
- $b^* = 1.59$: $\dfrac{1.59^{21}}{0.59} \approx \dfrac{1300}{0.59} \approx 2203$ — close
- $b^* = 1.58$: $\dfrac{1.58^{21}}{0.58} \approx \dfrac{1145}{0.58} \approx 1974$ — close

So $b^*_{1} \approx 1.58$.

**Step 2 — Compute $b^*_{2}$.**

We need $b^*$ such that:

$$501 \;=\; 1 + b^*_{2} + (b^*_{2})^{2} + \dots + (b^*_{2})^{20}$$

Iterating:

- $b^* = 1.2$: $\dfrac{1.2^{21} - 1}{0.2} \approx \dfrac{54.7 - 1}{0.2} \approx 268$ — too small
- $b^* = 1.25$: $\dfrac{1.25^{21} - 1}{0.25} \approx \dfrac{108 - 1}{0.25} \approx 428$ — too small
- $b^* = 1.3$: $\dfrac{1.3^{21} - 1}{0.3} \approx \dfrac{211 - 1}{0.3} \approx 700$ — too large
- $b^* = 1.27$: $\dfrac{1.27^{21}}{0.27} \approx \dfrac{159}{0.27} \approx 590$ — slightly too large
- $b^* = 1.26$: $\dfrac{1.26^{21}}{0.26} \approx \dfrac{141}{0.26} \approx 542$ — close
- $b^* = 1.255$: $\approx 503$ — essentially exact

So $b^*_{2} \approx 1.26$.

**Step 3 — Interpret the comparison.**

$$b^*_{2} \approx 1.26 \quad < \quad b^*_{1} \approx 1.58$$

Since $h_2$ achieves a lower effective branching factor, **$h_2$ is the more accurate heuristic**. Despite being only marginally lower, the impact on node expansions is dramatic: $h_2$ required $500$ nodes vs. $h_1$'s $2000$ — a **4× reduction**. This illustrates the exponential power of the heuristic accuracy effect.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Verifying Dominance

**Problem.** On the 8-puzzle, two admissible heuristics are:
- $h_1(n) = $ number of misplaced tiles
- $h_2(n) = $ Manhattan distance (sum of grid distances of tiles from goal)

Show that $h_2$ dominates $h_1$, and use the dominance theorem to predict the relative node counts.

**Solution.** For every tile, the Manhattan distance is always $\ge 1$ (if the tile is misplaced) and equals the number of *grid steps* required to bring it to its goal position, which is always $\ge 1$ (if misplaced). Therefore, for any misplaced tile, its Manhattan distance $\ge 1$. Summing over all misplaced tiles:

$$h_{2}(n) \;=\; \sum_{\text{misplaced tiles}} (\text{Manhattan distance of tile}) \;\ge\; \sum_{\text{misplaced tiles}} 1 \;=\; h_{1}(n)$$

Hence $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for every state $n$. By the **dominance theorem**, A* with $h_2$ will expand a subset of the nodes expanded by A* with $h_1$:

$$\text{Nodes expanded by A*}(h_2) \;\le\; \text{Nodes expanded by A*}(h_1)$$

Empirically, A* with $h_2$ expands roughly 6× fewer nodes on standard 8-puzzle benchmarks than A* with $h_1$, and achieves an effective branching factor of about 1.42 vs. 2.79 for $h_1$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs in Heuristic Design

| Heuristic Property | Effect on Optimality | Effect on Speed | Effect on Memory | Design Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Admissibility | Guarantees optimality | Neutral | Neutral | Must be proven mathematically |
| Consistency | Guarantees graph-search optimality | Allows early termination | Reduces re-expansions | Stronger constraint than admissibility |
| Higher accuracy (closer to $h^*$) | Preserved if admissibility holds | **Exponentially** fewer nodes | **Exponentially** less memory | Higher design complexity (e.g., pattern databases) |
| Domination of $h_2$ over $h_1$ | Preserved | Strictly fewer or equal nodes | Strictly less or equal memory | Must prove $h_2 \ge h_1$ pointwise |
| Relaxed-problem heuristic | Admissible by construction | Problem-dependent | Problem-dependent | Solving a simpler problem |
| Pattern database heuristic | Admissible by construction | Very high accuracy | High memory cost | Pre-computation + storage |
| Learning-based heuristic | May not be admissible | Very fast | Compact | Loss of formal guarantees |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — A* Search Tree with Two Heuristics of Different Accuracy

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes a small A* search tree where two heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$ are compared at each node. $h_2$ is more accurate (closer to $h^*$) and hence leads the search down a narrower, deeper path to the goal, while $h_1$ (less accurate) causes the algorithm to explore more "side" branches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["S0  f=10  h1=8  h2=9"]
    A1["A1  f=12  h1=7  h2=8"]
    A2["B1  f=14  h1=6  h2=7"]
    A3["C1  f=16  h1=5  h2=6"]
    A4["GOAL f=18  h1=4  h2=4"]
    B0["S0 expanded by h1 only"]
    B1["X1  f=15  h1=8"]
    B2["X2  f=17  h1=7"]
    B3["X3  f=19  h1=6"]
    C0["Y1  f=16  h1=9"]
    C1["Y2  f=18  h1=8"]
    C2["Y3  f=20  h1=7"]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4

    A0 -.->|h1 misled| B0
    B0 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3

    A0 -.->|h1 misled| C0
    C0 --> C1
    C1 --> C2

    classDef accurate fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef misleading fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef goalNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4 accurate
    class B0,B1,B2,B3,C0,C1,C2 misleading
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue path (S0 → A1 → A2 → A3 → GOAL)** is the narrow, deep path that an accurate heuristic (such as $h_2$) follows directly to the goal.
- The **red side-branches (X1, X2, X3, Y1, Y2, Y3)** are nodes that a less-accurate heuristic (such as $h_1$) would have to explore because it fails to recognize the correct direction early.
- The **green GOAL node** is reached at depth 4 along the blue path; the red branches are "wasted" subtrees that contribute to a higher effective branching factor.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Effective Branching Factor as a Quality Curve

This block diagram maps the conceptual relationship between heuristic accuracy (x-axis) and the effective branching factor (y-axis), showing the exponential penalty for using weak heuristics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["Heuristic Quality"] --> Q2["h(n) closer to h* n"]
    Q2 --> Q3["b* approaches 1"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Exponentially fewer nodes expanded"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Faster search and lower memory"]

    R1["Heuristic Quality"] --> R2["h(n) far from h* n"]
    R2 --> R3["b* approaches branching factor B"]
    R3 --> R4["Approaches uninformed search cost"]
    R4 --> R5["Slow, memory intensive search"]

    classDef good fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5 good
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 bad
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Heuristic-Generation Pipeline (Relaxed Problems)

This functional block diagram summarizes the standard KTU-approved pipeline for constructing admissible heuristics automatically from a problem's formal definition: define a *relaxed* problem, solve it optimally, and use the optimal solution cost as $h(n)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Original Problem P with constraints C1, C2, C3"]
    P1["Relaxed Problem P prime  remove one or more constraints"]
    P2["Optimal Solution of P prime  h prime n"]
    P3["Use h prime n as heuristic h n for A*"]
    P4["A* search on original problem P using h n"]

    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    Q0["Proof obligation"]
    Q1["h n is admissible because h prime n equals h* of relaxed problem which is at most h* of original problem"]

    P3 --> Q0
    Q0 --> Q1

    classDef orig fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef relax fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef proof fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class P0 orig
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 relax
    class Q0,Q1 proof
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **yellow block** is the original, hard problem with all its real-world constraints.
- The **blue blocks** represent the relaxation pipeline: remove a constraint, solve the simpler problem optimally, harvest the cost as the heuristic.
- The **green blocks** encode the formal proof obligation: the relaxation must produce a cost that is $\le$ the true cost of the original problem (so the heuristic is admissible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Examples in the KTU Syllabus**
> - 8-puzzle: $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) comes from relaxing the constraint that *only one tile can be moved at a time*; $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) comes from relaxing the constraint that *tiles cannot pass through each other*.
> - Route-finding: $h(n)$ = straight-line distance is admissible because the actual road distance is $\ge$ the Euclidean distance.
> - VLSI routing: $h(n)$ = Manhattan distance is admissible because wires cannot move diagonally and cannot pass through obstacles diagonally.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Heuristic-Accuracy Performance Trade-off (Block Architecture)

This block diagram presents a high-level engineering view of the trade-off space in choosing or designing a heuristic for an AI system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Domain Expert Knowledge"] --> B["Candidate Heuristic h1 n simple fast to compute"]
    C["Pattern Database Lookup"] --> D["Candidate Heuristic h2 n high accuracy high memory"]
    E["Learned Model ML"] --> F["Candidate Heuristic h3 n fastest to evaluate non admissible in general"]

    B --> G["Quality measured by b* on benchmark suite"]
    D --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H["Select heuristic that meets accuracy, admissibility, time, and memory budgets"]

    classDef expert fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
    classDef pattern fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef learned fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class A,B expert
    class C,D pattern
    class E,F learned
    class G,H decision
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 2]
**Define a heuristic function. With an example, explain the concept of an admissible heuristic.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

A **heuristic function $h(n)$** is a function that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ in the state space to a goal state. It encodes *domain-specific knowledge* to guide the search toward the goal more efficiently than uninformed methods like BFS or UCS.

A heuristic is called **admissible** if it **never overestimates** the true minimum cost, i.e.:

$$h(n) \le h^{*}(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

**Example:** In route-finding on a road map, $h(n) =$ *straight-line distance* from city $n$ to the destination is admissible, because the straight-line path is always the shortest possible path — no actual road route can be shorter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *admissibility* (never overestimates) with *consistency* (triangle inequality). Admissibility is sufficient for **tree search** optimality; consistency is required for **graph search** optimality.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]
**What is the effective branching factor? How is it used to measure the quality of a heuristic function?**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

The **effective branching factor $b^*$** is defined as the branching factor of a *uniform* search tree of depth $d$ that contains the same total number of expanded nodes $N$ as the actual search:

$$N + 1 \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$$

It is used as a **single-number summary** of heuristic quality:
- $b^* = 1$ ⇒ the heuristic is perfect (search is a straight line).
- $b^*$ close to the graph's underlying branching factor ⇒ the heuristic is poor (search is nearly uninformed).
- A lower $b^*$ indicates a more accurate heuristic, and a *small* reduction in $b^*$ yields a *huge* reduction in node expansions because the relationship is exponential.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Module 2 typically carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The two alternatives below are independent and cover the topic from complementary angles. Both have two sub-parts of 7 marks each, as per the KTU pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain heuristic functions in informed search. Discuss the properties of **admissibility** and **consistency** of a heuristic with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **effective branching factor** of a heuristic. Show that even a small change in $b^*$ leads to an exponential change in the number of nodes expanded.

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **heuristic function $h(n)$** in informed search provides an estimate of the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal. It is the engine that distinguishes an informed algorithm like **A\*** or **Greedy Best-First Search** from uninformed algorithms.

**Admissibility:** A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if for every node $n$:

$$h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the *true* minimum cost from $n$ to a goal. An admissible heuristic is *optimistic* — it never overestimates the remaining cost.

> *Example:* In the 8-puzzle, $h_1(n) =$ number of misplaced tiles is admissible because each misplaced tile requires at least one move, so the true solution cost is $\ge h_1(n)$. In route-finding, straight-line distance is admissible because no road path can be shorter than a straight line.

**Consistency (Monotonicity):** A heuristic $h$ is **consistent** if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ via action $a$:

$$h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

This is the **triangle inequality** for heuristics. Consistency is stronger than admissibility: every consistent heuristic is admissible, but not vice versa.

> *Example:* $h_2(n) =$ Manhattan distance for the 8-puzzle is consistent. The Manhattan distance can only decrease (or stay the same) by at most the cost of the move, satisfying the triangle inequality.

**Significance:**
- Admissibility ⇒ A* (tree-search) is **optimal**.
- Consistency ⇒ A* (graph-search) is **optimal and efficient** (no re-expansion of closed nodes).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Definition of Effective Branching Factor:**

The effective branching factor $b^*$ is the branching factor of a *uniform tree* of depth $d$ that would require the same number of node expansions $N$ as the actual search. By counting the total nodes in a uniform tree:

$$N + 1 \;=\; 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + \dots + (b^{*})^{d} \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}$$

**Neat Diagram (drawn in the answer script):**

```
Depth 0:               O                       (1 node)
Depth 1:           O   O   O                   (b* nodes)
Depth 2:         O O O O O O O O               (b*^2 nodes)
Depth 3:       many many many many ...         (b*^3 nodes)
...
Depth d:       numerous nodes ...              (b*^d nodes)
```

A smaller $b^*$ produces a "skinnier" tree (focused search), while a larger $b^*$ produces a "bushy" tree (wasted exploration).

**Exponential Effect (Proof Sketch):**

We have:

$$(b^{*})^{d+1} \;\approx\; N \cdot (b^{*} - 1) + 1$$

For large $d$ and $b^* > 1$, the dominant term is $(b^*)^{d+1}$, so $N \approx (b^*)^{d+1}$. Taking logarithms:

$$\log N \;\approx\; (d+1) \cdot \log b^{*}$$

For a fixed depth $d$, the relationship between $\log N$ and $b^*$ is *linear* in the log-domain, which means $N$ is **exponential in $b^*$**.

**Numerical illustration at depth $d = 10$:**

| Heuristic | $b^*$ | Approx. $N$ |
|---|---|---|
| Excellent | 1.1 | $\approx 16$ |
| Good | 1.3 | $\approx 273$ |
| Fair | 1.5 | $\approx 2,000$ |
| Poor | 2.0 | $\approx 1,000,000$ |

**Conclusion:** A *tiny* improvement in heuristic accuracy (e.g., $b^* = 2.0 \to 1.5$) saves roughly **3 orders of magnitude** in node expansions. This is the central practical motivation for engineering highly accurate admissible heuristics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B)**
> 1. Students often **forget the "+1"** in $N+1 = \frac{(b^*)^{d+1}-1}{b^*-1}$ — the root node counts! Losing 1 mark.
> 2. Many students confuse $h^*(n)$ (the optimal cost *function*) with the optimal solution *node* in the answer. Use $h^*(n)$ to denote the function.
> 3. For Part (b), if you only quote the formula without an *explanation* of the exponential sensitivity, you will lose 2–3 marks. Always include the logarithm-based reasoning.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **dominance** property between two heuristics? Prove that if $h_2$ dominates $h_1$, then A* with $h_2$ expands no more nodes than A* with $h_1$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **relaxed-problem technique** for generating heuristic functions. Show that the heuristic obtained from a relaxed problem is always admissible.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Definition of Dominance:**

Given two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$ on the same problem, $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$ if for every node $n$ reachable from the start state:

$$h_{2}(n) \;\ge\; h_{1}(n)$$

**Proof of the Dominance Theorem:**

Consider any node $n$ that is expanded by A* with $h_1$. We will show that $n$ is *also* expanded by A* with $h_2$, hence the set of nodes expanded by A*($h_2$) is a subset of those expanded by A*($h_1$).

The key observation is the **f-contour**: A* expands all nodes with $f(n) < C^*$, where $C^*$ is the optimal solution cost. For A*($h_1$), any expanded node satisfies:

$$g(n) + h_1(n) \;<\; C^*$$

Since $h_2 \ge h_1$ (dominance) and $g(n)$ is the same in both runs (problem is the same):

$$g(n) + h_2(n) \;\ge\; g(n) + h_1(n) \;<\; C^*$$

Therefore the same node $n$ is *also* in the f-contour of A*($h_2$) and hence expanded by A*($h_2$).

This establishes:

$$\text{Nodes}(A^*, h_2) \;\subseteq\; \text{Nodes}(A^*, h_1)$$

and therefore:

$$\vert \text{Nodes}(A^*, h_2) \vert \;\le\; \vert \text{Nodes}(A^*, h_1) \vert$$

**Example:** For the 8-puzzle, $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) dominates $h_1$ (misplaced tiles). Empirically, A*($h_2$) expands roughly 6× fewer nodes than A*($h_1$).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**The Relaxed-Problem Technique:**

To construct a heuristic for a hard problem $P$:

1. **Define a relaxed problem $P'$** by *removing one or more constraints* of $P$. The relaxed problem has a (typically) larger state space and is easier to solve.
2. **Solve $P'$ optimally** from any state $n$; the optimal cost is $h'(n)$.
3. **Set $h(n) = h'(n)$** and use it as the heuristic for A* on the original problem $P$.

**Examples:**

| Original Problem | Relaxation | Resulting Heuristic |
|---|---|---|
| 8-puzzle (tiles block each other) | Tiles can move independently | Manhattan distance |
| 8-puzzle | Tiles can teleport to goal | Misplaced tiles |
| Road navigation | Roads have no one-way rules, no traffic | Straight-line distance |
| Rubik's cube (legal moves only) | Any face can be turned any number of times | h = number of misplaced cubies |
| Travelling salesman (visit each city once) | Cities can be revisited | MST-based heuristic |

**Proof of Admissibility:**

Let $h^*(P, n)$ denote the optimal cost of solving the *original* problem $P$ from $n$, and $h^*(P', n)$ the optimal cost of solving the *relaxed* problem $P'$ from $n$.

**Claim:** $h^*(P', n) \le h^*(P, n)$ for every state $n$.

**Proof:** Any feasible solution of $P$ from $n$ to a goal is *also* a feasible solution of $P'$ (because $P'$ has *fewer* constraints). Therefore the optimal cost of $P'$ cannot exceed the optimal cost of $P$. Hence:

$$h(n) \;=\; h^*(P', n) \;\le\; h^*(P, n) \;=\; h^*(n)$$

This is precisely the admissibility condition $h(n) \le h^*(n)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization to Multiple Relaxations**
> If you remove *more* constraints (a "more relaxed" problem), the resulting heuristic is *less informative* but easier to compute. The art of heuristic engineering lies in finding the sweet spot — a relaxation that is *just relaxed enough* to be solvable quickly but *not so relaxed* that the heuristic is trivial (e.g., $h \equiv 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B Alternative)**
> 1. In the dominance proof, students often forget to mention the **f-contour argument** and simply assert the result — this is **incomplete** and loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. In the relaxed-problem technique, the proof of admissibility hinges on the phrase "*any feasible solution of P is also feasible in P'*" — students who write the wrong direction (P' ⊆ P) will lose 2 marks.
> 3. Always **give at least one concrete example** (e.g., 8-puzzle) in Part (b); abstract explanation alone is insufficient for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Heuristic Search Strategies**

### A. Core Definitions
- [x] **Heuristic function $h(n)$** = estimate of cost from $n$ to nearest goal.
- [x] **Path cost $g(n)$** = actual cost from start to $n$.
- [x] **Evaluation $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$** = estimated cost of solution via $n$ (used in A*).
- [x] **True cost $h^*(n)$** = optimal remaining cost; *never known a priori*.
- [x] **Effective branching factor $b^*$** = branching factor of equivalent uniform tree.

### B. Critical Properties
- [x] **Admissible:** $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Ensures A* (tree-search) optimality.
- [x] **Consistent (Monotone):** $h(n) \le c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ for all edges. Ensures A* (graph-search) optimality.
- [x] **Dominated:** $h_2 \ge h_1$ pointwise $\Rightarrow$ A*($h_2$) expands $\le$ nodes than A*($h_1$).
- [x] **Perfect:** $h(n) = h^*(n)$ for all $n$ $\Rightarrow$ $b^* = 1$, search is a straight line.

### C. Key Formulas
- [x] $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — A* evaluation function.
- [x] $N + 1 = \frac{(b^*)^{d+1} - 1}{b^* - 1}$ — Effective branching factor definition.
- [x] Admissibility: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$.
- [x] Consistency: $h(n) \le c(n,a,n') + h(n')$.

### D. Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance
- [x] **Lower $b^*$ ⇒ exponentially fewer nodes expanded.**
- [x] A 0.1 reduction in $b^*$ can save 1–3 orders of magnitude in node count.
- [x] Accuracy improvements must *not* break admissibility (else optimality is lost).
- [x] Beyond admissibility, designers trade off heuristic *computation time* vs *information value*.

### E. Methods to Generate Heuristics
- [x] **Relaxed problems** — most common; always admissible by construction.
- [x] **Pattern databases** — pre-compute exact costs for abstract sub-problems; admissible.
- [x] **Landmarks** — sub-goals that *must* be reached; admissible lower bounds.
- [x] **Disjoint pattern databases** — additive for sub-problems that share no state variables.
- [x] **Learned heuristics (ML)** — fast but generally *not* admissible; used in deep search.

### F. Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Do **not** confuse admissibility with consistency.
- [x] Do **not** forget the "+1" in the effective branching factor formula.
- [x] Do **not** claim a heuristic is admissible without proof (cite relaxation or a domination argument).
- [x] Do **not** use an inadmissible heuristic in A* and expect optimality — use Weighted A* or Greedy Search instead.
- [x] Do **not** confuse $h^*(n)$ (function) with the goal node.

### G. Standard Examples to Memorize
- [x] 8-puzzle: $h_1$ = misplaced tiles, $h_2$ = Manhattan distance. $h_2$ dominates $h_1$.
- [x] Route-finding: $h$ = straight-line distance (admissible, consistent on planar maps with metric costs).
- [x] Rubik's cube: $h$ = max over faces of correctly placed cubies (admissible).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Word of Advice for KTU 2024**
> The topic "Heuristic Functions and the Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance" is one of the most **exam-friendly** topics in Module 2. Almost every KTU question on this topic tests one of three things: (1) definition + admissibility proof, (2) effective branching factor calculation, (3) relaxed-problem method. Practice all three styles; the marks are yours for the taking.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Generate and Test Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Generate and Test* is a problem-solving paradigm in Artificial Intelligence where the system systematically **generates** candidate solutions from the search space, **tests** each candidate against a goal/acceptance criterion, and **terminates** when a satisfactory solution is found or the space is exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine you have locked your car keys inside your house and you try opening the lock with every key on your keychain. You *generate* (pick) one key, *test* it (try inserting and turning), and if it works, you stop. Otherwise, you generate the next key and repeat. The Generate-and-Test algorithm works exactly like this brute-force attempt — a methodical but potentially exhaustive search.

**Key Terminology Table:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Generator** | A procedure that enumerates all possible candidate solutions |
| **Test** | A predicate function (Boolean) that validates a candidate |
| **Solution Space** | The exhaustive set of all possible candidates |
| **Heuristic** | Optional function to prune the candidate generation |

## 1.2 Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Greedy Best-First Search* is an informed search strategy that **expands the node that appears closest to the goal** as estimated by a heuristic function $f(n) = h(n)$, ignoring the cost already incurred to reach that node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Picture yourself hiking down a mountain in dense fog. At every fork, you choose the path that *visually looks* like it descends the fastest. You don't look back to check how much you've already climbed — you simply act on the local "best-looking" choice. That is greed: optimize the immediate decision without considering global cost.

**Heuristic Function Notation:**
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal
- For GBFS, $f(n) = h(n)$ (only heuristic considered)

## 1.3 A* Search Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *A\** is an informed best-first search algorithm that finds the **least-cost path** from a start node to a goal node by combining the actual cost from start and a heuristic estimate of the remaining cost: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> A\* is like a GPS navigator that knows two things: (1) how far you've already driven (the *actual* distance — $g$), and (2) the straight-line "as-the-crow-flies" distance to your destination (the *estimate* — $h$). It always recommends the next turn that minimizes total: *already driven + still to go*. This is smarter than a greedy search that only looks at the straight-line distance.

**Cost Components:**
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$ (known, exact)
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal (estimated)
- $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ = estimated total cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$

## 1.4 Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Constraint Satisfaction Problem* is a mathematical formulation defined by a set of **variables**, their **domains**, and **constraints** that restrict the values the variables can simultaneously take. A solution is an assignment of values to all variables satisfying every constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> A Sudoku puzzle is a perfect CSP. The *variables* are the 81 empty cells. The *domains* are the digits 1–9. The *constraints* are the row, column, and 3×3 box uniqueness rules. You assign digits to cells such that no rule is broken. Map coloring (no two adjacent countries share a color) and timetable scheduling (no teacher in two places at once) are also classic CSPs.

**CSP Triple Definition:**
$$\text{CSP} = \langle X, D, C \rangle$$
Where:
- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ — finite set of variables
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ — domain set for each variable
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ — set of constraints
