A **proposition** is a declarative statement that is either **True (T)** or **False (F)** but never both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomic Propositions** are represented by uppercase letters: $P, Q, R, \ldots$
> Examples: $P$ = "It is raining", $Q$ = "The ground is wet"

### 2.1.2 Logical Connectives (The KTU Big Five)

| Connective | Symbol | Read As | Truth Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Negation | $\lnot P$ | NOT P | True when P is False |
| Conjunction | $P \land Q$ | P AND Q | True only when both are True |
| Disjunction | $P \lor Q$ | P OR Q | False only when both are False |
| Implication | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | P IMPLIES Q | False only when P=T, Q=F |
| Biconditional | $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ | P IFF Q | True when both have same truth value |

### 2.1.3 Well-Formed Formula (WFF) Rules
A formula is a WFF in PL if and only if:
1. It is an atomic proposition symbol ($P, Q, R, \ldots$), **OR**
2. It is of the form $\lnot \alpha$ where $\alpha$ is a WFF, **OR**
3. It is of the form $(\alpha \circ \beta)$ where $\circ \in \{\land, \lor, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow\}$ and both $\alpha, \beta$ are WFFs.

### 2.1.4 Semantic Equivalences (The "Cheat Codes")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These equivalences appear in **nearly every KTU exam paper**. Memorize them.

| Law | Equivalence |
|---|---|
| Double Negation | $\lnot \lnot P \equiv P$ |
| De Morgan's 1 | $\lnot(P \land Q) \equiv \lnot P \lor \lnot Q$ |
| De Morgan's 2 | $\lnot(P \lor Q) \equiv \lnot P \land \lnot Q$ |
| Implication Elimination | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$ |
| Contrapositive | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot Q \Rightarrow \lnot P$ |
| Distributive | $P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R)$ |
| Absorption | $P \lor (P \land Q) \equiv P$ |

## 2.2 First-Order Logic (FOL) — The Power Upgrade

FOL overcomes PL's biggest limitation: **inability to express general rules**. PL can say "Socrates is mortal" but cannot say "**All** humans are mortal."

### 2.2.1 FOL Syntax Components

| Element | Notation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant** | Lowercase: $a, b, c$ | $john$ |
| **Variable** | Uppercase: $X, Y, Z$ | $X$ |
| **Predicate** | Uppercase: $P(\cdot)$ | $Mortal(X)$ |
| **Function** | Lowercase: $f(\cdot)$ | $fatherOf(john)$ |
| **Universal Quantifier** | $\forall$ | $\forall X \; P(X)$ |
| **Existential Quantifier** | $\exists$ | $\exists X \; P(X)$ |

### 2.2.2 Quantifier Rules (KTU High-Yield)

$$\lnot \forall X \; P(X) \equiv \exists X \; \lnot P(X)$$

$$\lnot \exists X \; P(X) \equiv \forall X \; \lnot P(X)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope of Quantifiers:** A quantifier's scope extends to the smallest following formula. Parentheses matter. Example: $\forall X \; P(X) \Rightarrow Q(X)$ is very different from $\forall X \; (P(X) \Rightarrow Q(X))$.

## 2.3 Inference Rules — The Reasoning Engine

### 2.3.1 Propositional Inference Rules

| Rule | Schema | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | $\dfrac{P, \; P \Rightarrow Q}{\therefore Q}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest |
| **Modus Tollens (MT)** | $\dfrac{\lnot Q, \; P \Rightarrow Q}{\therefore \lnot P}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism (HS)** | $\dfrac{P \Rightarrow Q, \; Q \Rightarrow R}{\therefore P \Rightarrow R}$ | ⭐⭐ |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism (DS)** | $\dfrac{P \lor Q, \; \lnot P}{\therefore Q}$ | ⭐⭐ |
| **Conjunction Intro (CI)** | $\dfrac{P, \; Q}{\therefore P \land Q}$ | ⭐ |
| **Simplification (Simp)** | $\dfrac{P \land Q}{\therefore P}$ | ⭐ |

### 2.3.2 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) — The FOL Workhorse

$$\dfrac{P(c_1, \ldots, c_n), \; Q_1, \ldots, Q_m, \; (P(v_1, \ldots, v_n) \land Q_1' \land \ldots \land Q_m' \Rightarrow R(v_1, \ldots, v_n))}{\therefore R(c_1, \ldots, c_n)}$$

**Conditions:** $c_i$ is a constant substitution for variable $v_i$, and $Q_i$ unifies with $Q_i'$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Source of Truth for KTU Board Exams (Module 3).**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution Rule | $\dfrac{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k, \; m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_n}{(\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \cdots \lor m_n)}$ where $\ell_i$ and $m_j$ are complementary | Theorem proving |
| Unification Condition | $\theta$ is a unifier if $S\theta = T\theta$ (apply $\theta$ to both sides yields identical expression | FOL inference |
| Most General Unifier (MGU) | Unique (up to renaming) unifier with fewest constraints | Backward Chaining |
| Skolem Function | Replaces $\exists X$ in scope of $\forall Y$ with $f(Y)$ | CNF conversion |
| Validity | True in **every** interpretation | $\models \alpha$ |
| Satisfiability | True in **at least one** interpretation | $M \models \alpha$ |
| Entailment | $\alpha \models \beta$ iff every model of $\alpha$ is a model of $\beta$ | Inference goal |
| Soundness | Inference derives only entailed sentences | Property check |
| Completeness | All entailed sentences can be derived | Property check |

## 2.5 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (Facts $\rightarrow$ Conclusions) | Goal-driven (Goal $\rightarrow$ Sub-goals) |
| **Starts With** | Known facts | Query/goal |
| **Used In** | Production systems, monitoring | Query answering, PROLOG |
| **Termination** | When no new facts emerge | When goal proven or no rules apply |
| **Efficiency** | May do unnecessary work | Focused, more efficient for queries |
| **KTU Question Type** | "Show all derived facts" | "Prove the goal" |

## 2.6 Resolution — The Ultimate Inference

Resolution is **sound and complete** for propositional logic (refutation complete for FOL). The procedure:

1. Convert all KB sentences to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
2. Negate the goal/query.
3. Repeatedly apply the **resolution rule** to clauses.
4. If the empty clause ($\square$) is derived, the goal is **proven** (refutation).

### CNF Conversion Pipeline (KTU-Favorite)
$$\text{Original} \rightarrow \text{Implication Elimination} \rightarrow \text{Negation Inward (De Morgan)} \rightarrow \text{Standardize Variables} \rightarrow \text{Skolemize} \rightarrow \text{Drop } \forall \rightarrow \text{Distribute } \lor \text{ over } \land \rightarrow \text{CNF}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: CNF Conversion of a Complex FOL Sentence

**Problem:** Convert the following sentence to **Prenex CNF**:
$$\forall X \; [\, \lnot (\, P(X) \Rightarrow (\forall Y \; [\, Q(X,Y) \Rightarrow P(f(X)) \,] \,) \,) \,]$$

### Step 1 — Eliminate the Implication

Using $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$:

$$\forall X \; [\, \lnot (\, \lnot P(X) \lor (\forall Y \; [\, \lnot Q(X,Y) \lor P(f(X)) \,] \,) \,) \,]$$

### Step 2 — Push Negations Inward (De Morgan's)

Apply $\lnot(A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$:

$$\forall X \; [\, P(X) \land \lnot(\forall Y \; [\, \lnot Q(X,Y) \lor P(f(X)) \,] \,) \,]$$

Apply $\lnot \forall \equiv \exists \lnot$ to inner quantifier:

$$\forall X \; [\, P(X) \land \exists Y \; \lnot(\, \lnot Q(X,Y) \lor P(f(X)) \,) \,]$$

Apply $\lnot(A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$ again:

$$\forall X \; [\, P(X) \land \exists Y \; (Q(X,Y) \land \lnot P(f(X))) \,]$$

### Step 3 — Standardize Variables Apart

Rename bound variables to avoid clashes:

$$\forall X \; [\, P(X) \land \exists Y \; (Q(X,Y) \land \lnot P(f(X))) \,]$$

### Step 4 — Skolemize

The existential $Y$ is inside the scope of universal $X$, so $Y$ becomes a **Skolem function** of $X$: $Y \rightarrow g(X)$.

$$P(X) \land (Q(X, g(X)) \land \lnot P(f(X)))$$

### Step 5 — Drop Universal Quantifiers

Universal quantifiers are implicit in CNF:

$$P(X) \land Q(X, g(X)) \land \lnot P(f(X))$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Clause Set

Each conjunct becomes a clause:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \; P(X) \\
C_2 &: \; Q(X, g(X)) \\
C_3 &: \; \lnot P(f(X))
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** This is the standard KTU 7-step CNF procedure. Each step is worth marks — skipping steps is the #1 cause of partial credit loss.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Unification Algorithm

**Problem:** Unify the atomic sentences:
$$P(f(X), g(Y, Z), b) \quad \text{and} \quad P(f(a), g(W, c), b)$$

### Step 1 — Initialize substitution set
$$\theta = \{\}$$

### Step 2 — Recurse on predicate symbols
Both are $P$ — match the arguments pairwise.

### Step 3 — Unify $f(X)$ with $f(a)$

By structural recursion: $f$ matches, $X$ must equal $a$.
$$\theta = \{X / a\}$$
Apply $\theta$ to remaining terms:

$$P(f(a), g(Y, Z), b) \quad \text{vs} \quad P(f(a), g(W, c), b)$$

### Step 4 — Unify $g(Y, Z)$ with $g(W, c)$

$g$ matches. Unify $Y$ with $W$:
$$\theta = \{X/a, \; Y/W\}$$
Apply: $g(W, Z)$ vs $g(W, c)$ — unify $Z$ with $c$:
$$\theta = \{X/a, \; Y/W, \; Z/c\}$$

### Step 5 — Unify $b$ with $b$

Trivially unifiable.

### Final MGU (Most General Unifier)
$$\boxed{\theta = \{X/a, \; Y/W, \; Z/c\}}$$

The unified result is $P(f(a), g(W, c), b)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MGU Uniqueness:** The MGU is unique up to variable renaming. If at any step you attempt to unify a variable with a term containing that same variable, unification **fails** (occurs check failure).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Forward Chaining on a Definite Clause KB

**Knowledge Base:**
1. $American(x) \land Weapon(y) \land Sells(x, y, z) \land Hostile(z) \Rightarrow Criminal(x)$
2. $Owns(Nono, M_1)$
3. $Missile(M_1)$
4. $Owns(x, M_1) \Rightarrow Sells(West, x, Nono)$
5. $Missile(x) \land Owns(Nono, x) \Rightarrow Sells(West, x, Nono)$
6. $American(West)$
7. $Enemy(x, America) \Rightarrow Hostile(x)$
8. $Enemy(Nono, America)$

### Derivation Trace (FC Algorithm)

**Iteration 1:** Fire rule using (3) and (2):
- Match: $Missile(x)$ with $Missile(M_1)$ → $\{x/M_1\}$
- Match: $Owns(Nono, x)$ with $Owns(Nono, M_1)$ → consistent
- **Derive:** $Sells(West, M_1, Nono)$

**Iteration 2:** Fire rule (1) by binding:
- $x = West$, $y = M_1$, $z = Nono$
- Check premises: $American(West)$ ✓, $Weapon(M_1)$? (not yet), $Sells(West, M_1, Nono)$ ✓, $Hostile(Nono)$? (not yet)
- Blocked — need more facts.

**Iteration 3:** Fire rule (8) with (7):
- $x = Nono$
- **Derive:** $Hostile(Nono)$

**Iteration 4:** Fire rule (1) again:
- All four premises now satisfied
- **Derive:** $Criminal(West)$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Chaining Property:** It is **complete** for definite clauses (Horn clauses with exactly one positive literal). It derives the **least fixed point** of the KB.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Resolution Refutation

**Goal:** Prove $Criminal(West)$

**Step 1 — Add the negation of goal:**
$$\lnot Criminal(West)$$

**Step 2 — Combine with rule (1) (after CNF):**
$$C_4: \lnot American(x) \lor \lnot Weapon(y) \lor \lnot Sells(x,y,z) \lor \lnot Hostile(z) \lor Criminal(x)$$

**Step 3 — Resolve with $\lnot Criminal(West)$:**

Use substitution $\{x/West\}$. Resolvent:
$$C_5: \lnot American(West) \lor \lnot Weapon(y) \lor \lnot Sells(West,y,z) \lor \lnot Hostile(z)$$

**Step 4 — Resolve with $American(West)$:**
$$C_6: \lnot Weapon(y) \lor \lnot Sells(West,y,z) \lor \lnot Hostile(z)$$

**Step 5 — Continue resolving** through $Sells$, $Hostile$, $Weapon$ premises until the **empty clause $\square$** is derived.

**Conclusion:** $\square$ derived $\Rightarrow$ Goal is refuted $\Rightarrow$ $KB \models Criminal(West)$ ✓

## 3.5 Truth Table Construction (PL Foundation)

For a formula with $n$ atomic propositions, the truth table has $2^n$ rows.

**Example:** $(P \Rightarrow Q) \Leftrightarrow (\lnot P \lor Q)$

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $\lnot P$ | $\lnot P \lor Q$ | Equivalence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | F | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | F | T |
| F | T | T | T | T | T |
| F | F | T | T | T | T |

All T's in the last column $\Rightarrow$ formula is a **tautology** (valid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A formula is **valid** iff its truth table is all T's. A formula is **satisfiable** iff at least one T exists. A formula is **unsatisfiable** (contradiction) iff all F's.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Knowledge-Based Agent Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KB[Knowledge Base T]
    IE[Inference Engine]
    TELL[TELL operation]
    ASK[ASK operation]
    SENSORS[Percept Sensors]
    WORLD[Environment World]
    ACTUATORS[Effectors]

    SENSORS --> TELL
    WORLD --> SENSORS
    TELL --> KB
    KB --> IE
    IE --> ASK
    ASK --> ACTUATORS
    ACTUATORS --> WORLD
```

## 4.2 FOL Knowledge Base Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[English Statement] --> B[Identify Objects Predicates]
    B --> C[Identify Quantifiers]
    C --> D[Construct FOL Formula]
    D --> E{Is it a Horn Clause?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Forward or Backward Chaining]
    E -- No --> G[Convert to CNF]
    G --> H[Apply Resolution]
    F --> I[Derive Conclusion]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Resolution Refutation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Step 1: KB sentences to CNF] --> P2[Step 2: Negate the goal query]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Add negated goal to clause set]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Repeatedly resolve complementary literals]
    P4 --> P5{Empty clause box derived}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Goal is PROVEN refutation complete]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Resolution exhausted]
    P7 --> P8[Goal not entailed by KB]
```

## 4.4 Forward Chaining Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Initialize Facts set with KB facts] --> F2{Any rule with all premises in facts}
    F2 -- Yes --> F3[Fire the rule add conclusion to facts]
    F3 --> F4{New fact added}
    F4 -- Yes --> F2
    F4 -- No --> F5[Fixed point reached terminate]
    F2 -- No --> F5
    F5 --> F6[Check if query is in final facts]
```

## 4.5 Backward Chaining Goal Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Goal: Prove Q] --> G2{Q is a fact in KB}
    G2 -- Yes --> G3[Success]
    G2 -- No --> G4{A rule P1 P2 ... Pn implies Q}
    G4 -- Yes --> G5[Recursively prove P1]
    G4 -- No --> G6[Failure]
    G5 --> G7[Recursively prove P2]
    G7 --> G8[Recursively prove Pn]
    G8 --> G9{All subgoals proven}
    G9 -- Yes --> G3
    G9 -- No --> G6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Propositional Logic with an example. List its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Propositional Logic is a formal language for representing knowledge using atomic propositions (declarative statements that are either True or False) and logical connectives ($\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$).

**Example:** Let $P$ = "It is sunny", $Q$ = "I carry an umbrella". Then $P \Rightarrow \lnot Q$ means "If it is sunny, I will not carry an umbrella."

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **No internal structure** — cannot express "Socrates is mortal" with the underlying reason "all humans are mortal."
2. **No variables or quantifiers** — cannot generalize over objects.
3. **Expressive weakness** — facts about many objects require exponentially many propositions.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Modus Ponens (MP):** The most fundamental inference rule.
$$\dfrac{P, \; P \Rightarrow Q}{\therefore Q}$$
**Example:** "It is raining ($P$). If it is raining, the ground is wet ($P \Rightarrow Q$). Therefore, the ground is wet ($Q$)."

**Modus Tollens (MT):** Reasoning backwards through an implication.
$$\dfrac{\lnot Q, \; P \Rightarrow Q}{\therefore \lnot P}$$
**Example:** "The ground is not wet ($\lnot Q$). If it is raining, the ground is wet ($P \Rightarrow Q$). Therefore, it is not raining ($\lnot P$)."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Convert the following sentence into **Prenex Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**:
$$\forall X \; [\, \lnot (\, \forall Y \; P(X,Y) \,) \lor (\, \exists Z \; Q(X,Z) \,) \,]$$
**(7 marks)**

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Eliminate the implication:** None present, skip.

**Step 2 — Push negations inward:**
Apply $\lnot \forall Y \; P(X,Y) \equiv \exists Y \; \lnot P(X,Y)$:
$$\forall X \; [\, (\exists Y \; \lnot P(X,Y)) \lor (\exists Z \; Q(X,Z)) \,]$$

**Step 3 — Standardize variables:** Rename $Z$ to $W$:
$$\forall X \; [\, (\exists Y \; \lnot P(X,Y)) \lor (\exists W \; Q(X,W)) \,]$$

**Step 4 — Skolemize:**
- $\exists Y$ inside $\forall X$: $Y \rightarrow f(X)$
- $\exists W$ inside $\forall X$: $W \rightarrow g(X)$

$$\forall X \; [\, \lnot P(X, f(X)) \lor Q(X, g(X)) \,]$$

**Step 5 — Drop universal quantifier** and distribute:
$$\lnot P(X, f(X)) \lor Q(X, g(X))$$

**Final CNF Clause:**
$$\boxed{\{\lnot P(X, f(X)), \; Q(X, g(X))\}}$$

---

**b)** Apply the **Unification algorithm** to find the MGU of:
$$P(a, X, f(g(Y))) \quad \text{and} \quad P(Z, f(b), f(W))$$
**(7 marks)**

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** $P = P$ ✓, recurse on arguments.

**Step 2:** Unify $a$ with $Z$: $\theta_1 = \{Z/a\}$
Result: $P(a, X, f(g(Y)))$ vs $P(a, f(b), f(W))$

**Step 3:** Unify $X$ with $f(b)$: $\theta_2 = \{X/f(b)\}$
Result: $P(a, f(b), f(g(Y)))$ vs $P(a, f(b), f(W))$

**Step 4:** Unify $f(g(Y))$ with $f(W)$: $f$ matches, recurse on $g(Y)$ vs $W$.
$\theta_3 = \{W/g(Y)\}$

**Step 5:** Apply all substitutions:
$$\boxed{\theta_{MGU} = \{Z/a, \; X/f(b), \; W/g(Y)\}}$$

**Unified Expression:** $P(a, f(b), f(g(Y)))$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Consider the following **Knowledge Base**:
- $F_1: \forall x \; (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$
- $F_2: King(John)$
- $F_3: Greedy(John)$

Prove $Evil(John)$ using **Forward Chaining**. Show every iteration.
**(7 marks)**

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Initial Facts Set:** $\{King(John), \; Greedy(John)\}$

**Iteration 1:** Check rule $F_1$:
- Match: $King(x) \rightarrow King(John)$, so $x = John$
- Match: $Greedy(x) \rightarrow Greedy(John)$, consistent
- **Derive:** $Evil(John)$

**Iteration 2:** Re-check rules — no new facts derivable.

**Updated Facts Set:** $\{King(John), \; Greedy(John), \; Evil(John)\}$

**Query Check:** Is $Evil(John)$ in the facts set? **YES ✓**

**Conclusion:** $KB \models Evil(John)$ proved via Forward Chaining in 1 iteration.

---

**b)** Using the same KB, prove $Evil(John)$ using **Backward Chaining**. Show the goal tree.
**(7 marks)**

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Goal:** $Evil(John)$

**Step 1:** Search KB for $Evil(John)$ — not a direct fact.

**Step 2:** Find rule with $Evil$ in head: $F_1$ ($King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)$)
- Unify $Evil(x)$ with $Evil(John)$: substitution $\{x/John\}$
- **New Subgoals:** $King(John)$ and $Greedy(John)$

**Step 3:** Prove $King(John)$ — found in $F_2$. ✓

**Step 4:** Prove $Greedy(John)$ — found in $F_3$. ✓

**Goal Tree:**
```
GOAL: Evil(John)
   └── Rule F1 (x=John)
          ├── SUBGOAL: King(John)  ← FACT F2 ✓
          └── SUBGOAL: Greedy(John) ← FACT F3 ✓
   └── RESULT: PROVEN ✓
```

**Conclusion:** $KB \models Evil(John)$ proved via Backward Chaining.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **CNF Conversion:** Forgetting to standardize variables apart BEFORE skolemization (–1 mark per step missed).
> 2. **Unification:** Applying substitution to BOTH sides before continuing the recursion (–2 marks).
> 3. **Forward Chaining:** Not showing the iteration number and the new fact added in each step (–2 marks).
> 4. **Backward Chaining:** Failing to draw the goal tree — examiners expect it explicitly.
> 5. **Resolution:** Not including the substitution used in each resolution step (–1 mark per missing substitution).
> 6. **Quantifier Negation:** Writing $\lnot \forall \equiv \forall \lnot$ instead of $\lnot \forall \equiv \exists \lnot$ (instant 0 for that step).
> 7. **Confusing soundness vs completeness:** Soundness = "no false conclusions"; Completeness = "all true conclusions derivable."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Logic):**

- **Propositional Logic** handles only True/False atoms and 5 connectives.
- **FOL** adds variables, predicates, functions, and the two quantifiers $\forall, \exists$.
- **Implication elimination** rule: $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$.
- **De Morgan's laws** are the most-tested equivalences in KTU exams.
- **Modus Ponens** is the workhorse of forward and backward chaining.
- **CNF conversion** has 6 strict steps: $\rightarrow$ Impl. Elim. $\rightarrow$ Neg. Inward $\rightarrow$ Std. Vars. $\rightarrow$ Skolemize $\rightarrow$ Drop $\forall$ $\rightarrow$ Distribute.
- **Skolem functions** depend on the surrounding **universal** quantifiers only.
- **Unification** finds the MGU by recursive structural matching with an **occurs check**.
- **Forward Chaining** = data-driven; **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven.
- **Resolution** is **refutation complete** for FOL — you prove $\lnot G$ is unsatisfiable.
- **Validity vs Satisfiability:** Valid = true everywhere; Satisfiable = true somewhere.
- **Soundness + Completeness** are the two quality metrics for any inference algorithm.
- **Horn clauses** have at most one positive literal — the basis of PROLOG and FC/BC.
- **Entailment** $KB \models \alpha$ means: every model of KB is a model of $\alpha$.
