<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 · Propositional Logic

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Propositional Logic (also called Propositional Calculus or Sentential Logic) is a branch of mathematical logic that deals with the relationships between whole propositions (statements) considered as atomic units. A proposition is a declarative sentence that is unambiguously either **True (T)** or **False (F)** — never both, never neither.*

The word **proposition** originates from the Latin *propositio*, meaning "a setting forth" or "statement". In Artificial Intelligence, propositional logic forms the **foundational layer** of Knowledge Representation & Reasoning. Before we can make a robot reason, plan, or diagnose a disease, we need a language in which facts and rules can be precisely stated, combined, and mechanically verified — propositional logic is that language.

### 1.1 The Building Blocks — Atoms & Connectives

| Symbol | Name | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $P, Q, R, \dots$ | Propositional Variables (Atoms) | Indivisible factual statements |
| $\lnot$ | Negation (NOT) | "It is not the case that…" |
| $\land$ | Conjunction (AND) | "Both … and …" |
| $\lor$ | Disjunction (OR) | "Either … or … (or both)" |
| $\Rightarrow$ | Implication (IF-THEN) | "If … then …" |
| $\Leftrightarrow$ | Biconditional (IFF) | "… if and only if …" |
| $\top, \bot$ | Tautology, Contradiction | Always True, Always False |

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Courtroom Lawyer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Courtroom Trial**
> Imagine you are a defence lawyer in a courtroom. You cannot argue with the witness; you can only **combine facts** that have already been accepted as evidence. Each piece of evidence is a **proposition** ("The accused was at the scene", "The CCTV footage is authentic"). Using connectives like AND (joint evidence), OR (alternative suspects), NOT (ruling out alibis), and IF-THEN (drawing conclusions from facts), you build a logical case. A valid argument is one where, *if all premises are true, the conclusion MUST be true* — there is no logical loophole for the prosecutor to escape through.

### 1.3 What is *Not* a Proposition?

A sentence fails to be a proposition if it is:
1. **A question** — "Is it raining?" (No truth value yet)
2. **A command** — "Close the door!" (No truth value)
3. **A paradox** — "This statement is false." (Cannot be consistently True or False)
4. **Subjective/Emotive** — "Chocolate is delicious." (Truth varies by person)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test for a Proposition:** Ask the question *"Is this sentence definitely TRUE or definitely FALSE?"* If the answer is *yes*, it is a proposition. If the answer is *it depends*, *maybe*, or *cannot tell*, it is not a proposition suitable for propositional logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Syntax of Propositional Logic (The Grammar)

The **syntax** tells us *how to write* valid logical formulas; it says nothing about truth. Formulas are built recursively:

1. Every propositional symbol $P_i$ is a formula.
2. The constants $\top$ (true) and $\bot$ (false) are formulas.
3. If $\alpha$ is a formula, then $\lnot \alpha$ is a formula.
4. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are formulas, then $(\alpha \land \beta)$, $(\alpha \lor \beta)$, $(\alpha \Rightarrow \beta)$, $(\alpha \Leftrightarrow \beta)$ are formulas.
5. Nothing else is a formula (closure under the above rules).

## 2.2 Semantics (The Meaning) — Truth Tables

The **semantics** assigns truth values. Every connective is defined exhaustively by its truth table:

| $P$ | $Q$ | $\lnot P$ | $P \land Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:-----------------:|:----------------------:|
| T | T | F | **T** | **T** | **T** | **T** |
| T | F | F | **F** | **T** | **F** | **F** |
| F | T | T | **F** | **T** | **T** | **F** |
| F | F | T | **F** | **F** | **T** | **T** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Implication Trap:** $P \Rightarrow Q$ is **False ONLY** when $P$ is True and $Q$ is False. In all other cases it is True. This often confuses students because in ordinary English "If…then…" implies a causal connection — in logic there is **no causation**, only a truth-functional relationship.

## 2.3 Important Formula Classes

| Class | Definition | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Tautology (Valid)** | True under every interpretation | Denoted $\models \alpha$ or $\equiv \top$ |
| **Contradiction (Unsatisfiable)** | False under every interpretation | Denoted $\equiv \bot$ |
| **Contingent** | True under some, false under some | Neither tautology nor contradiction |
| **Satisfiable** | True under *at least one* interpretation | All tautologies are satisfiable |

## 2.4 Logical Equivalence ($\equiv$)

Two formulas $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **logically equivalent**, written $\alpha \equiv \beta$, iff they have identical truth tables (i.e., $\models (\alpha \Leftrightarrow \beta)$).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Fundamental Logical Equivalences

| Law | Equivalence |
|---|---|
| **Identity** | $P \land \top \equiv P,\quad P \lor \bot \equiv P$ |
| **Domination** | $P \lor \top \equiv \top,\quad P \land \bot \equiv \bot$ |
| **Idempotent** | $P \lor P \equiv P,\quad P \land P \equiv P$ |
| **Double Negation** | $\lnot(\lnot P) \equiv P$ |
| **Commutative** | $P \land Q \equiv Q \land P,\quad P \lor Q \equiv Q \lor P$ |
| **Associative** | $(P \land Q) \land R \equiv P \land (Q \land R)$ |
| **Distributive** | $P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R)$ |
| **De Morgan's** | $\lnot(P \land Q) \equiv \lnot P \lor \lnot Q$ |
| **De Morgan's** | $\lnot(P \lor Q) \equiv \lnot P \land \lnot Q$ |
| **Implication** | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$ |
| **Contrapositive** | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot Q \Rightarrow \lnot P$ |
| **Biconditional** | $P \Leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \Rightarrow Q) \land (Q \Rightarrow P)$ |

## 2.5 Inference Rules for Deduction (Sound Rules)

| Rule | Form | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens** | From $P,\ P \Rightarrow Q$ infer $Q$ | Affirming the antecedent |
| **Modus Tollens** | From $\lnot Q,\ P \Rightarrow Q$ infer $\lnot P$ | Denying the consequent |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism** | From $P \Rightarrow Q,\ Q \Rightarrow R$ infer $P \Rightarrow R$ | Chain of implications |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism** | From $P \lor Q,\ \lnot P$ infer $Q$ | Elimination of one disjunct |
| **Resolution** | From $P \lor Q,\ \lnot P \lor R$ infer $Q \lor R$ | The single most important rule for AI |
| **AND-Elimination** | From $P \land Q$ infer $P$ (and $Q$) | Decomposing a conjunction |
| **AND-Introduction** | From $P,\ Q$ infer $P \land Q$ | Combining facts |

> [!TIP]
> **Resolution** is the *only* inference rule needed for a complete theorem prover for propositional logic — this is why it is the workhorse of automated reasoning, SAT solvers, and Prolog's inference engine.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Circuit Design:** A propositional formula $\Leftrightarrow$ a Boolean circuit. Minimising a formula = simplifying gates.
- **Software Verification:** Hoare Logic, model checking, and assertion proving all reduce to propositional satisfiability (SAT).
- **Search Engines & Knowledge Graphs:** Rule-based systems use propositional inference for fact chaining.
- **Expert Systems (MYCIN, DENDRAL):** Medical diagnosis rules are essentially propositional Horn clauses.
- **SAT Solvers (DPLL, CDCL):** Power modern hardware verification, cryptography, and AI planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — De Morgan's Law (Exhaustive Truth-Table Proof)

**Statement to Prove:** $\lnot(P \land Q) \equiv \lnot P \lor \lnot Q$

We construct the joint truth table and verify the final columns are identical.

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \land Q$ | $\lnot(P \land Q)$ | $\lnot P$ | $\lnot Q$ | $\lnot P \lor \lnot Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------------------:|
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |
| T | F | F | **T** | F | T | **T** |
| F | T | F | **T** | T | F | **T** |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |

**Step 1 (Row 1):** $P=T, Q=T \Rightarrow P \land Q = T \Rightarrow \lnot(P \land Q) = F$.  
**Step 2 (Row 1 cont.):** $\lnot P = F,\ \lnot Q = F \Rightarrow \lnot P \lor \lnot Q = F \lor F = F$.  
**Step 3 (Row 2):** $P=T, Q=F \Rightarrow P \land Q = F \Rightarrow \lnot(P \land Q) = T$.  
**Step 4 (Row 2 cont.):** $\lnot P = F,\ \lnot Q = T \Rightarrow \lnot P \lor \lnot Q = F \lor T = T$.  
**Step 5 (Row 3):** $P=F, Q=T \Rightarrow P \land Q = F \Rightarrow \lnot(P \land Q) = T$.  
**Step 6 (Row 3 cont.):** $\lnot P = T,\ \lnot Q = F \Rightarrow \lnot P \lor \lnot Q = T \lor F = T$.  
**Step 7 (Row 4):** $P=F, Q=F \Rightarrow P \land Q = F \Rightarrow \lnot(P \land Q) = T$.  
**Step 8 (Row 4 cont.):** $\lnot P = T,\ \lnot Q = T \Rightarrow \lnot P \lor \lnot Q = T \lor T = T$.

**Conclusion:** The two output columns $\lnot(P \land Q)$ and $\lnot P \lor \lnot Q$ are **identical in every row**, hence the equivalence is verified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Implication Equivalence via Truth Table

**Statement to Prove:** $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $\lnot P$ | $\lnot P \lor Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:----------------:|
| T | T | **T** | F | **T** |
| T | F | **F** | F | **F** |
| F | T | **T** | T | **T** |
| F | F | **T** | T | **T** |

**Step-by-step justification of each row:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1: } & P=T, Q=T \\
& P \Rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(by definition of $\Rightarrow$)} \\
& \lnot P = F,\ \lnot P \lor Q = F \lor T = T \\
& \text{Both columns equal } T. \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 2: } & P=T, Q=F \\
& P \Rightarrow Q = F \quad \text{(only false case)} \\
& \lnot P = F,\ \lnot P \lor Q = F \lor F = F \\
& \text{Both columns equal } F. \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 3: } & P=F, Q=T \\
& P \Rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(vacuous truth)} \\
& \lnot P = T,\ \lnot P \lor Q = T \lor T = T \\
& \text{Both columns equal } T. \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 4: } & P=F, Q=F \\
& P \Rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(vacuous truth)} \\
& \lnot P = T,\ \lnot P \lor Q = T \lor F = T \\
& \text{Both columns equal } T. \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Since all **four** rows match, the equivalence holds for all interpretations. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof of Validity using Resolution

**Problem:** Prove that the following argument is **logically valid**:

$$
\text{Premise 1: } P \lor Q \\
\text{Premise 2: } \lnot P \\
\text{Premise 3: } (P \lor Q) \Rightarrow R \\
\hline
\text{Conclusion: } R
$$

**Step 1 — Convert all premises to Clause Form (CNF).**

Convert Premise 3 using $A \Rightarrow B \equiv \lnot A \lor B$:

$$
(P \lor Q) \Rightarrow R \equiv \lnot(P \lor Q) \lor R
$$

Apply De Morgan's to $\lnot(P \lor Q)$:

$$
\equiv (\lnot P \land \lnot Q) \lor R
$$

Apply Distributive law $(A \land B) \lor C \equiv (A \lor C) \land (B \lor C)$:

$$
\equiv (\lnot P \lor R) \land (\lnot Q \lor R)
$$

**Step 2 — Collect all clauses:**

$$
\text{(i) } P \lor Q \qquad \text{(ii) } \lnot P \qquad \text{(iii) } \lnot P \lor R \qquad \text{(iv) } \lnot Q \lor R
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Resolution systematically.**

Resolve (i) and (ii): complement is $P$ in (i) and $\lnot P$ in (ii), drop them, keep remainder $Q$.

$$
\text{(v) } Q \qquad \text{[Resolution of (i) and (ii)]}
$$

Resolve (iv) and (v): complement is $Q$ in (iv) and $\lnot Q$ (implicit) in (v); we rewrite (v) as $Q \lor \bot$, so resolution yields $R$.

$$
\text{(vi) } R \qquad \text{[Resolution of (iv) and (v)]}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Since the empty clause would be needed to reach a contradiction and we instead *derived the goal clause* $R$, the argument is **valid**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Reasoning Style

| Reasoning Task | Method | Time Complexity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Checking a single inference | Truth Table | $O(2^{n})$ | Small $n \le 5$ |
| Checking a single inference | Natural Deduction | Polynomial | Human-readable proofs |
| Checking satisfiability | SAT Solver (DPLL/CDCL) | NP-complete (worst) | Large CNFs, hardware |
| Proving theorem from KB | Forward / Backward Chaining | Polynomial | Horn-clause KBs |
| General automated proof | Resolution Refutation | Exponential worst | General FOL (extended) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Architecture of Propositional Logic Reasoning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Knowledge Base KB] --> nodeB[Parse Each Statement into Atomic Propositions P, Q, R]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Choose Connective: NOT AND OR IMPLIES IFF}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Construct Formula alpha using Syntax Rules]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Build Truth Table over all 2 power n interpretations]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Is formula True in every row?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Classify as TAUTOLOGY - Valid WFF]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH{Is formula False in every row?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Classify as CONTRADICTION - Unsatisfiable]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[Classify as CONTINGENT - Satisfiable]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Apply Resolution / Modus Ponens to derive new clauses]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Add derived clause back to KB]
    nodeL --> nodeM{Goal clause derived?}
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeN[PROVED - Argument Valid]
    nodeM -- No --> nodeO[Backtrack and Try Alternative Inference]
    nodeO --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Resolution Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Premise: P or Q] --> res1
    p2[Premise: not P or R] --> res1
    res1[Resolution Step] --> out1[Derived Clause: Q or R]
    p3[Premise: not R] --> res2
    out1 --> res2
    res2[Resolution Step] --> out2[Derived Clause: Q]
    p4[Goal: Prove Q] --> match{Unify with Derived Clause}
    out2 --> match
    match -- Yes --> success[VALID ARGUMENT - GOAL PROVED]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Truth Table Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Begin with formula having n propositional symbols] --> gen[Generate 2 power n rows of truth assignments]
    gen --> eval[For each row, evaluate atomic symbols left to right]
    eval --> col[Compute final column of formula]
    col --> decide{All True?}
    decide -- Yes --> taut[Tautology - Always Valid]
    decide -- No --> decide2{All False?}
    decide2 -- Yes --> contra[Contradiction - Unsatisfiable]
    decide2 -- No --> conting[Contingent - Truth Depends on Interpretation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners often award partial marks for a **labelled flowchart** of the reasoning process even when the final answer is incomplete. Drawing the *structure* of the proof — premises → connectives → inference → conclusion — is a high-yield presentation trick.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a proposition. Give two examples of sentences that are not propositions, with justification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A *proposition* is a declarative sentence that is either unambiguously **True (T)** or **False (F)** but not both.
- **Example 1 (1 Mark):** *"What is the time?"* — This is a question; it has no truth value, hence not a proposition.
- **Example 2 (1 Mark):** *"Close the window!"* — This is a command/imperative sentence; commands do not assert a fact, hence not a proposition.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and prove the idempotent law of propositional logic using a truth table.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** $P \lor P \equiv P$ and $P \land P \equiv P$

**Truth Table Proof for $P \lor P \equiv P$:**

| $P$ | $P$ | $P \lor P$ | $P$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:---:|
| T | T | **T** | **T** |
| F | F | **F** | **F** |

Since columns 3 and 4 are identical (1 Mark each column match + 1 Mark statement), the law is proved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the syntax and semantics of propositional logic with a suitable example. Construct the truth table for the formula: $(P \Rightarrow Q) \land (\lnot Q \lor R)$ and determine whether it is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Using the resolution method, prove the validity of the following argument:*
- *Premise 1: If it rains, the ground is wet. ($R \Rightarrow W$)*
- *Premise 2: If the ground is wet, the match is cancelled. ($W \Rightarrow C$)*
- *Premise 3: The match is not cancelled. ($\lnot C$)*
- *Conclusion: Therefore, it did not rain. ($\lnot R$)*

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

**Syntax (2 Marks):** Propositional logic syntax defines the grammatical structure of well-formed formulas (WFFs). Rules: (i) Atomic symbols $P, Q, R, \dots$ are WFFs. (ii) Constants $\top, \bot$ are WFFs. (iii) If $\alpha, \beta$ are WFFs then so are $\lnot \alpha$, $\alpha \land \beta$, $\alpha \lor \beta$, $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$, $\alpha \Leftrightarrow \beta$.

**Semantics (2 Marks):** Semantics assigns meaning — a truth value (T or F) to each formula under a given interpretation. Each connective has a truth-functional definition given by its truth table.

**Example (1 Mark):** "It is raining AND the ground is wet" → $R \land W$.

**Truth Table Construction for $(P \Rightarrow Q) \land (\lnot Q \lor R)$ (2 Marks):**

| $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $\lnot Q$ | $\lnot Q \lor R$ | $(P \Rightarrow Q) \land (\lnot Q \lor R)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:----------------:|:------------------------------------------:|
| T | T | T | **T** | F | **T** | **T** |
| T | T | F | **T** | F | **F** | **F** |
| T | F | T | **F** | T | **T** | **F** |
| T | F | F | **F** | T | **T** | **F** |
| F | T | T | **T** | F | **T** | **T** |
| F | T | F | **T** | F | **F** | **F** |
| F | F | T | **T** | T | **T** | **T** |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | **T** | **T** |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Since the final column contains both T and F, the formula is a **Contingency** (satisfiable but not a tautology).

---

**Part (b) Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert to CNF (3 Marks):**

- Premise 1: $R \Rightarrow W \equiv \lnot R \lor W$
- Premise 2: $W \Rightarrow C \equiv \lnot W \lor C$
- Premise 3: $\lnot C$ (already in CNF)
- Negated Conclusion: $R$ (we negate the conclusion to derive contradiction)

**Step 2 — List all clauses (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{(i) } \lnot R \lor W \qquad \text{(ii) } \lnot W \lor C \qquad \text{(iii) } \lnot C \qquad \text{(iv) } R
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Resolution sequentially (3 Marks):**

Resolve (i) and (iv): on $R$ and $\lnot R$. Result: $W$.

$$
\text{(v) } W \quad \text{[from (i),(iv)]}
$$

Resolve (ii) and (v): on $W$ and $\lnot W$. Result: $C$.

$$
\text{(vi) } C \quad \text{[from (ii),(v)]}
$$

Resolve (iii) and (vi): on $C$ and $\lnot C$. Result: empty clause.

$$
\text{(vii) } \square \quad \text{[CONTRADICTION from (iii),(vi)]}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark):** The empty clause $\square$ is derived from the premises plus the negated conclusion. Therefore our assumption that the conclusion is false leads to a contradiction, hence the original conclusion $\lnot R$ is **valid**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *State De Morgan's Laws. Prove both laws using truth tables. Mention one real-world application of each law in digital circuit design.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider the following knowledge base:*
- *P1: $A \lor B$*
- *P2: $\lnot A$*
- *P3: $A \lor C$*
- *P4: $\lnot C \lor D$*

*Using forward chaining, derive all possible new facts. Is $D$ entailed by the KB?*

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

**Statement of Laws (1 Mark each = 2 Marks):**
- $\lnot(P \land Q) \equiv \lnot P \lor \lnot Q$
- $\lnot(P \lor Q) \equiv \lnot P \land \lnot Q$

**Proof of Law 1 (2 Marks):** Already established in Section 3.1 of these notes — refer to the joint truth table showing both sides produce F, T, T, T respectively. They match.

**Proof of Law 2 (2 Marks):**

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $\lnot(P \lor Q)$ | $\lnot P$ | $\lnot Q$ | $\lnot P \land \lnot Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:----------------------:|
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |
| T | F | T | **F** | F | T | **F** |
| F | T | T | **F** | T | F | **F** |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |

Both columns identical ⇒ Law 2 proved.

**Application (1 Mark):** De Morgan's Law allows an OR-gate followed by a NOT (NOR) to be replaced by an AND of two NOT gates, and vice-versa (NAND). This is the foundation of universal gate design in CMOS circuits.

---

**Part (b) Model Answer:**

**Initial KB Facts (1 Mark):** $\{A \lor B,\ \lnot A,\ A \lor C,\ \lnot C \lor D\}$

**Step 1 — Forward Chain using Disjunctive Syllogism on P1 and P2 (2 Marks):**

$P1: A \lor B$ and $P2: \lnot A \Rightarrow$ by Disjunctive Syllogism derive $B$.

**Step 2 — Try other inference (2 Marks):**

$P3: A \lor C$ and $P2: \lnot A \Rightarrow$ by Disjunctive Syllogism derive $C$.

**Step 3 — Chain further (2 Marks):**

$P4: \lnot C \lor D$ and newly derived $C \Rightarrow$ by Modus Ponens (since $C \Rightarrow D$ is equivalent to $\lnot C \lor D$) derive $D$.

**Final Derived Facts (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{KB after forward chaining: } \{B,\ C,\ D\}
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, $D$ is **logically entailed** by the KB. We write $KB \models D$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls Where KTU Students Frequently Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\Rightarrow$ with $\Leftrightarrow$:** A biconditional demands equivalence both ways; an implication is one-way. Do NOT write $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ when only $P \Rightarrow Q$ holds.
> 2. **Skipping the Negated-Conclusion step in Resolution:** To prove validity, you must add the *negation* of the goal clause to the KB and derive the **empty clause ($\square$)**. Deriving the goal itself (without negation) is an incomplete proof by KTU standards and attracts partial credit only.
> 3. **Forgetting CNF conversion:** You cannot apply resolution directly to arbitrary formulas — they must first be converted to **Conjunctive Normal Form** using $\Rightarrow$ elimination, De Morgan's, distributive law, and clause separation. Skipping this step is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Misreading material implication:** Remember $P \Rightarrow Q$ is **true** whenever $P$ is false ("vacuously true"). This trips up 40% of students in truth-table problems.
> 5. **Missing the column labels and binary choices in truth tables:** Every column header must be written, and rows must cover all $2^n$ combinations. An incomplete table = 0 marks for that question.
> 6. **Not stating the rule used:** Each resolution step must cite *which two clauses were resolved* and *which literal was eliminated*. Bare derived clauses without provenance lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Propositional Logic**

- ☐ A **proposition** is a declarative sentence with a definite truth value (T or F). Questions, commands, paradoxes are *not* propositions.
- ☐ The **five core connectives** are $\lnot, \land, \lor, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$. Master their truth tables — they appear in **every** KTU paper.
- ☐ **Material implication** $P \Rightarrow Q$ is false only in the single case $P=T, Q=F$; it is **vacuously true** when $P$ is false.
- ☐ **Logical equivalence** $\alpha \equiv \beta$ means identical truth tables; equivalently $\models (\alpha \Leftrightarrow \beta)$.
- ☐ **De Morgan's Laws** are the most-tested equivalence: $\lnot(P \land Q) \equiv \lnot P \lor \lnot Q$ and $\lnot(P \lor Q) \equiv \lnot P \land \lnot Q$.
- ☐ **Implication rewriting:** $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$ — essential for converting to CNF.
- ☐ **Contrapositive** $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot Q \Rightarrow \lnot P$ is logically equivalent (but not the same as converse or inverse).
- ☐ **Modus Ponens** ($P, P \Rightarrow Q \vdash Q$) and **Modus Tollens** ($\lnot Q, P \Rightarrow Q \vdash \lnot P$) are the foundational inference rules.
- ☐ **Resolution** is a complete inference rule for propositional logic; to prove a goal $G$ from KB, add $\lnot G$ and derive the **empty clause**.
- ☐ **Resolution requires CNF:** Convert using $\Rightarrow$ elimination → De Morgan's → Distributive law → Clause separation.
- ☐ A formula with **$n$ propositional symbols** has exactly **$2^n$ rows** in its truth table.
- ☐ A **tautology** is true in all $2^n$ rows; a **contradiction** is false in all rows; everything else is **contingent**.
- ☐ **Forward chaining** derives new facts until no more inferences possible; **backward chaining** starts from the goal and works backwards.
- ☐ Application domains: digital logic, SAT solvers, expert systems, knowledge representation, automated theorem proving.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Propositional Logic**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reasoning Patterns in Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reasoning Patterns in Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reasoning Patterns in Propositional Logic** refer to the systematic, rule-based methods used by an intelligent agent to derive new logical conclusions (theorems) from a given set of premises (knowledge base) using well-defined inference rules. These patterns form the mathematical backbone of automated theorem proving, knowledge-based systems, and logic programming.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, reasoning patterns are categorized into:
- **Syntactic Reasoning (Proof-based):** Manipulating logical sentences purely based on their structure (e.g., Modus Ponens, Resolution).
- **Semantic Reasoning (Model-based):** Enumerating truth assignments to verify entailment (Truth Table Method).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes *Resolution as a complete inference procedure* for propositional logic, along with the foundational inference rules (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, etc.) and their application in Forward and Backward Chaining algorithms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Courtroom Detective

Imagine a **deductive detective** in a courtroom. The judge has a stack of verified facts (the **Knowledge Base**). The detective wants to prove the defendant is guilty (the **Query / Goal**). The detective cannot look inside the defendant's mind — they can only use **rules of evidence** (inference rules) to chain facts together:

- If witness says "John was at the scene" and the camera confirms "The robber was at the scene" → we can infer "John was the robber" (**Modus Ponens**).
- If we want to prove "It rained" but cannot prove it directly, we assume the opposite "It did NOT rain" and derive a contradiction (**Proof by Contradiction / Refutation**).

This is *exactly* how AI systems reason. The Knowledge Base is the evidence, the rules are the inference rules, and the goal is the verdict.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Master:**
> - **Premise:** A statement assumed to be true.
> - **Inference Rule:** A template for generating new valid sentences from existing ones.
> - **Soundness:** A reasoning system is *sound* if it only derives true conclusions from true premises.
> - **Completeness:** A reasoning system is *complete* if it can derive *any* true conclusion that logically follows from the premises.

### 1.3 The Fundamental Reasoning Equation

The core goal of any reasoning pattern can be expressed as:

$$KB \models \alpha$$

This reads as: "The Knowledge Base **entails** the sentence α." In other words, in **every** world (model) where the KB is true, α is also true. Reasoning patterns provide the *mechanism* to prove this entailment holds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Taxonomy of Inference Rules

Inference rules in propositional logic operate on a pattern of the form:

$$\frac{\text{premise}_1, \text{premise}_2, \dots, \text{premise}_n}{\text{conclusion}}$$

The line above is a **turnstile** $(\vdash)$ meaning "derives." Each rule guarantees that if the premises are true, the conclusion must be true (logical entailment).

### 2.2 The "Big Seven" Inference Rules (High-Yield for KTU)

#### 2.2.1 Modus Ponens (Affirming the Antecedent)
The most famous rule in all of logic.

$$\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad P}{Q}$$

- **Logic:** If P implies Q, and P is true, then Q must be true.
- **Real-world example:** If it rains → the ground is wet. It rains. Therefore, the ground is wet.

#### 2.2.2 Modus Tollens (Denying the Consequent)
$$\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad \neg Q}{\neg P}$$

- **Logic:** If P implies Q, and Q is false, then P must also be false.
- **Real-world example:** If the fuse is blown → lights are off. Lights are ON. Therefore, the fuse is NOT blown.

#### 2.2.3 Hypothetical Syllogism (Chaining Implications)
$$\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad Q \rightarrow R}{P \rightarrow R}$$

- **Logic:** Transitivity of implication.

#### 2.2.4 Disjunctive Syllogism
$$\frac{P \vee Q, \quad \neg P}{Q}$$

- **Logic:** One of P or Q holds; P does not hold; therefore Q holds.

#### 2.2.5 Addition (Or-Introduction)
$$\frac{P}{P \vee Q}$$

#### 2.2.6 Simplification (And-Elimination)
$$\frac{P \wedge Q}{P} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{P \wedge Q}{Q}$$

#### 2.2.7 Resolution (The Complete Rule)
$$\frac{P \vee Q, \quad \neg P \vee R}{Q \vee R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Resolution is the "King" Rule:** Resolution is *refutation-complete* for propositional logic. This means: if a sentence α is entailed by a KB, then Resolution will *eventually* derive a contradiction proving $\neg \alpha$, guaranteeing termination. Every other rule is essentially a special case of resolution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Rule Name | Logical Form | Mnemonic Keyword | Forward / Backward |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modus Ponens | $\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \; P}{Q}$ | Affirm antecedent | Both |
| Modus Tollens | $\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \; \neg Q}{\neg P}$ | Deny consequent | Backward |
| Hypothetical Syllogism | $\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \; Q \rightarrow R}{P \rightarrow R}$ | Chain | Forward |
| Disjunctive Syllogism | $\frac{P \vee Q, \; \neg P}{Q}$ | Eliminate OR | Backward |
| Addition | $\frac{P}{P \vee Q}$ | Weaken / Introduce OR | Forward |
| Simplification | $\frac{P \wedge Q}{P}$ | Eliminate AND | Forward |
| Conjunction | $\frac{P, \; Q}{P \wedge Q}$ | Join | Forward |
| **Resolution** | $\frac{P \vee Q, \; \neg P \vee R}{Q \vee R}$ | Cancel literals | Both |

### 2.4 Classification of Reasoning Patterns

Reasoning patterns are broadly divided into two families:

1. **Forward Chaining (Data-Driven Reasoning):**
   - Starts from the known facts in the KB.
   - Repeatedly applies Modus Ponens to derive all possible conclusions.
   - **Stops** when no new facts can be added OR the goal is reached.
   - Best for: monitoring systems, diagnostic agents.

2. **Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven Reasoning):**
   - Starts from the query/goal.
   - Works backward to find supporting facts in the KB.
   - **Best for:** query-answering systems (e.g., Prolog).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automated Theorem Provers (ATP):** SAT solvers in hardware verification use resolution to prove chip designs are bug-free.
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems (e.g., MYCIN):** Use backward chaining over rule bases to suggest diseases.
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection uses forward chaining to flag attacks from network events.
- **Smart Home IoT:** Rule-based engines use modus ponens to trigger actions (e.g., "if motion detected AND time is night → turn on lights").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof Techniques: The Three Pillars

#### 3.1.1 Direct Proof (Forward Reasoning)

In a direct proof, we start with the premises and apply inference rules step-by-step until we reach the goal.

**Example Problem:** Given:
- $P_1$: $P \rightarrow Q$
- $P_2$: $Q \rightarrow R$
- $P_3$: $P$

**Prove:** $R$

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Step 1:} \quad P \rightarrow Q \quad &\text{(Premise } P_1\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 2:} \quad P \quad &\text{(Premise } P_3\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 3:} \quad Q \quad &\text{(Modus Ponens on Steps 1, 2)} \\
& \text{Step 4:} \quad Q \rightarrow R \quad &\text{(Premise } P_2\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 5:} \quad R \quad &\text{(Modus Ponens on Steps 3, 4)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Proof by Contradiction (Refutation — KTU's Most Asked Pattern)

Instead of proving α directly, we assume $\neg \alpha$ and try to derive the **empty clause** (denoted as $\square$), which represents a contradiction.

**The Refutation Algorithm:**

$$KB \models \alpha \quad \iff \quad KB \cup \{\neg \alpha\} \text{ is unsatisfiable}$$

**Step-by-Step Example (Refutation using Resolution):**

**Given KB:**
- $P_1$: $P \vee Q$
- $P_2$: $\neg P \vee R$
- $P_3$: $\neg R$

**Goal:** Prove $Q$ using refutation.

**Solution Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Step 1:} \quad \neg Q \quad &\text{(Negate the goal to start refutation)} \\
& \text{Step 2:} \quad P \vee Q \quad &\text{(Given } P_1\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 3:} \quad \neg Q \vee \neg P \quad &\text{(Resolvent of Step 2 with itself? NO — use with }\neg Q\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 3 (correct):} \quad P \quad &\text{(Resolution: }P \vee Q \text{ with }\neg Q\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 4:} \quad \neg P \vee R \quad &\text{(Given } P_2\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 5:} \quad R \quad &\text{(Resolution: Step 3 }P\text{ with }P_2\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 6:} \quad \neg R \quad &\text{(Given } P_3\text{)} \\
& \text{Step 7:} \quad \square \quad &\text{(Resolution: Step 5 }R \text{ with Step 6 }\neg R\text{)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we derived a contradiction ($\square$), our assumption $\neg Q$ is false. Therefore, $Q$ is entailed. **QED.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Rule

Use this decision matrix when solving KTU problems:

| If the KB contains... | And you want to prove... | Then the preferred rule is... |
|---|---|---|
| $P \rightarrow Q$ and $P$ | $Q$ | Modus Ponens |
| $P \rightarrow Q$ and $\neg Q$ | $\neg P$ | Modus Tollens |
| $P \vee Q$ and $\neg P$ | $Q$ | Disjunctive Syllogism |
| Multiple $\vee$ clauses | Any conclusion | **Resolution** |
| $P$ alone | $P \vee Q$ | Addition |
| $P \wedge Q$ | $P$ (or $Q$) | Simplification |
| Chained implications $P \rightarrow Q \rightarrow R$ | $P \rightarrow R$ | Hypothetical Syllogism |

### 3.3 Converting to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) — The Prerequisite for Resolution

Resolution requires all sentences in **CNF** (a conjunction of disjunctions of literals). Conversion procedure:

**Step-by-Step CNF Conversion of:** $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (\neg P \rightarrow R)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Step 1: Eliminate } \rightarrow \\
& \equiv (\neg P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R) \\
& \\
& \text{Step 2: Move } \neg \text{ inward (using De Morgan's)} \\
& \text{Already in good shape.} \\
& \\
& \text{Step 3: Standardize variables (no sharing of } P \text{ across sub-formulas)} \\
& \text{Already unique.} \\
& \\
& \text{Step 4: Skolemize (no } \exists \text{ in propositional, so skip)} \\
& \\
& \text{Step 5: Drop } \wedge \text{ (treat as a set of clauses)} \\
& \{ \{\neg P, Q\}, \; \{P, R\} \} \\
& \\
& \text{Step 6: Final CNF form} \\
& (\neg P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Identity Cheat Sheet (for KTU):**
> - $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$
> - $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$
> - $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$
> - $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ (Double Negation)
> - $P \vee (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)$ (Distributivity)

### 3.4 Horn Clauses and Definite Clauses (Forward/Backward Chaining Foundation)

A **Horn Clause** is a clause with **at most one positive literal**. They come in three forms:

1. **Definite Clause (Rule):** $P_1 \wedge P_2 \wedge \dots \wedge P_n \rightarrow Q$ — exactly one positive literal.
2. **Fact:** $P$ (a clause with one positive literal, zero negatives).
3. **Integrity Constraint / Goal Clause:** $\neg P_1 \vee \neg P_2 \vee \dots \vee \neg P_n$ — zero positive literals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Significance:** Forward and Backward Chaining algorithms operate *exclusively* on Horn Clauses. This restriction makes reasoning **linear time** ($O(n)$), which is why real-time expert systems prefer them. Resolution is more general but exponentially harder in the worst case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of the Reasoning Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates the end-to-end flow of a propositional logic reasoning system, from knowledge acquisition to conclusion derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Knowledge Base KB] --> B[Parse Sentences]
    C[Query / Goal alpha] --> D[Negate Goal to get NOT alpha]
    B --> E[Convert to CNF]
    E --> F[Set of Clauses]
    D --> F
    F --> G{Reasoning Strategy}
    G --> H[Forward Chaining]
    G --> I[Backward Chaining]
    G --> J[Resolution Refutation]
    H --> K{Goal Reached?}
    I --> K
    J --> L{Contradiction Derived?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Goal alpha is TRUE]
    L -->|No| N[Goal alpha cannot be proved]
    K -->|Yes| O[Conclude alpha]
    K -->|No| P[Infer New Facts]
    P --> H
```

### 4.2 Inference Rule Hierarchy (Mermaid Graph)

The diagram below maps the relationships between the major inference rules and the resolution rule that subsumes them.

```mermaid
graph LR
    MP[Modus Ponens] -->|special case| RES[Resolution]
    MT[Modus Tollens] -->|special case| RES
    DS[Disjunctive Syllogism] -->|special case| RES
    HS[Hypothetical Syllogism] -->|chain of| MP
    AD[Addition] --> RES
    SIM[Simplification] --> RES
    CNJ[Conjunction] --> RES
    RES -->|operates on| CNF[CNF Clauses]
    CNF -->|input to| RC[Refutation Proof]
```

### 4.3 Forward Chaining Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fact P1] --> R1[Rule: P1 and P2 implies Q]
    F2[Fact P2] --> R1
    R1 --> NF1[New Fact: Q]
    NF1 --> R2[Rule: Q and P3 implies R]
    F3[Fact P3] --> R2
    R2 --> NF2[New Fact: R]
    NF2 --> GOAL{Is R the Goal?}
    GOAL -->|Yes| STOP[Stop: Proved]
    GOAL -->|No| CONT[Continue Chaining]
```

### 4.4 Backward Chaining Goal Reduction Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Goal: Prove R] --> RED1[Reduce to subgoals Q and P3]
    RED1 --> SUB1[Subgoal: Prove Q]
    RED1 --> SUB2[Subgoal: Prove P3]
    SUB1 --> RED2[Reduce to P1 and P2]
    RED2 --> LEAF1[Known: P1]
    RED2 --> LEAF2[Known: P2]
    SUB2 --> LEAF3[Known: P3 in KB]
    LEAF1 --> SUCCESS[All subgoals proven]
    LEAF2 --> SUCCESS
    LEAF3 --> SUCCESS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Modus Ponens. Demonstrate its application with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Modus Ponens** (Latin for "the way that affirms") is a fundamental rule of inference in propositional logic. The rule states that if a conditional statement $P \rightarrow Q$ is true, and the antecedent $P$ is known to be true, then the consequent $Q$ must also be true.

The formal logical form is:

$$\frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad P}{\therefore Q}$$

**Example:**
- Premise 1: If it is raining $(P)$, then the ground is wet $(Q)$.  $\quad (P \rightarrow Q)$
- Premise 2: It is raining.  $\quad (P)$
- Conclusion: Therefore, the ground is wet.  $\quad (Q)$

This rule is **sound** — it never derives a false conclusion from true premises — and forms the operational basis of forward-chaining inference engines. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Resolution? Why is it called a complete inference procedure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Resolution** is an inference rule that operates on clauses in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF). Given two clauses containing complementary literals (a literal and its negation), resolution produces a new clause (the *resolvent*) by eliminating those complementary literals and combining the rest. The general form is:

$$\frac{P \vee Q, \quad \neg P \vee R}{Q \vee R}$$

The complementary pair $P$ and $\neg P$ is cancelled, yielding the resolvent $Q \vee R$.

**Why is it complete?**
Resolution is **refutation-complete** for propositional logic. This means: if a knowledge base KB entails some sentence $\alpha$ (i.e., $KB \models \alpha$), then applying resolution repeatedly to $KB \cup \{\neg \alpha\}$ will *eventually* derive the empty clause $\square$ (a contradiction). This guarantees that resolution will either prove the goal or determine that it cannot be proven — it never "misses" a valid conclusion. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Convert the following sentence into Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):
$$(P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow (\neg R \wedge S)$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Resolution Refutation**, prove that $S$ is entailed by the following Knowledge Base:
- $P_1$: $P \rightarrow Q$
- $P_2$: $R \rightarrow \neg Q$
- $P_3$: $R$
- $P_4$: $\neg S \rightarrow \neg P$ (Hint: derive a contradiction with the negation of $S$)

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) CNF Conversion — Step-by-Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Given: } (P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow (\neg R \wedge S) \\
& \\
& \text{Step 1: Eliminate outer } \rightarrow \\
& \equiv \neg(P \rightarrow Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge S) \\
& \\
& \text{Step 2: Eliminate inner } \rightarrow \\
& \equiv \neg(\neg P \vee Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge S) \\
& \\
& \text{Step 3: Apply De Morgan's to } \neg(\neg P \vee Q) \\
& \equiv (P \wedge \neg Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge S) \\
& \\
& \text{Step 4: Distribute } \wedge \text{ over } \vee \\
& \equiv (P \vee \neg R) \wedge (P \vee S) \wedge (\neg Q \vee \neg R) \wedge (\neg Q \vee S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final CNF (Set of Clauses):**
$$\{ \{P, \neg R\},\; \{P, S\},\; \{\neg Q, \neg R\},\; \{\neg Q, S\} \}$$

**(7 marks)** — 1 mark for Step 1, 1 for Step 2, 1 for Step 3, 2 for distribution, 2 for final form.

---

**(b) Resolution Refutation Proof:**

**Knowledge Base (converted to CNF):**
- $C_1$: $\neg P \vee Q$  (from $P \rightarrow Q$)
- $C_2$: $\neg R \vee \neg Q$  (from $R \rightarrow \neg Q$)
- $C_3$: $R$  (given)
- $C_4$: $P \vee S$  (from $\neg S \rightarrow \neg P$, equivalently $S \vee P$)
- $C_5$: $\neg S$  (negation of goal)

**Refutation Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Step 1: } R \quad (C_3) \\
& \text{Step 2: } \neg R \vee \neg Q \quad (C_2) \\
& \text{Step 3: } \neg Q \quad \text{(Resolution: Step 1, Step 2)} \\
& \text{Step 4: } \neg P \vee Q \quad (C_1) \\
& \text{Step 5: } \neg P \quad \text{(Resolution: Step 3, Step 4)} \\
& \text{Step 6: } P \vee S \quad (C_4) \\
& \texttext{Step 7: } S \quad \text{(Resolution: Step 5, Step 6)} \\
& \text{Step 8: } \neg S \quad (C_5) \\
& \text{Step 9: } \square \quad \text{(Resolution: Step 7, Step 8)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since a contradiction is derived, our assumption $\neg S$ is false. Therefore, $S$ is entailed by the KB. **(7 marks)** — 1 mark per major resolvent step, 2 marks for correctly identifying the refutation structure.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Forward Chaining** and **Backward Chaining** algorithms. Compare them in terms of direction, application domain, and termination conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Horn Clause Knowledge Base:
- $A \wedge B \rightarrow C$
- $C \wedge D \rightarrow E$
- $A \wedge E \rightarrow F$
- $B$
- $D$

Using **Forward Chaining**, derive all possible facts. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Forward vs. Backward Chaining (7 marks):**

**Forward Chaining (Data-Driven):**
- **Direction:** Starts from known facts in the KB and applies Modus Ponens repeatedly.
- **Mechanism:** Iteratively checks which rules have all antecedents satisfied; if so, fires the rule and adds the consequent to the known facts.
- **Termination:** Stops when no new facts are generated OR the goal is reached.
- **Best for:** Monitoring, planning, real-time control systems.
- **Complexity:** $O(n)$ for Horn Clauses.

**Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven):**
- **Direction:** Starts from the query/goal and works backward to find supporting facts.
- **Mechanism:** Reduces a goal to sub-goals by finding rules whose consequent matches; recursively proves each antecedent.
- **Termination:** Stops when all sub-goals reduce to known facts OR an unsolvable branch is hit.
- **Best for:** Query-answering, diagnostic systems (e.g., Prolog, MYCIN).
- **Complexity:** Linear in the size of the KB in the best case.

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | Known facts | Query / Goal |
| Strategy | Data-driven | Goal-driven |
| Inference Rule | Modus Ponens | Modus Tollens (in reverse) |
| Output | All derivable facts | Proof tree for the query |
| Use Case | Monitoring, planning | Diagnosis, Q&A systems |

**(7 marks)** — 2 marks for forward chaining explanation, 2 for backward, 3 for the comparison table.

---

**(b) Forward Chaining Derivation (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1:**
- Known facts: $\{B, D\}$
- Rule: $A \wedge B \rightarrow C$ — antecedent $B$ is satisfied, but $A$ is missing. **Cannot fire.**
- Rule: $C \wedge D \rightarrow E$ — $C$ missing. **Cannot fire.**

**Iteration 2:** No new facts can be added. Forward chaining **halts**.

**Final Knowledge Base after Forward Chaining:** $\{B, D\}$

**Conclusion:** Without $A$ and $C$ being asserted, the only derivable facts are $B$ and $D$. The goal $F$ **cannot be derived** because the rule $A \wedge B \rightarrow C$ has an unprovable antecedent $A$. **(7 marks)** — 1 mark per iteration step, 2 marks for the conclusion and explanation of the halting condition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Skipping the Negation Step:** In refutation proofs, students often forget to add $\neg \alpha$ (negation of the goal) to the KB. This *immediately* loses 2–3 marks because refutation is meaningless without the negated query.
> 2. **Incorrect CNF Conversion:** Failing to distribute $\wedge$ over $\vee$ properly is the #1 error. Always double-check using the identity $A \vee (B \wedge C) \equiv (A \vee B) \wedge (A \vee C)$.
> 3. **Confusing Soundness with Completeness:** Soundness = "derives only TRUE things." Completeness = "derives ALL TRUE things." Examiners *love* testing this distinction. Confusing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Misidentifying Forward vs. Backward Chaining:** Writing forward chaining when the question asks for backward (or vice versa) is a *fatal* structural error. Always re-read the question stem.
> 5. **Forgetting to Show the Rule Name:** KTU examiners award partial credit for *naming* the inference rule used at each step (Modus Ponens, Resolution, etc.). Don't just write the resolvent — label it!

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Core Rules to Memorize:** Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism, Disjunctive Syllogism, Resolution.
>
> ✅ **Resolution is the most general and complete rule** — it subsumes almost all other rules.
>
> ✅ **Refutation = Negate the goal + derive the empty clause (□).** This is KTU's most-tested technique.
>
> ✅ **CNF Conversion Order:** (1) Eliminate $\rightarrow$, (2) Move $\neg$ inward, (3) Standardize, (4) Skolemize, (5) Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$, (6) Drop $\wedge$.
>
> ✅ **Horn Clauses = at most ONE positive literal.** This restriction enables linear-time forward/backward chaining.
>
> ✅ **Forward Chaining = Data-Driven = Modus Ponens.** Best for deriving ALL facts.
>
> ✅ **Backward Chaining = Goal-Driven = Inverse Modus Ponens.** Best for proving ONE specific goal.
>
> ✅ **Soundness ≠ Completeness.** A system can be sound but incomplete, or complete but unsound. Resolution is *both* sound and (refutation-)complete.
>
> ✅ **The "Empty Clause" □** is the universal symbol for contradiction. Seeing it = "Goal proved!"
>
> ✅ **Master the CNF identities** (De Morgan's, Distributivity, Implication elimination) — they appear in *every* resolution problem.
>
> ✅ **Practice writing proofs in the standard KTU tabular format** with explicit rule names and step numbers. Clarity of presentation can fetch 1–2 grace marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** First Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**First Order Logic (FOL)**, also called **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)**, **Predicate Calculus**, or **Quantificational Logic**, is a formal symbolic reasoning system used in Artificial Intelligence to represent knowledge about objects and their relationships in a mathematically precise manner. It extends **Propositional Logic (PL)** by introducing three powerful linguistic constructs:

1. **Objects** (also called terms or individuals)
2. **Relations** (predicates over objects)
3. **Variables and Quantifiers** (to express generality and existence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"First-Order Logic is a knowledge representation language that is sufficiently expressive to represent a wide variety of facts and is powerful enough to support a general-purpose inference mechanism. It allows statements to be made about entire collections of objects rather than enumerating them individually."*

In First-Order Logic, the *universe of discourse* (the set of all objects we are talking about) is quantified, but the logic itself is restricted — quantifiers range over **individual objects only**, never over **predicates** or **functions**. This is precisely what makes it "first-order." If we allowed quantification over predicates themselves, we would step into **Higher-Order Logic (HOL)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree Registrar

Imagine you are a **registry officer in a government office** responsible for maintaining a family's legal records.

- **Propositional Logic** would be like stamping a single page that says *"Ram is a father"* — fixed, indivisible, true or false.
- **First-Order Logic** would be like drafting a **legal template** that says: *"For every person **X**, there exists a person **Y** such that **Y** is the father of **X**."* — This one template handles **millions of family members** without rewriting the rule.

The **template (rule)** is the FOL sentence. The **people (X, Y)** are the variables over which the rule applies. The **"for every"** and **"there exists"** are quantifiers. This is the leap: from stating *individual facts* to stating *general laws about objects*.

## 1.3 Why First-Order Logic Matters in AI

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Expressive Power** | Can represent *all* of mathematics (Peano Arithmetic, ZFC Set Theory) |
| **Sound Inference** | Supports **sound** (truth-preserving) inference rules |
| **Complete Inference** | Supports **complete** inference rules for FOL fragments |
| **Foundational for KBs** | Basis of every Knowledge Base in expert systems, NLP, and automated theorem proving |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation (Exam Favorite!):**
> FOL is **semi-decidable** — if a sentence is *entailed* by the KB, the algorithm will eventually find a proof. However, if it is **not** entailed, the algorithm may run **forever** without halting. This is a famous KTU viva and 3-mark question.

## 1.4 Building Blocks of FOL

$$\text{FOL Vocabulary} = \{\underbrace{\text{Constants}}_{\text{specific objects}}, \underbrace{\text{Variables}}_{\text{placeholders}}, \underbrace{\text{Predicates}}_{\text{relations}}, \underbrace{\text{Functions}}_{\text{mappings}, \underbrace{\text{Connectives}}_{\land,\lor,\neg,\Rightarrow,\Leftrightarrow}, \underbrace{\text{Quantifiers}}_{\forall, \exists}\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Constants** name specific objects (e.g., `Ravi`, `India`).
> **Variables** stand for unnamed objects (e.g., `x`, `y`).
> **Predicate symbols** denote relations (e.g., `Father(x, y)`).
> **Function symbols** map objects to objects (e.g., `LeftLegOf(x)`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Syntax of First-Order Logic

The syntax of FOL is built recursively. Every well-formed expression is constructed from a fixed alphabet of symbols according to a **grammar**.

### 2.1.1 Logical Symbols (Fixed across all domains)

$$\Sigma_{logical} = \{ (,\space),\space ,\space\neg,\space\wedge,\space\vee,\space\Rightarrow,\space\Leftrightarrow,\space\forall,\space\exists,\space =\}$$

The **equality symbol** = is treated as a special logical predicate (identity relation).

### 2.1.2 Non-Logical Symbols (Domain-Dependent)

$$\Sigma_{non\text{-}logical} = \underbrace{P_1, P_2, \ldots}_{\text{predicate symbols}} \cup \underbrace{f_1, f_2, \ldots}_{\text{function symbols}} \cup \underbrace{c_1, c_2, \ldots}_{\text{constant symbols}}$$

### 2.1.3 Grammatical Formation Rules (Backus-Naur Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{Sentence} \rangle &::= \langle \text{AtomicSentence} \rangle \;\mid\; \neg \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \;\mid\; \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \oplus \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \;\mid\; Q\langle \text{Variables} \rangle \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \\
\langle \text{AtomicSentence} \rangle &::= \text{Predicate}(\text{Terms}) \;\mid\; \text{Term} = \text{Term} \\
\langle \text{Term} \rangle &::= \text{Function}(\text{Terms}) \;\mid\; \text{Constant} \;\mid\; \text{Variable} \\
\text{Where } \oplus &\in \{ \wedge, \vee, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow \} \text{ and } Q \in \{ \forall, \exists \}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 The Semantics of FOL

Semantics assigns **meaning** (truth value) to sentences by mapping the non-logical symbols onto a real-world mathematical structure called a **Model** or **Interpretation**.

### 2.2.1 Components of a Model $\mathcal{M}$

$$
\mathcal{M} = \langle D, \mathcal{I} \rangle
$$

where:
- $D$ = **Domain of Discourse** (a non-empty set of objects)
- $\mathcal{I}$ = **Interpretation Function** mapping:
  - each constant $c \mapsto$ an element $\mathcal{I}(c) \in D$
  - each $n$-ary function $f \mapsto \mathcal{I}(f) : D^n \to D$
  - each $n$-ary predicate $P \mapsto \mathcal{I}(P) \subseteq D^n$ (a relation)

### 2.2.2 Variable Assignment

A **variable assignment** $\mu$ is a function $\mu : \text{Variables} \to D$. The interpretation of a term $t$ under $\mu$ is written $\mathcal{M}[\mu \vert t]$ and defined recursively.

## 2.3 Quantifiers — The Heart of FOL

### 2.3.1 Universal Quantifier ($\forall$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M} &\models \forall x \, \phi(x) \quad \iff \quad \text{for every } d \in D, \; \mathcal{M} \models \phi[x/d]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain meaning:** "For ALL objects in the domain, the statement holds."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Statements are Implicit Conjunctions:**
> $\forall x \, P(x)$ is logically equivalent to $P(o_1) \land P(o_2) \land \ldots \land P(o_n)$ over a finite domain $\{o_1, \ldots, o_n\}$.
> **To falsify it, find just ONE counter-example.**

### 2.3.2 Existential Quantifier ($\exists$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M} &\models \exists x \, \phi(x) \quad \iff \quad \text{there exists } d \in D \text{ such that } \mathcal{M} \models \phi[x/d]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain meaning:** "There EXISTS at least one object in the domain for which the statement holds."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existential Statements are Implicit Disjunctions:**
> $\exists x \, P(x)$ is logically equivalent to $P(o_1) \lor P(o_2) \lor \ldots \lor P(o_n)$.
> **To satisfy it, find at least ONE witness.**

### 2.3.3 The Famous Quantifier Duality

$$
\neg \forall x \, \phi(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg\phi(x)
$$

$$
\neg \exists x \, \phi(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg\phi(x)
$$

This **De Morgan-like** duality is the most-tested identity in KTU question papers on FOL.

## 2.4 Free vs. Bound Variables

A variable occurrence is **bound** if it lies within the scope of a quantifier over that variable; otherwise it is **free**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scope of } \forall x \, \phi(x) &: \text{ is the sub-formula } \phi(x) \\
\text{Bound variable example} &: \forall x \; \big[ \text{Loves}(x, y) \big] \;\; (x \text{ is bound}) \\
\text{Free variable example} &: \forall x \; \big[ \text{Loves}(x, y) \big] \;\; (y \text{ is free})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A sentence with any free variable is NOT a well-formed closed sentence** of FOL. Free variables mean the statement has no determinate truth value. Always rename bound variables to avoid *variable capture* during inference.

## 2.5 The Equality Predicate

The symbol $=$ in FOL is interpreted as the **identity relation**:

$$
\mathcal{M} \models (t_1 = t_2) \iff \mathcal{M}[\mu \vert t_1] = \mathcal{M}[\mu \vert t_2]
$$

It is reflexive ($x = x$), symmetric, and transitive. In knowledge bases, equality enables **unique-names assumption (UNA)** reasoning.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Symbolic Form | Plain-English Equivalent | Truth Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Universal Quantifier | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | For all x, P(x) | False iff one counter-example exists |
| 2 | Existential Quantifier | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | There exists x such that P(x) | True iff at least one witness exists |
| 3 | Negation of $\forall$ | $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | Not all x satisfy P | Some x violates P |
| 4 | Negation of $\exists$ | $\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | No x satisfies P | Every x violates P |
| 5 | Quantifier Swap | $\forall x \, \forall y \, P(x,y) \equiv \forall y \, \forall x \, P(x,y)$ | Order does not matter for same quantifier | Identical truth conditions |
| 6 | Quantifier Swap | $\exists x \, \exists y \, P(x,y) \equiv \exists y \, \exists x \, P(x,y)$ | Order does not matter for same quantifier | Identical truth conditions |
| 7 | Mixed Quantifier | $\forall x \, \exists y \, P(x,y) \not\equiv \exists y \, \forall x \, P(x,y)$ | Order MATTERS for mixed quantifiers | Different meanings |
| 8 | Standard Interpretation | $\mathcal{M} \models \phi$ | Model M satisfies sentence $\phi$ | $\phi$ is true under M |
| 9 | Entailment | $KB \models \phi$ | Every model of KB is a model of $\phi$ | $\phi$ follows logically from KB |
| 10 | Validity | $\models \phi$ | $\phi$ is true in every model | Tautology of FOL |

> [!TIP]
> **The Mixed Quantifier Swap (row 7) is the single most common KTU mistake.** $\forall x \, \exists y \, \text{Loves}(x,y)$ says *"everyone loves SOMEONE"* (possibly different person for each). $\exists y \, \forall x \, \text{Loves}(x,y)$ says *"someone is loved by EVERYONE"* (a celebrity). These are NOT equivalent!

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translating English to FOL

**English Statement:**
> *"Every student in the AI class owns a laptop that runs a programming language used in Kerala universities."*

### Step-by-Step Translation

Let us first define our vocabulary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Constants} &: \{\text{aiClass}\} \\
\text{Predicates} &: \{\text{Student}(x), \text{Owns}(x,y), \text{Laptop}(y), \text{Runs}(y,z), \text{Lang}(z)\} \\
\text{Goal} &: \text{Translate the natural language claim into a formal FOL sentence.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step A — Break Down the English Sentence

The sentence contains nested structure. We read it from the **outermost quantifier inward**:

> "**Every** student" → introduces $\forall x$
> "in the AI class" → conjunction $\text{Student}(x) \land \text{In}(x, \text{aiClass})$
> "**owns**" → predicate $\text{Owns}(x, y)$
> "**a laptop**" → introduces a new variable $y$ with $\exists y$ or nested condition
> "that **runs** a language" → introduces $\exists z$ and $\text{Runs}(y,z)$

### Step B — Compose the Formula (Nested Reading)

The outermost quantifier is $\forall$ (every). Inside, we claim existence of a laptop and a language:

$$
\forall x \, \big[ \text{Student}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, \exists z \, (\text{Laptop}(y) \land \text{Runs}(y,z) \land \text{Lang}(z) \land \text{Owns}(x,y)) \big]
$$

### Step C — Verify Well-Formedness

- $x$ is bound by the outer $\forall$.
- $y$ and $z$ are bound by the inner $\exists$.
- No free variables remain. ✓
- All connectives are standard. ✓
- Therefore, it is a **closed sentence** of FOL. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always identify quantifier scope **first**, then ensure every variable is bound. A common KTU error is leaving a variable unbound after translation — this automatically costs 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Quantifier Negation Deduction

**Problem:** Negate the following FOL sentence in standard form (with negation only in front of atomic formulas):

$$
\phi = \forall x \, \big[ \text{Bird}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Friend}(x, y) \land \text{Loves}(x, y)) \big]
$$

### Step 1 — Apply Outer Negation

$$
\neg \phi \;\equiv\; \neg \forall x \, \big[ \text{Bird}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Friend}(x, y) \land \text{Loves}(x, y)) \big]
$$

### Step 2 — Use $\neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg$

$$
\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg \big[ \text{Bird}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Friend}(x, y) \land \text{Loves}(x, y)) \big]
$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate Implication via $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inner:} \quad & \text{Bird}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{...}) \;\equiv\; \neg \text{Bird}(x) \lor \exists y \, (\text{...}) \\
\text{Negation:} \quad & \neg \big[\neg \text{Bird}(x) \lor \exists y \, (\text{...})\big] \;\equiv\; \text{Bird}(x) \land \neg \exists y \, (\text{...})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply $\neg \exists \equiv \forall \neg$ to the Inner Existential

$$
\neg \exists y \, (\text{Friend}(x, y) \land \text{Loves}(x, y)) \;\equiv\; \forall y \, \neg (\text{Friend}(x, y) \land \text{Loves}(x, y))
$$

### Step 5 — De Morgan Inside

$$
\forall y \, (\neg \text{Friend}(x, y) \lor \neg \text{Loves}(x, y))
$$

### Step 6 — Final Assembled Formula

$$
\boxed{\neg \phi \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \big[ \text{Bird}(x) \land \forall y \, (\neg \text{Friend}(x, y) \lor \neg \text{Loves}(x, y)) \big]}
$$

### Step 7 — Plain English Interpretation

> *"There exists a bird such that for every entity, that entity is either NOT a friend of the bird OR does NOT love the bird."*

Equivalently: *"There exists a bird with NO friends that love it."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving Quantifier Order Matters (Mixed Quantifier Asymmetry)

We want to show:

$$
\forall x \, \exists y \, \text{Loves}(x, y) \;\not\equiv\; \exists y \, \forall x \, \text{Loves}(x, y)
$$

### Proof of Non-Equivalence (Counter-Model Construction)

**LHS:** $\forall x \, \exists y \, \text{Loves}(x, y)$ — *Everyone loves someone (possibly different).*

**RHS:** $\exists y \, \forall x \, \text{Loves}(x, y)$ — *There is someone loved by everyone.*

We construct a **counter-model** where LHS is true but RHS is false.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain } D &= \{ \text{Alice}, \text{Bob} \} \\
\text{Loves}^{\mathcal{I}} &= \{ (\text{Alice}, \text{Alice}), (\text{Bob}, \text{Alice}) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check LHS (everyone loves someone):**
- Alice loves Alice. ✓
- Bob loves Alice. ✓
- Both satisfied → LHS is TRUE.

**Check RHS (someone loved by everyone):**
- Who is loved by everyone? Only Alice is loved by anyone, but is Alice loved by Alice? Yes. Is Alice loved by Bob? Yes.
- So Alice is loved by both → RHS is TRUE in this model too. Need to break this!

### Better Counter-Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain } D &= \{ \text{Alice}, \text{Bob}, \text{Carol} \} \\
\text{Loves}^{\mathcal{I}} &= \{ (\text{Alice}, \text{Bob}), (\text{Bob}, \text{Carol}), (\text{Carol}, \text{Alice}) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check LHS:**
- Alice loves Bob. ✓
- Bob loves Carol. ✓
- Carol loves Alice. ✓
- LHS is TRUE.

**Check RHS:**
- Is Alice loved by everyone? No, Bob loves Carol. ✗
- Is Bob loved by everyone? No, Carol loves Alice. ✗
- Is Carol loved by everyone? No, Alice loves Bob. ✗
- **No single person is universally loved → RHS is FALSE.**

Therefore LHS $\not\equiv$ RHS. **The order of mixed quantifiers changes meaning.** ∎

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Unification (Foundational Algorithm)

**Unification** finds a substitution $\theta$ that makes two atomic FOL expressions identical. It is the engine of resolution-based inference.

**Given expressions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x) \\
\phi_2 &= \text{Knows}(y, \text{Mary})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal:** Find the **most general unifier (MGU)** $\theta$.

### Algorithm Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Difference set} = \{ \text{John}, y \} \\
                       & \text{Since John is a constant and } y \text{ is a variable, set } y \leftarrow \text{John}. \\
                       & \theta_1 = \{ y / \text{John} \} \\
\text{Apply } \theta_1: \quad & \phi_1 \theta_1 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x) \\
                               & \phi_2 \theta_1 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mary}) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Difference set} = \{ x, \text{Mary} \} \\
                       & \text{Since } x \text{ is a variable, set } x \leftarrow \text{Mary}. \\
                       & \theta_2 = \{ x / \text{Mary} \} \\
\text{Apply } \theta_2: \quad & \phi_1 \theta_1 \theta_2 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mary}) \\
                               & \phi_2 \theta_1 \theta_2 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mary}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Both expressions now IDENTICAL → Unification succeeded.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final MGU:**

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{MGU}} = \{ y / \text{John}, \; x / \text{Mary} \}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Inference Rules for FOL

| Rule | Premise Form | Conclusion | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Universal Elimination (UI)** | $\forall x \, \phi(x)$ | $\phi(g)$ for any ground term $g$ | Instantiate with a constant or ground term |
| **Existential Elimination (EI)** | $\exists x \, \phi(x)$ | $\phi(c)$ where $c$ is a *Skolem constant* | The new constant must NOT appear elsewhere |
| **Existential Introduction** | $\phi(c)$ for some constant $c$ | $\exists x \, \phi(x)$ | Promote constant to variable |
| **Universal Introduction** | $\phi(c)$ for arbitrary $c$ | $\forall x \, \phi(x)$ | Only valid if $c$ is "arbitrary" (not named) |
| **Generalized Modus Ponens** | $p_1', p_2', \ldots, p_n', \; (p_1 \land p_2 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)$ | $q\theta$ | Where $\theta$ unifies $p_i$ with $p_i'$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Introduction is the most abused rule.** You CANNOT generalize from a single named constant. Ravi is a man does NOT imply $\forall x \, \text{Man}(x)$. This is the classical fallacy of illicit conversion.

---

## 3.6 KTU Conversion-to-CNF Walkthrough

**Sentence:** $\forall x \, [\text{Creature}(x) \Rightarrow (\exists y \, \text{ParentOf}(y, x) \land \neg \text{Orphan}(x))]$

### Step 1 — Eliminate Implication
Replace $P \Rightarrow Q$ with $\neg P \lor Q$:
$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Creature}(x) \lor (\exists y \, \text{ParentOf}(y, x) \land \neg \text{Orphan}(x))]
$$

### Step 2 — Move Negation Inward (Standardize)
Already atomic — no change.

### Step 3 — Standardize Variables
Variables already distinct.

### Step 4 — Skolemize (Remove $\exists$)
Replace $y$ with a **Skolem function** of $x$: $f(x)$.
$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Creature}(x) \lor (\text{ParentOf}(f(x), x) \land \neg \text{Orphan}(x))]
$$

### Step 5 — Drop Universal Quantifiers
Assume all remaining variables are universally quantified.

### Step 6 — Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$
$$
[\neg \text{Creature}(x) \lor \text{ParentOf}(f(x), x)] \land [\neg \text{Creature}(x) \lor \neg \text{Orphan}(x)]
$$

### Step 7 — Final CNF Clause Set
$$
\mathcal{C} = \big\{ \{\neg \text{Creature}(x), \; \text{ParentOf}(f(x), x)\}, \; \{\neg \text{Creature}(x), \; \neg \text{Orphan}(x)\} \big\}
$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FOL Syntax Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram visualizes how an FOL sentence is constructed from primitives, just like a compiler builds up expressions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SENTENCE] --> B[Atomic Sentence]
    A --> C[Quantified Sentence]
    A --> D[Compound Sentence]
    B --> B1[Predicate Terms]
    B --> B2[Equality Term1 = Term2]
    C --> C1[Universal forall x phi]
    C --> C2[Existential exists x phi]
    D --> D1[Negation not phi]
    D --> D2[Conjunction phi AND psi]
    D --> D3[Disjunction phi OR psi]
    D --> D4[Implication phi implies psi]
    B1 --> T[TERM]
    T --> T1[Constant Symbol]
    T --> T2[Variable]
    T --> T3[Function Symbol Terms]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style T fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Knowledge Representation Pipeline in FOL

This shows how raw English knowledge flows through formalization into an executable inference process.

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Natural Language Statement] --> P1[Identify Domain Objects]
    P1 --> P2[Identify Predicates and Functions]
    P2 --> P3[Choose Quantifiers]
    P3 --> P4[Compose FOL Sentence]
    P4 --> P5[Validate WFF]
    P5 --> V{Is Formula Closed?}
    V -->|No| P6[Bind Free Variables]
    P6 --> P5
    V -->|Yes| P7[Convert to CNF]
    P7 --> P8[Apply Resolution]
    P8 --> P9[Derive New Sentences]
    style N1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style P7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style P9 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Quantifier Scope & Binding Diagram

This illustrates how $\forall$ and $\exists$ bind variables within their syntactic scope, leaving others free.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[forall x] --> B[Inner Formula phi x]
    B --> C[exists y]
    C --> D[psi x y]
    D --> E[Predicates with x and y]
    F[Variable z outside quantifier] --> G[Free Variable z]
    style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Unification Algorithm Flow

The MGU (Most General Unifier) is computed iteratively by resolving differences between two terms.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Two Atomic Sentences] --> B[Compute Difference Set DISAGREE]
    B --> C{Is DISAGREE empty?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Return accumulated substitution theta]
    C -->|No| E{Is first element a variable?}
    E -->|No| F[FAIL: Occurs Check or Type Mismatch]
    E -->|Yes| G[Apply Occurs Check]
    G --> H{Does variable occur in other term?}
    H -->|Yes| F
    H -->|No| I[Add variable / term to substitution]
    I --> J[Apply substitution to both sentences]
    J --> B
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 FOL vs. Propositional Logic — Capability Comparison

| Capability | Propositional Logic | First-Order Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Represent objects | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Represent relations | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Quantify over individuals | ❌ No | ✅ Yes ($\forall, \exists$) |
| Quantify over predicates | ❌ No | ❌ No (that is HOL) |
| Express "All men are mortal" | Painful (one clause per man) | $\forall x \, (\text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$ |
| Truth tables feasible | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (infinite domains) |
| Decidability | ✅ Decidable | ⚠️ Semi-decidable |
