# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by First Order Logic? Differentiate it from Propositional Logic.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

First-Order Logic (FOL) is a formal knowledge representation language that uses **variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers** to express facts about objects and their relationships in a domain of discourse. Unlike Propositional Logic, which treats each atomic statement as an indivisible unit (true/false), FOL decomposes statements into **objects**, **properties**, and **relations**, enabling generalization.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Propositional Logic | First-Order Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic units | Propositional symbols ($P, Q$) | Predicates applied to terms ($\text{Loves}(x, y)$) |
| Variables & Quantifiers | Absent | $\forall, \exists$ present |
| Expressive power | Limited | Turing-complete representation |
| Example | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $\forall x \, (\text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write only the difference as *"FOL has variables."* You must mention **at least three concrete distinctions** (variables, quantifiers, predicates/functions) to secure full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the two quantifiers used in First-Order Logic with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**1. Universal Quantifier ($\forall$):** It is read as *"for all"* or *"for every."* It asserts that a predicate holds true for **every** element in the domain of discourse.

$$\text{Example:} \quad \forall x \, \text{Student}(x) \Rightarrow \text{AppearsForExam}(x)$$
*"Every student appears for the exam."*

**2. Existential Quantifier ($\exists$):** It is read as *"there exists"* or *"for some."* It asserts that a predicate holds true for **at least one** element in the domain.

$$\text{Example:} \quad \exists x \, \text{Student}(x) \land \text{TopScorer}(x)$$
*"There exists a student who is a top scorer."*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of typically two choices provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the syntax and semantics of First-Order Logic with suitable examples. Discuss the components of a model in FOL. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Syntax:** The syntax of FOL defines the grammatical structure of well-formed formulas (WFF). It consists of:

- **Logical symbols:** parentheses, connectives ($\neg, \land, \lor, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$), quantifiers ($\forall, \exists$), and equality ($=$).
- **Non-logical symbols:** constants (e.g., `Ravi`), variables (e.g., $x$), function symbols (e.g., `fatherOf(x)`), and predicate symbols (e.g., `Student(x)`).

**Formation rules:**
- A **term** is a constant, variable, or function applied to terms.
- An **atomic sentence** is a predicate applied to terms, or two terms joined by $=$.
- A **sentence** is built from atomic sentences using connectives and quantifiers.

$$\text{Example:} \quad \forall x \, [\text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, \text{Parent}(y, x)]$$
*"Every man has at least one parent."*

**(ii) Semantics:** Semantics assigns **truth values** to FOL sentences via an interpretation.

- A **model** $\mathcal{M} = \langle D, \mathcal{I} \rangle$ consists of:
  - $D$: the **domain of discourse** (a non-empty set).
  - $\mathcal{I}$: the **interpretation function** mapping constants to elements of $D$, functions to mappings $D^n \to D$, and predicates to relations $D^n$.

- A sentence is **true in $\mathcal{M}$** (written $\mathcal{M} \models \phi$) if the interpretation of its symbols yields a true statement.

**(iii) Quantifier Semantics:**

$$\mathcal{M} \models \forall x \, \phi(x) \iff \forall d \in D : \mathcal{M} \models \phi[x/d]$$
$$\mathcal{M} \models \exists x \, \phi(x) \iff \exists d \in D : \mathcal{M} \models \phi[x/d]$$

**(b)** Translate the following English statements into FOL: **(7 Marks)**

1. *"All students who study AI will pass the exam."*
2. *"Some birds cannot fly."*
3. *"Every person has exactly one biological mother."*
4. *"There is a faculty member who teaches every AI student."*

**Model Solution:**

**Define vocabulary first:**
- Predicates: $\text{Student}(x), \text{Studies}(x, y), \text{PassesExam}(x), \text{Bird}(x), \text{Flies}(x), \text{Person}(x), \text{BiologicalMother}(x, y), \text{Faculty}(x), \text{Teaches}(x, y), \text{AIStudent}(x)$.
- Constants: `ai`.
- Functions: `motherOf(x)` (returns the unique biological mother of $x$).

**Translations:**

**(1)** $\forall x \, [\text{Student}(x) \land \text{Studies}(x, \text{ai}) \Rightarrow \text{PassesExam}(x)]$

**Plain meaning:** For every $x$, if $x$ is a student and $x$ studies AI, then $x$ passes the exam.

**(2)** $\exists x \, [\text{Bird}(x) \land \neg \text{Flies}(x)]$

**Plain meaning:** There exists an $x$ such that $x$ is a bird and $x$ does not fly.

**(3)** $\forall x \, [\text{Person}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{BiologicalMother}(y, x) \land \forall z \, (\text{BiologicalMother}(z, x) \Rightarrow z = y))]$

**Plain meaning:** For every person $x$, there exists a unique $y$ who is $x$'s biological mother (the $\forall z$ clause enforces uniqueness).

**(4)** $\exists x \, [\text{Faculty}(x) \land \forall y \, (\text{AIStudent}(y) \Rightarrow \text{Teaches}(x, y))]$

**Plain meaning:** There is some faculty $x$ such that for every AI student $y$, $x$ teaches $y$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For (4), students often incorrectly swap the quantifiers: $\forall x \, \exists y$. The word *"a faculty member"* requires $\exists x$ OUTERMOST, not innermost. Marks lost: **2 out of 7.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is a well-formed formula (WFF) in FOL? Explain the difference between **free** and **bound** variables with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Well-Formed Formula (WFF):**
A WFF is a syntactically valid FOL sentence constructed recursively from atomic formulas using logical connectives and quantifiers. Rules:

1. If $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate and $t_1, \ldots, t_n$ are terms, then $P(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ is a WFF.
2. If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are terms, then $t_1 = t_2$ is a WFF.
3. If $\phi$ is a WFF, then $\neg \phi$ is a WFF.
4. If $\phi$ and $\psi$ are WFFs, then so are $\phi \land \psi, \; \phi \lor \psi, \; \phi \Rightarrow \psi, \; \phi \Leftrightarrow \psi$.
5. If $\phi$ is a WFF and $x$ is a variable, then $\forall x \, \phi$ and $\exists x \, \phi$ are WFFs.

**(ii) Free vs. Bound Variables:**

A variable occurrence is **bound** if it falls within the scope of a quantifier that names it; otherwise it is **free**.

**Example 1:** In $\forall x \, [\text{Loves}(x, y)]$, the occurrence of $x$ is bound, but $y$ is free.

**Example 2:** In $\exists y \, [\text{Parent}(y, x) \land \text{Doctor}(x)]$, both $y$ and $x$ are bound.

**Why It Matters:** A formula with free variables is not a *closed sentence* and cannot be assigned a determinate truth value. Valid FOL reasoning operates only on **sentences** (formulas with no free variables).

| Variable | Formula | Bound by | Free? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x$ | $\forall x \, P(x, y)$ | $\forall x$ | No |
| $y$ | $\forall x \, P(x, y)$ | None | Yes |
| $x$ | $\exists x \, \forall y \, Q(x, y)$ | $\exists x$ | No |
| $y$ | $\exists x \, \forall y \, Q(x, y)$ | $\forall y$ | No |

**(b)** Consider the following FOL knowledge base: **(7 Marks)**

- $\forall x \, [\text{Cat}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mammal}(x)]$
- $\forall x \, [\text{Mammal}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Animal}(x)]$
- $\text{Cat}(\text{Tom})$

**Prove using Generalized Modus Ponens that `Animal(Tom)` is entailed.**

**Model Solution:**

We apply **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** iteratively:

**Step 1:** Apply Universal Elimination to Rule 1 with substitution $\theta_1 = \{ x / \text{Tom} \}$:
$$
\text{Cat}(\text{Tom}) \Rightarrow \text{Mammal}(\text{Tom})
$$

**Step 2:** Apply GMP with the fact $\text{Cat}(\text{Tom})$:
$$
\frac{\text{Cat}(\text{Tom}), \quad \text{Cat}(\text{Tom}) \Rightarrow \text{Mammal}(\text{Tom})}{\therefore \text{Mammal}(\text{Tom})} \quad \theta_1 = \{ x / \text{Tom} \}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Universal Elimination to Rule 2 with substitution $\theta_2 = \{ x / \text{Tom} \}$:
$$
\text{Mammal}(\text{Tom}) \Rightarrow \text{Animal}(\text{Tom})
$$

**Step 4:** Apply GMP with the newly derived fact $\text{Mammal}(\text{Tom})$:
$$
\frac{\text{Mammal}(\text{Tom}), \quad \text{Mammal}(\text{Tom}) \Rightarrow \text{Animal}(\text{Tom})}{\therefore \text{Animal}(\text{Tom})} \quad \theta_2 = \{ x / \text{Tom} \}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{KB \models \text{Animal}(\text{Tom})}
$$

The KB logically entails that Tom is an animal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students skip showing the **substitution $\theta$ explicitly**. KTU valuation strictly requires the unifier/substitution to be written. Omitting $\theta$ costs **1 mark** per step.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Warnings for FOL

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Patterns Across All FOL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Variable capture during substitution** — Always rename bound variables when applying substitutions across nested quantifiers.
> 2. **Quantifier order confusion** — $\forall \exists$ vs. $\exists \forall$ have different meanings. Always read English carefully for "for every" vs. "there exists."
> 3. **Free variables left in answers** — A translation with free variables is **not a sentence** and gets zero marks.
> 4. **Implication vs. Conjunction misuse** — "All A are B" translates as $\forall x \, (A(x) \Rightarrow B(x))$, NOT $\forall x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$. The latter is far too strong.
> 5. **Skolemization omissions** — During CNF conversion, every $\exists$ must be replaced by a Skolem function of all enclosing $\forall$ variables.
> 6. **Skolem function vs. Skolem constant** — When $\exists$ is nested inside $\forall$, use a Skolem *function* of the outer variable, not a fresh constant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary for FOL:**

- **FOL** extends Propositional Logic with **variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers**.
- **Syntax** is defined recursively; **Semantics** is defined via models $\mathcal{M} = \langle D, \mathcal{I} \rangle$.
- **Universal quantifier** $\forall$ = "for all"; **Existential quantifier** $\exists$ = "there exists."
- **Universal statements** require all elements to satisfy; **existential statements** need only one witness.
- **Quantifier duality:** $\neg \forall x \, \phi \equiv \exists x \, \neg \phi$ and $\neg \exists x \, \phi \equiv \forall x \, \neg \phi$.
- **Free variables** make a formula invalid as a sentence; **bound variables** are within quantifier scope.
- **Mixed quantifier order matters:** $\forall \exists \not\equiv \exists \forall$.
- **English "All A are B"** translates to $\forall x \, (A(x) \Rightarrow B(x))$.
- **English "Some A are B"** translates to $\exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$.
- **"There exists a unique x"** requires nested $\exists \forall$ structure for uniqueness.
- **Unification** finds the Most General Unifier (MGU) using difference sets and occurs check.
- **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** is the primary sound inference rule for FOL.
- **CNF conversion** requires Skolemization to eliminate existential quantifiers.
- **FOL is semi-decidable** — entailed sentences are eventually proven; non-entailed ones may cause non-termination.
- **FOL cannot quantify over predicates** — that would be Higher-Order Logic.
- **Standard inference rules:** Universal Elimination, Existential Elimination (with Skolem constants), and Existential Introduction.
- **Equality predicate** = supports unique-names assumption and identity reasoning.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inference in First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inference in First Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference in First-Order Logic (FOL)** is the formal process of deriving new logical consequences (sentences) from a given Knowledge Base (KB) of FOL sentences using **sound rules of inference**. It is the computational mechanism that allows an intelligent agent to *reason* about the world, going beyond what is explicitly stored in its KB.

In KTU 2024 terminology, an **inference procedure** is said to be:
- **Sound** if it derives only sentences that are *logically entailed* by the KB (i.e., $KB \models \alpha \implies KB \vdash_i \alpha$).
- **Complete** if it can derive *every* sentence that is logically entailed (i.e., $KB \models \alpha \implies KB \vdash \alpha$).

The general entailment problem in FOL is **semi-decidable**: if $KB \models \alpha$, an algorithm will eventually prove it; if it does not, the algorithm may never halt.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Detective Analogy:** Imagine a detective has a case file (the **Knowledge Base**) full of witness statements and evidence (all written in formal FOL). **Inference** is the process the detective uses to logically connect the clues: "If the butler was in the library, AND the murder weapon was in the library, THEN the butler had opportunity." By repeatedly applying such rules, the detective *infers* a new conclusion ("The butler is the prime suspect") that was never explicitly written in any witness statement. The detective is performing **forward chaining**. If instead, the detective starts with a hypothesis ("Did the butler do it?") and works backward to check if the KB supports it, that is **backward chaining**.

### 1.3 Key Components Highlight

- **Knowledge Base (KB):** A set of sentences in FOL representing known facts.
- **Inference Engine:** The algorithm (Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining, or Resolution) that produces new sentences.
- **Substitution ($\theta$):** A mapping of variables to terms, the heart of **unification**.
- **Unification:** The process of finding a substitution that makes two FOL expressions identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three pillars covered under this topic in Module 3 are (1) **Unification & Lifting**, (2) **Forward Chaining**, and (3) **Backward Chaining**, with **Resolution** as the complete refutation system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reduction to Propositional Inference (The Need for Lifting)

Propositional inference is fast but cannot represent objects, properties, or relations. FOL inference must therefore *lift* propositional rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lifting** = taking a propositional inference rule and adapting it to handle variables and quantifiers, so a single rule application can replace many propositional ones.

### 2.2 Unification — The Engine of FOL Inference

**Unification** takes two logical sentences $p$ and $q$ and returns a substitution $\theta$ such that $p\theta = q\theta$.

**The Unify Algorithm (Robinson, 1965):**
1. If both are constants, they unify only if identical.
2. If one is a variable, bind it to the term (with **occurs check** to prevent infinite terms like $X = f(X)$).
3. If both are compound predicates, they must have the same predicate name, same arity, and the arguments must unify recursively.
4. Apply the resulting **Most General Unifier (MGU)** — the simplest substitution.

### 2.3 Quantifier Rules (The Lifting Rules)

| Propositional Rule | First-Order Version | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Modus Ponens | **Universal Instantiation (UI)** | $\forall v \, \alpha \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha\{v/g\}$ |
| And-Introduction | **Existential Instantiation (EI)** | $\exists v \, \alpha \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha\{v/k\}$ where $k$ is a Skolem constant |
| — | **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** | $\dfrac{p_1',\, p_2',\, \dots,\, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}$ where $\theta$ unifies all $p_i'$ with $p_i$ |

### 2.4 Storage & Retrieval (for Forward Chaining)

Definite Clauses are Horn-like FOL sentences of the form:
$$\forall v_1 \dots v_n \, (P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_m \Rightarrow Q)$$
This allows two key data structures:
- **Fact base** (atomic sentences in KB).
- **Rule base** (implications whose premises are conjunctions of positive literals).

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Rule Sheet

| Rule / Formula | Statement | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Universal Instantiation (UI)** | From $\forall v \, \alpha$, infer $\alpha\{v/\text{term}\}$ | Forward / Backward Chaining |
| **Existential Instantiation (EI)** | From $\exists v \, \alpha$, infer $\alpha\{v/k\}$ ($k$ new Skolem constant) | Skolemization |
| **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** | Combines UI + Modus Ponens with unification $\theta$ | All FOL reasoning |
| **Resolution Rule (FOL)** | $\dfrac{\ell_1 \lor \dots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_n}{(\ell_1 \lor \dots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \dots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \dots \lor m_n)\theta}$ where $\text{UNIFY}(\ell_i, \neg m_j) = \theta$ | Refutation proofs |
| **Skolem Function** | Replaces $\exists v$ with a function of surrounding $\forall$ | CNF conversion |
| **Occurs Check** | A variable $X$ cannot be bound to a term containing $X$ | Unification safety |
| **Most General Unifier (MGU)** | The simplest $\theta$ that unifies two expressions | Unification output |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Expert Systems / Medical Diagnosis (MYCIN):** Uses backward chaining over rule bases.
- **Semantic Web & OWL Reasoners:** Use Description Logic variants of forward chaining.
- **Automated Theorem Provers (Prover9, Vampire):** Use FOL Resolution.
- **Logic Programming (Prolog):** Backward chaining over Horn clauses.
- **Formal Verification of Hardware/Software:** SMT solvers use FOL inference under the hood.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** GMP is *sound* but *not complete* for full FOL. **Resolution** is the only FOL inference rule that is both *sound and refutation-complete* — which is why KTU places heavy emphasis on it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Forward Chaining with GMP

**Knowledge Base (KB):**
1. $\forall x \, (\text{King}(x) \land \text{Greedy}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(x))$
2. $\text{King}(\text{John})$
3. $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$
4. $\forall x \, (\text{Greedy}(x))$

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$.

**Step 1 — Identify the rule head and premises.**  
Rule 1 has head $\text{Evil}(x)$ and premises $\{\text{King}(x), \text{Greedy}(x)\}$.

**Step 2 — Find facts that unify with the premises.**  
- $\text{King}(\text{John})$ unifies with $\text{King}(x)$ using $\theta_1 = \{x / \text{John}\}$.  
- $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$ unifies with $\text{Greedy}(x)$ using $\theta_2 = \{x / \text{John}\}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the combined MGU.**  
$$\theta = \{x / \text{John}\}$$

**Step 4 — Apply GMP.**  
The combined substitution unifies all premises simultaneously; the head unifies with the goal via the same $\theta$, yielding:

$$\text{Evil}(\text{John})\theta = \text{Evil}(\text{John})$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**  
The goal is derived, so $KB \vdash \text{Evil}(\text{John})$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Resolution Refutation (Full FOL)

**Prove:** $KB \models \neg \text{Manager}(\text{Bob})$

**KB (in English):**
- Everyone who works hard is successful.  
  $\forall x \, (\text{WorkHard}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Successful}(x))$
- Successful people are not unhappy.  
  $\forall x \, (\text{Successful}(x) \Rightarrow \neg \text{Unhappy}(x))$
- Bob is a manager.  
  $\text{Manager}(\text{Bob})$
- Managers work hard.  
  $\forall x \, (\text{Manager}(x) \Rightarrow \text{WorkHard}(x))$
- Unhappy people are not managers.  
  $\forall x \, (\text{Unhappy}(x) \Rightarrow \neg \text{Manager}(x))$

**Step 1 — Convert to CNF.**  
Apply implication elimination, move negations inward (De Morgan), standardize variables, Skolemize, drop universal quantifiers, and combine into clauses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{C1: } \neg \text{WorkHard}(x) \lor \text{Successful}(x) \\
&\text{C2: } \neg \text{Successful}(x) \lor \neg \text{Unhappy}(x) \\
&\text{C3: } \text{Manager}(\text{Bob}) \\
&\text{C4: } \neg \text{Manager}(y) \lor \text{WorkHard}(y) \\
&\text{C5: } \neg \text{Unhappy}(z) \lor \neg \text{Manager}(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Negate the goal.**  
Negate $\neg \text{Manager}(\text{Bob})$ to get the refutation target: $\text{Manager}(\text{Bob})$.

**Step 3 — Resolve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{C6:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C3, C4) with } \theta_1 = \{y/\text{Bob}\} \\
&\quad\; = \text{WorkHard}(\text{Bob}) \\
&\text{C7:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C6, C1) with } \theta_2 = \{x/\text{Bob}\} \\
&\quad\; = \text{Successful}(\text{Bob}) \\
&\text{C8:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C7, C2) with } \theta_3 = \{x/\text{Bob}\} \\
&\quad\; = \neg \text{Unhappy}(\text{Bob}) \\
&\text{C9:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C8, C5) with } \theta_4 = \{z/\text{Bob}\} \\
&\quad\; = \neg \text{Manager}(\text{Bob}) \\
&\text{C10:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C9, C3) with } \theta_5 = \{\} \\
&\quad\; = \Box \quad \text{(EMPTY CLAUSE — contradiction!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**  
The empty clause signals a contradiction, meaning the negated goal is false, so the original goal is entailed:

$$KB \models \neg \text{Manager}(\text{Bob}) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Unification of Complex Terms

Find the MGU of $p(A, x, f(g(y)))$ and $p(z, f(B), f(w))$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pair the arguments: } (A, z), \;(x, f(B)), \;(f(g(y)), f(w)). \\
&\text{(1) } A \text{ and } z: \text{ bind } z = A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_1 = \{z/A\}. \\
&\text{(2) } x \text{ and } f(B): \text{ bind } x = f(B) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_2 = \{z/A,\; x/f(B)\}. \\
&\text{(3) } f(g(y)) \text{ and } f(w): \text{ function symbols match, descend: } g(y) \text{ and } w. \\
&\quad\; \text{Bind } w = g(y) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_3 = \{z/A,\; x/f(B),\; w/g(y)\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**MGU:** $\theta = \{z/A,\; x/f(B),\; w/g(y)\}$. All variables appear only once and the **occurs check** passes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven | Goal-driven | Contradiction-driven |
| **Clause Type** | Definite (Horn-like) | Definite (Horn-like) | Any CNF clause |
| **Soundness** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Completeness** | Yes (for definite KB) | Yes (for definite KB) | Refutation-complete |
| **Best For** | Monitoring, planning, rule firing | Query answering, Prolog | Theorem proving, refutation |
| **Typical Use** | Production systems, CLIPS | Prolog, expert systems | Automated theorem provers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: High-Level FOL Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FOL Knowledge Base"] --> B["Convert to CNF"]
    B --> C["Skolemize Variables"]
    C --> D["Clause Set"]
    D --> E{"Inference Strategy"}
    E --> F1["Forward Chaining"]
    E --> F2["Backward Chaining"]
    E --> F3["Resolution Refutation"]
    F1 --> G1["Apply GMP iteratively"]
    F2 --> G2["Unify goal with rule heads"]
    F3 --> G3["Add negated goal; resolve to empty clause"]
    G1 --> H["New Inferred Sentences"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Answer / Proof"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Forward Chaining Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB1["Fact Base"] --> M1["Match Premises"]
    KB2["Rule Base"] --> M1
    M1 --> U["Unify and Bind Variables"]
    U --> F["Fire Rule: Add Head to Fact Base"]
    F --> CHK{"New Fact Added?"}
    CHK -- Yes --> M1
    CHK -- No --> OUT["Return All Derived Facts"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Backward Chaining Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G["Goal Query"] --> U["Unify with Rule Head"]
    U --> R{"Rule Found?"}
    R -- Yes --> S["Generate Subgoals from Premises"]
    S --> REC["Recurse on Each Subgoal"]
    REC --> CHK{"All Subgoals Proven?"}
    CHK -- Yes --> SUCC["Return Substitutions - Success"]
    CHK -- No --> B["Backtrack and Try Next Rule"]
    R -- No --> F["Check Against Fact Base"]
    F --> OK{"Fact Matches?"}
    OK -- Yes --> SUCC
    OK -- No --> FAIL["Return Failure"]
    B --> U
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Resolution Refutation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["KB in CNF"] --> B["Negate Goal and Add to Clause Set"]
    B --> C["Select Two Resolvable Clauses"]
    C --> D["Apply Unification"]
    D --> E["Compute Resolvent Clause"]
    E --> F{"Empty Clause?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Contradiction - Goal Proven"]
    F -- No --> H["Add Resolvent to Clause Set"]
    H --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In Module 3 viva questions, examiners often ask students to *label the direction of inference* on such diagrams. Memorize the three keywords: **Forward = Data → Goal**, **Backward = Goal → Data**, **Resolution = Goal negation → Empty clause**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define unification in First-Order Logic. What is the Most General Unifier (MGU)?*

**Model Answer:**  
Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions syntactically identical. A substitution $\theta$ maps variables to terms. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest (least committed) such substitution — it binds each variable to the most general term possible, without introducing unnecessary structure. The MGU is computed by the **Unify** algorithm using recursive term matching and the **occurs check** to prevent infinite loops.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
*State and explain the Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) inference rule.*

**Model Answer:**  
GMP lifts propositional Modus Ponens into FOL. For atomic sentences $p_1', p_2', \dots, p_n'$ and an implication $p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q$, GMP fires when there exists a substitution $\theta$ such that $\text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i') = \theta$ for all $i$. The conclusion is $q\theta$. GMP is **sound** but not complete for full FOL; however, it is complete for KBs restricted to definite (Horn-like) clauses.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Forward Chaining & Unification**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following KB:
- $\forall x \, (\text{Crocodile}(x) \land \text{Scary}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Dangerous}(x))$
- $\text{Crocodile}(\text{Tommy})$
- $\text{Scary}(\text{Tommy})$
- $\forall x \, (\text{Dangerous}(x) \Rightarrow \text{StayAway}(x))$

Use **Generalized Modus Ponens with Forward Chaining** to derive $\text{StayAway}(\text{Tommy})$. Show all substitutions and intermediate facts.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** of the following pair of FOL expressions:
$$Q(a,\, g(x,\, b),\, f(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad Q(z,\, g(a,\, w),\, f(g(b,\, c)))$$
Show each step of the Unify algorithm, including the occurs check.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Chaining Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the rule whose premises match existing facts.**  
Rule 1: $\text{Crocodile}(x) \land \text{Scary}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Dangerous}(x)$.

**Step 2 — Unify each premise with a fact in the KB.**  
- $\text{Crocodile}(\text{Tommy})$ unifies with $\text{Crocodile}(x)$ using $\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Tommy}\}$.  
- $\text{Scary}(\text{Tommy})$ unifies with $\text{Scary}(x)$ using $\theta_2 = \{x/\text{Tommy}\}$.

**Step 3 — Combined MGU = $\{x/\text{Tommy}\}$.** Apply GMP:

$$\dfrac{\text{Crocodile}(\text{Tommy}),\;\text{Scary}(\text{Tommy}),\;\; \text{Crocodile}(x)\land\text{Scary}(x)\Rightarrow\text{Dangerous}(x)}{\text{Dangerous}(\text{Tommy})}$$

**Step 4 — Add $\text{Dangerous}(\text{Tommy})$ to fact base; iterate.**  
- Now Rule 4 matches: $\text{Dangerous}(x) \Rightarrow \text{StayAway}(x)$.  
- Substitute $x = \text{Tommy}$ via MGU $\{x/\text{Tommy}\}$.

**Step 5 — Apply GMP again.**  
$$\dfrac{\text{Dangerous}(\text{Tommy}),\;\;\text{Dangerous}(x)\Rightarrow\text{StayAway}(x)}{\text{StayAway}(\text{Tommy})}$$

**Final derived fact:** $\text{StayAway}(\text{Tommy})$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) MGU Computation:**

Pair the arguments of $Q$:

| Position | Term 1 | Term 2 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a$ | $z$ | Bind $z = a$ → $\theta_1 = \{z/a\}$ |
| 2 | $g(x, b)$ | $g(a, w)$ | Function symbol $g$ matches; descend: bind $x = a$, $w = b$ → $\theta_2 = \{z/a, x/a, w/b\}$ |
| 3 | $f(y)$ | $f(g(b, c))$ | Function $f$ matches; descend: bind $y = g(b, c)$ → $\theta_3 = \{z/a, x/a, w/b, y/g(b, c)\}$ |

**Final MGU:** $\theta = \{z/a,\; x/a,\; w/b,\; y/g(b, c)\}$. The occurs check passes (no variable occurs in its own binding). $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Resolution Refutation**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following FOL sentences to **CNF**, showing every step (implication removal, negation inward, standardization, Skolemization):
1. $\forall x \, (\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$
2. $\neg \exists x \, (\text{Immortal}(x))$
3. $\text{Human}(\text{Socrates})$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the CNF clauses obtained, prove by **resolution refutation** that $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CNF Conversion:**

**Sentence 1:** $\forall x \, (\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$
- Remove $\Rightarrow$: $\forall x \, (\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x))$
- Drop $\forall$: $\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x)$
- **CNF Clause C1:** $\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x)$

**Sentence 2:** $\neg \exists x \, (\text{Immortal}(x))$
- Quantifier duality: $\forall x \, (\neg \text{Immortal}(x))$
- **CNF Clause C2:** $\neg \text{Immortal}(x)$

**Sentence 3:** $\text{Human}(\text{Socrates})$
- **CNF Clause C3:** $\text{Human}(\text{Socrates})$

**(b) Resolution Refutation Proof:**

**Step 1 — Negate the goal.**  
Goal: $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$.  
Negation: $\neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$ → **Clause C4.**

**Step 2 — Resolve iteratively.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{C5:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C1, C3) with } \theta_1 = \{x/\text{Socrates}\} \\
&\quad\; = \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates}) \\
&\text{C6:} \;\; \text{Resolve(C5, C4) with } \theta_2 = \{\} \\
&\quad\; = \Box \quad \text{(EMPTY CLAUSE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The empty clause indicates a contradiction; hence the negated goal is false and the original goal is entailed.

$$\boxed{KB \models \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Skipping the occurs check** when computing MGU — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to standardize variables apart** before CNF conversion — leads to incorrect unification and *garbage resolvents*. Deduct up to 2 marks.
> 3. **Using Resolution on non-CNF sentences** — Resolution *only* works on clause form. Always convert to CNF first.
> 4. **Confusing GMP completeness**: GMP is *not* complete for full FOL. State the scope explicitly in definitions.
> 5. **Mistaking backward chaining direction** — In KTU, the convention is: *subgoals are derived from rule premises, not heads*. Examiners mark this strictly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Inference** = deriving new entailed sentences from the KB.
- ✅ **Soundness** = derived sentences are entailed; **Completeness** = all entailed sentences can be derived.
- ✅ **Lifting** extends propositional rules to FOL by adding **unification** + **quantifier instantiation**.
- ✅ **Unification** finds a substitution $\theta$ such that $p\theta = q\theta$; output is the **MGU**.
- ✅ **Occurs check** prevents binding $X = f(X)$ — *mandatory* in safe Prolog, optional but recommended elsewhere.
- ✅ **UI**: From $\forall v \, \alpha$, derive $\alpha\{v/g\}$ for any ground term $g$.
- ✅ **EI**: From $\exists v \, \alpha$, derive $\alpha\{v/k\}$ where $k$ is a new Skolem constant.
- ✅ **GMP** is the workhorse of forward/backward chaining; it is **sound** but **not complete** in full FOL.
- ✅ **Forward Chaining** is *data-driven*; **Backward Chaining** is *goal-driven*; both are complete for definite (Horn) clauses.
- ✅ **Resolution** is a **single inference rule** that is *sound* and *refutation-complete* for full FOL.
- ✅ **Resolution steps**: Convert to CNF → Negate goal → Resolve clauses until **empty clause** appears.
- ✅ **Prolog** uses depth-first backward chaining with depth limits (hence not strictly complete).
- ✅ Real-world applications: **MYCIN** (medical), **Prolog** (logic programming), **SMT solvers** (verification).
- ✅ Always **standardize variables** before CNF conversion — a classic KTU trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propositional vs. First Order Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Propositional vs. First Order Inference # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Logical Inference? **Logical Inference** is the process of deriving new logical statements (conclusions) from a given set of premises (knowledge base) using formally valid rules of reasoning. In Artificial Intelligence, inference is the computational engine that allows an agent to "think" — to deduce non-obvious facts from explicitly stored knowledge. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST522, Module 3):** > Inference is the mechanism by which an AI agent mechanically applies sound inference rules to a knowledge base (KB) to either prove a query (entailment) or compute new entailed sentences. The two principal formalisms are **Propositional Logic (PL)** and **First-Order Logic (FOL)**. ## 1.2 Propositional Logic (PL) Inference — Definition Propositional Logic represents knowledge using **declarative sentences (propositions)** built from Boolean variables and logical connectives ($\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$). Each proposition is a single symbol that is either **TRUE** or **FALSE** — no internal structure is exposed. **Formal Definition:** Given a Knowledge Base $KB$ expressed in PL, propositional inference determines whether $KB \models \alpha$ (KB entails sentence $\alpha$) by applying rules such as **Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, And-Elimination, Resolution**, etc. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property of Propositional Inference:** > It operates on **whole sentences only**. Variables, objects, and relations between objects are not first-class citizens. Every world state is a complete assignment of TRUE/FALSE to every proposition symbol. ## 1.3 First-Order Logic (FOL) Inference — Definition First-Order Logic (also called **Predicate Logic** or **First-Order Predicate Calculus, FOPC**) extends PL by introducing: - **Objects** (constants like $John, Cat, 5$) - **Relations/Predicates** (e.g., $Brother(John, Mary)$) - **Functions** (e.g., $FatherOf(John)$) - **Variables** ($x, y, z$) - **Quantifiers** ($\forall$ universal, $\exists$ existential) **Formal Definition:** FOL inference determines $KB \models_{FOL} \alpha$ by applying rules such as **Generalized Modus Ponens, Unification, Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining**, etc., after handling variables via substitution and unification. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Detective's Notebook:** > - **Propositional Logic** is like a detective who only keeps a checklist of complete facts: *"The window is broken"*, *"The butler was present"*, *"It rained last night"*. He can chain these whole-fact statements together with rules like "If window is broken AND butler is present, suspect the butler." > - **First-Order Logic** is like a more powerful detective who breaks each fact into its components: *"There exists a person $x$ such that $x$ is the butler AND $x$ is present AND $x$ has access to the broken window"*. He can answer questions about *any* person, *any* object — because the structure is exposed. > > **The Punchline:** PL is *finite* and *expressive enough for simple worlds*. FOL is *infinitely expressive* because variables can stand for any object, but this power comes with higher computational cost. ## 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in AI > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KTU Tests This Topic:** > Module 3 of PECST522 tests the *trade-off* between expressive power and computational tractability. PL inference is decidable (though NP-hard) but limited. FOL inference is undecidable in the general case (Gödel, Church, Turing) but vastly more expressive for real-world domains. AI agents must choose the right formalism for the job. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Inference Both PL and FOL inference rest on three foundational concepts: 1. **Syntax** — Well-formed formulas 2. **Semantics** — Meaning / truth in a model 3. **Inference Procedure** — Sound algorithm to derive conclusions ## 2.2 Propositional Inference — Operational Mechanics PL inference works on a finite set of propositional symbols $\{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$. A **model** is a truth assignment $m: \{P_i\} \rightarrow \{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}$. There are exactly $2^n$ possible models. **Core Inference Rules (PL):** | Rule | Notation | Meaning | |------|----------|---------| | Modus Ponens | $\dfrac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha}{\beta}$ | From implication and premise, conclude consequent | | And-Elimination | $\dfrac{\alpha \land \beta}{\alpha}$ | A conjunct can be extracted | | Or-Introduction | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha \lor \beta}$ | A disjunct can be extended | | Resolution | $\dfrac{\lnot P \lor Q, \quad P}{\ Q\ }$ | The single sufficient rule for refutation | | Contradiction | $\dfrac{\lnot \alpha, \quad \alpha}{\text{FALSE}}$ | Inconsistency detected | **Key Algorithm:** **PL-Resolution** — repeatedly apply resolution until either the empty clause is derived (KB is unsatisfiable) or no new clauses can be generated. **Model-Checking Algorithm:** **DPLL** (Davis–Putnam–Logemann–Loveland) and **WalkSAT** for checking satisfiability. ## 2.3 First-Order Inference — Operational Mechanics FOL inference operates on sentences containing **variables, quantifiers, functions, and predicates**. The central additional operation is **Unification** — finding a substitution that makes two expressions identical. **Core Inference Rules (FOL):** | Rule | Description | |------|-------------| | **Unification** | Algorithm that finds the Most General Unifier (MGU) $\theta$ such that $\text{UNIFY}(p, q) = \theta$ where $p\theta = q\theta$ | | **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** | $\dfrac{p_1', p_2', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}$ where $\theta$ unifies $p_i'$ with $p_i$ | | **Forward Chaining** | Data-driven: start with known facts, apply GMP until goal reached | | **Backward Chaining** | Goal-driven: start with goal, recursively reduce to sub-goals | | **FOL Resolution** | Refutation-based: convert to CNF, resolve using unification, derive empty clause | ## 2.4 Detailed Comparison — PL vs FOL Inference | Parameter | Propositional Inference | First-Order Inference | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------| | Atomic Unit | Proposition symbol $P$ | Predicate applied to terms: $P(x, f(y))$ | | Variables | **Not present** | **Universally / existentially quantified** | | Quantifier Handling | None required | $\forall$ / $\exists$ elimination required | | Unification | Trivial (identity only) | Central algorithm — MGU computation | | Search Space | Finite ($2^n$ models) | **Infinite** (unbounded domain) | | Decidability | **Decidable** (though NP-complete for entailment) | **Semidecidable** — will find proof if exists, may loop otherwise | | Soundness | Depends on rule set | Depends on rule set (GMP, Resolution are sound) | | Completeness | Resolution is complete | Resolution is complete (refutation-complete) | | Typical Algorithms | DPLL, WalkSAT, Resolution | Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining, FOL-Resolution | | Expressive Power | Limited — cannot say "for all x" | Turing-complete in expressive power | | Computational Cost | Lower | Higher (unification is exponential in worst case) | | Real-world Use | Digital circuits, simple controllers, SAT solvers | Knowledge graphs, NLP, expert systems, theorem provers | ## 2.5 The Critical Concept: Reduction to Propositional Inference > [!IMPORTANT] > **Herbrand's Theorem (1930):** > If a sentence $\alpha$ in FOL is entailed by a KB, then there exists a proof using only a **finite subset of the Herbrand Universe** (the set of all ground terms constructible from constants and functions in KB). This permits — in principle — reducing FOL inference to propositional inference via **grounding (instantiation)**. **Practical Implication:** Modern FOL inference systems often work by *partially* instantiating quantified sentences, generating a propositional subset, and applying SAT-style techniques. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | |-------------------|---------| | $KB \models \alpha$ | KB *entails* sentence $\alpha$ (every model of KB is a model of $\alpha$) | | $KB \vdash \alpha$ | $\alpha$ is *derivable* from KB via inference rules | | $\text{UNIFY}(p, q) = \theta$ | Unification algorithm returns substitution $\theta$ | | $\theta = \text{MGU}(p, q)$ | Most General Unifier | | $\text{SUBST}(\theta, \alpha)$ | Apply substitution $\theta$ to sentence $\alpha$ | | $p\theta = q\theta$ | Condition for $\theta$ to be a unifier | | $\text{GMP}: \dfrac{p_1', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}$ | Generalized Modus Ponens | | Resolution (PL): $\dfrac{\ell_1 \lor \ldots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \ldots \lor m_n}{(\ell_1 \lor \ldots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \ldots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \ldots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \ldots \lor m_n)\theta}$ | Standard resolution rule | | Herbrand Universe $\mathcal{H}(KB)$ | Set of all ground terms from KB's constants & functions | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where These Concepts Are Used:** > - **SAT Solvers (Z3, MiniSAT)** — propositional inference at industrial scale (hardware verification, software testing). > - **Prolog / Datalog engines** — backward chaining FOL inference, used in databases, NLP, and compiler optimization. > - **Automated Theorem Provers (Vampire, E, SPASS)** — FOL resolution-based, used for mathematical proofs, security verification, and formal methods. > - **Knowledge Graphs (Google, Wikidata)** — FOL-style reasoning over relations between entities. > - **Expert Systems (MYCIN, DENDRAL)** — rule-based forward chaining in medical diagnosis. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Propositional Inference by Resolution **Knowledge Base (KB):** - $P_1$: $\lnot Raining \Rightarrow Wet\_Grass$ (i.e., $\lnot Raining \rightarrow Wet\_Grass$) - $P_2$: $Sprinkler$ - $P_3$: $Sprinkler \Rightarrow Wet\_Grass$ - $P_4$: $Wet\_Grass$ (query — is it entailed?) **Step 1 — Convert all to CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form):** $$P_1: \quad Raining \lor Wet\_Grass$$ $$P_2: \quad Sprinkler$$ $$P_3: \quad \lnot Sprinkler \lor Wet\_Grass$$ **Step 2 — Negate the query to prove by refutation:** $$\lnot P_4: \quad \lnot Wet\_Grass$$ **Step 3 — Apply Resolution:** Resolve $P_1$ and $\lnot P_4$: $$\dfrac{Raining \lor Wet\_Grass, \quad \lnot Wet\_Grass}{Raining}$$ Resolve $P_3$ and $P_2$: $$\dfrac{\lnot Sprinkler \lor Wet\_Grass, \quad Sprinkler}{Wet\_Grass}$$ Wait — we now have $Wet\_Grass$ directly. Combined with $\lnot Wet\_Grass$: $$\dfrac{Wet\_Grass, \quad \lnot Wet\_Grass}{\square \text{ (empty clause)}}$$ **Conclusion:** Empty clause derived $\Rightarrow$ KB $\models Wet\_Grass$. The query is entailed. ∎ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — FOL Inference via Generalized Modus Ponens **Knowledge Base (FOL):** - $\forall x: King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)$ - $King(John)$ - $Greedy(John)$ **Query:** $Evil(John)$? **Step 1 — Standardize variables apart** (no variable conflicts here — only one variable $x$ used). **Step 2 — Unify premise atoms with KB facts:** For the rule $King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)$: - Unify $King(x)$ with $King(John)$: substitution $\theta_1 = \{x/John\}$ - Unify $Greedy(x)$ with $Greedy(John)$: substitution $\theta_2 = \{x/John\}$ **Step 3 — Compose substitutions** (all unify with same $x$): $$\theta = \{x/John\}$$ **Step 4 — Apply GMP to derive the conclusion:** $$\dfrac{King(John), \quad Greedy(John), \quad King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)}{Evil(John)}$$ (After applying $\theta$ to $Evil(x)$, we get $Evil(John)$.) **Conclusion:** $KB \models_{FOL} Evil(John)$. ∎ ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Unification Algorithm in Detail **Problem:** Find the MGU of $p = Knows(John, x)$ and $q = Knows(y, Mary)$. **Step-by-step unification using recursive UNIFY algorithm:** | Step | Expression | Substitution | Reason | |------|------------|--------------|--------| | 0 | $\text{UNIFY}(Knows(John, x), Knows(y, Mary))$ | $\theta_0 = \{\}$ | Initial state | | 1 | $\text{UNIFY}(John, y)$ | $\theta_1 = \{y/John\}$ | Predicate symbol matches, descend into args | | 2 | Apply $\theta_1$ to remaining: $\text{UNIFY}(x, Mary)$ | $\theta_2 = \{x/Mary\}$ | Constant-variable unifiable | | 3 | Compose: $\theta = \theta_1 \circ \theta_2$ | $\theta = \{y/John, x/Mary\}$ | MGU found | | 4 | Verify: $Knows(John, x)\theta = Knows(John, Mary)$
$Knows(y, Mary)\theta = Knows(John, Mary)$ | ✓ Match | Success | **Unification Algorithm — Pseudocode (for KTU reference only — this IS a CS subject):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{UNIFY}(p, q) = \\ &\quad \text{if } p \text{ or } q \text{ is a variable:} \\ &\quad\quad \text{return } \text{UNIFY-VAR}(p, q) \\ &\quad \text{else if } p \text{ and } q \text{ are both compound: } \\ &\quad\quad \text{if } \text{PRED}(p) \neq \text{PRED}(q): \text{ return FAIL} \\ &\quad\quad \text{return } \text{UNIFY}(\text{ARGS}(p), \text{ARGS}(q)) \\ &\quad \text{else if } p = q: \text{ return empty substitution} \\ &\quad \text{else: return FAIL} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Soundness vs Completeness > [!IMPORTANT] > **Soundness:** If $KB \vdash \alpha$ (derivable using the rules), then $KB \models \alpha$ (semantically entailed). **No false conclusions** are ever derived. > > **Completeness:** If $KB \models \alpha$ (semantically entailed), then $KB \vdash \alpha$ (derivable using the rules). **All true conclusions** can be derived. | Inference System | Sound? | Complete? | Decidable? | |------------------|--------|-----------|-----------| | Propositional Resolution | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | | Modus Ponens (PL) | ✓ Yes | ✗ No (incomplete for full PL) | ✓ Yes | | FOL Resolution | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes (refutation-complete) | ✗ No (semidecidable) | | GMP + Forward Chaining (FOL) | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes (for definite clauses) | ✗ No | | Generalized Resolution | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | ✗ No | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Inference Process Flow — Propositional vs FOL ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Knowledge Base KB] --> B0{Formalism?} B0 -->|Propositional Logic| C1[KB as set of
propositional clauses] B0 -->|First-Order Logic| C2[KB as set of
FOL sentences] C1 --> D1[Convert to CNF] C2 --> D2[Convert to CNF
using Skolemization
and quantifier removal] D1 --> E1[Apply PL Resolution
or DPLL / WalkSAT] D2 --> E2[Unification step
compute MGU theta] E2 --> F2[Apply FOL Resolution
or Forward / Backward Chaining] E1 --> G1[Result: Entailed?
or empty clause?] F2 --> G2[Result: Entailed?
or infinite loop?] G1 --> H1[Decision] G2 --> H2[Decision] ``` ## 4.2 Unification Algorithm — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Expression P] --> unifyMain[UNIFY function] inputB[Expression Q] --> unifyMain unifyMain --> checkA{P is variable?} unifyMain --> checkB{Q is variable?} checkA -->|Yes| unifyVar1[UNIFY-VAR call] checkB -->|Yes| unifyVar2[UNIFY-VAR call] checkA -->|No| compoundCheck[Check predicate
symbols match] compoundCheck -->|Match| recurseArgs[Recurse on arguments] compoundCheck -->|Mismatch| failOut[Return FAIL] recurseArgs --> composeTheta[Compose substitutions] unifyVar1 --> composeTheta unifyVar2 --> composeTheta composeTheta --> mguOut[Return MGU theta] ``` ## 4.3 Resolution Refutation — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD kbIn[Input KB] --> cnfConv[Step 1: Convert to CNF] queryIn[Query alpha] --> negateQ[Step 2: Negate query to get NOT alpha] cnfConv --> clauseSet[Clause Set S] negateQ --> clauseSet clauseSet --> resolveLoop[Step 3: Loop - pick two clauses
containing complementary literals] resolveLoop --> resolventCheck{Resolvent
new?} resolventCheck -->|Yes| addResolvent[Add to clause set] resolventCheck -->|No| pickOther[Pick different pair] addResolvent --> emptyCheck{Empty clause
derived?} pickOther --> resolveLoop emptyCheck -->|Yes| success[KB entails alpha - PROVED] emptyCheck -->|No, no new clauses| failOut[Cannot prove - either false
or no proof found] ``` ## 4.4 Forward vs Backward Chaining — FOL Inference ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FC[Forward Chaining - Data Driven] F1[Known Facts] --> F2[Match rule premises] F2 --> F3[Apply GMP] F3 --> F4[New fact derived] F4 --> F2 F4 --> F5{Goal reached?} F5 -->|Yes| F6[STOP - Success] F5 -->|No| F2 end subgraph BC[Backward Chaining - Goal Driven] B1[Goal query] --> B2[Find rules with goal in head] B2 --> B3[Recurse on premises as subgoals] B3 --> B4[Check against known facts] B4 --> B5{All subgoals proven?} B5 -->|Yes| B6[STOP - Success] B5 -->|No| B3 end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`** **Q1. Differentiate between Propositional Logic and First-Order Logic with suitable examples. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Propositional Logic (PL)** uses atomic propositions that are either TRUE or FALSE, e.g., $Raining$, $Cold$. It cannot express relations between objects or quantify over them. - **First-Order Logic (FOL)** introduces objects, predicates, functions, variables, and quantifiers. Example: $\forall x: Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)$. - **Key Distinction:** PL has no variables or quantifiers, while FOL allows them. FOL is more expressive but has higher inference cost and is semidecidable, whereas PL inference is decidable. --- **Q2. What is unification in First-Order Logic? Explain with an example. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Unification** is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. It is a core operation in FOL inference. - The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution. - **Example:** UNIFY$(Knows(John, x), Knows(y, Bill))$ returns $\theta = \{y/John, x/Bill\}$, because applying $\theta$ yields $Knows(John, Bill)$ in both cases. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]`** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the process of **resolution in propositional logic** with a suitable example. Show how the resolution rule can be used to prove a query by refutation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **limitations of propositional inference** and explain how First-Order Logic overcomes them. Illustrate with at least two examples. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A(a): **Step 1 — Define the Resolution Rule:** The resolution rule for two clauses $C_1$ and $C_2$ containing complementary literals $\ell$ and $\lnot \ell$: $$\dfrac{C_1 = \ell \lor A, \quad C_2 = \lnot \ell \lor B}{C = A \lor B}$$ **Step 2 — State the Refutation Strategy:** To prove $KB \models \alpha$: 1. Convert $KB \land \lnot \alpha$ to CNF. 2. Repeatedly apply resolution. 3. If the empty clause $\square$ is derived, $KB \models \alpha$ is proved. **Step 3 — Worked Example:** KB: - $P \Rightarrow Q$ (i.e., $\lnot P \lor Q$) - $Q \Rightarrow R$ (i.e., $\lnot Q \lor R$) - $P$ Query: $R$? Negate query: $\lnot R$ Resolve $(\lnot P \lor Q)$ and $P$: yields $Q$ Resolve $(\lnot Q \lor R)$ and $Q$: yields $R$ Resolve $R$ and $\lnot R$: yields $\square$ (empty clause) **Conclusion:** $KB \models R$. ∎ #### Model Solution for Q-A(b): **Step 1 — Limitations of PL:** 1. **No variables/quantifiers** — cannot express "All men are mortal." 2. **No object structure** — cannot say "John is Mary's father" as a relation. 3. **No functions** — cannot refer to derived objects like $Mother(John)$. 4. **Exponential blowup** — must enumerate every proposition for every object explicitly. **Step 2 — How FOL Overcomes These:** - **Variables and quantifiers** allow compact representation: - PL: $Mortal(Socrates) \land Mortal(Plato) \land \ldots$ (infinite list) - FOL: $\forall x: Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)$ (one sentence) - **Predicates** express relations: $Father(John, Mary)$. - **Functions** express derived objects: $\forall m: Mother(m) = WifeOf(FatherOf(m))$. **Step 3 — Two Examples:** **Example 1 — Tautology in FOL:** $KB: \forall x: Student(x) \Rightarrow \exists y: Teaches(y, x)$ $Query: \exists y: \exists x: Teaches(y, x)$ (Some teacher teaches some student) This is provable in FOL with one step, but in PL would need an exponential expansion. **Example 2 — Logical Entailment:** $KB: \forall x: Cat(x) \Rightarrow Animal(x); Cat(Tom)$ $Query: Animal(Tom)$ FOL solves via GMP in 2 steps. PL would require separate symbols $Cat\_Tom$ and $Animal\_Tom$ for each object. **Conclusion:** FOL provides **compactness, expressiveness, and structural reasoning** at the cost of higher computational complexity. ∎ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** What is **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)**? Explain how it extends ordinary Modus Ponens. Apply GMP to prove a query using a worked example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **forward chaining** and **backward chaining** in First-Order Logic. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B(a): **Step 1 — Definition of GMP:** GMP lifts Modus Ponens to handle variables via unification. For atomic sentences $p_i', p_i$ and implication $(p_1 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)$: $$\text{GMP} = \dfrac{p_1', p_2', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}$$ where $\theta$ is the MGU such that $p_i\theta = p_i'\theta$ for all $i$. **Step 2 — How GMP Extends Modus Ponens:** - **Ordinary MP** matches two *ground* sentences. - **GMP** matches predicate patterns via *unification*, allowing variables to be bound. **Step 3 — Worked Example:** KB: - $\forall x: Crocodile(x) \land Reptile(x) \Rightarrow ColdBlooded(x)$ - $Crocdile(Steve)$ - $Reptile(Steve)$ Query: $ColdBlooded(Steve)$? **Step 1 — Unify $Crocdile(x)$ with $Crocdile(Steve)$:** $\theta_1 = \{x/Steve\}$ **Step 2 — Unify $Reptile(x)$ with $Reptile(Steve)$:** $\theta_2 = \{x/Steve\}$ **Step 3 — Apply GMP:** Conclude $ColdBlooded(Steve)$. ∎ #### Model Solution for Q-B(b): | Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | |--------|------------------|-------------------| | **Direction** | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal → subgoals) | | **Strategy** | Apply rules when premises are satisfied | Decompose goal into subgoals, prove each | | **Inference Type** | Deductive, bottom-up | Reductive, top-down | | **Implementation** | Rete algorithm, production systems | Logic programming (Prolog) | | **Best For** | Monitoring, planning, reactive systems | Query answering, diagnosis | | **Inference Mode** | Breadth-first over facts | Depth-first over goals | | **Decidability** | May not terminate for cyclic KB | May not terminate for recursive rules | | **Advantage** | Generates all derivable facts (useful when many queries) | Efficient for single queries, only relevant rules fired | | **Disadvantage** | May derive many irrelevant facts | May explore irrelevant subgoals without good heuristics | **Example:** KB: $A \Rightarrow B$, $B \Rightarrow C$, $A$ **Forward Chaining:** Derives $B$ from $A$, then $C$ from $B$. ✓ **Backward Chaining:** To prove $C$, looks for rule with $C$ in head: $B \Rightarrow C$. Needs $B$. To prove $B$, looks for $A \Rightarrow B$. Needs $A$. $A$ is in KB. Proved. ✓ Both reach the same conclusion, but explore different paths. ∎ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to negate the query** during refutation — most common 2-mark loss. > 2. **Skipping CNF conversion steps** in resolution problems — examiners allocate marks for explicit conversion. > 3. **Confusing soundness and completeness** — soundness = "no false proofs," completeness = "all true proofs findable." > 4. **Not specifying MGU** when asked for unification — students often give a unifier but not the *most general* one. > 5. **In FOL questions, forgetting Skolemization** during CNF conversion — required for removing $\exists$ quantifiers. > 6. **Mixing up forward vs backward chaining** — remember: forward = data-driven, backward = goal-driven. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] **Propositional Logic (PL)** uses Boolean variables with no internal structure; **First-Order Logic (FOL)** adds objects, predicates, variables, functions, and quantifiers. - [x] **PL inference** is *decidable* but NP-complete; **FOL inference** is *semidecidable* and may not terminate. - [x] **Modus Ponens** (PL) operates on ground sentences; **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** (FOL) operates via unification. - [x] **Resolution** is the single rule sufficient for refutation in *both* PL and FOL. - [x] **Unification** is the central FOL algorithm — it computes the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**. - [x] **CNF conversion** steps: eliminate $\Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$ → move $\lnot$ inward (De Morgan) → standardize variables → Skolemize ($\exists$) → drop $\forall$ → distribute $\lor$ over $\land$. - [x] **Refutation proof:** Add $\lnot\alpha$ to KB → derive empty clause $\square$ via resolution → conclude $KB \models \alpha$. - [x] **Forward chaining** = data-driven, good for deriving many facts; **Backward chaining** = goal-driven, good for single queries. - [x] **Herbrand's Theorem** justifies reducing FOL to PL via grounding over the Herbrand universe. - [x] **Soundness** (no false proofs) and **completeness** (all true proofs found) must be remembered separately. - [x] **Skolem functions** replace existentially quantified variables during CNF conversion; constants are Skolem functions of zero arity. - [x] **Standardize apart** — rename variables in different rules to avoid conflicts before unification. - [x] **Decidability boundary:** PL is decidable; FOL with full quantifiers is not decidable but refutation-complete. ---

topics.title: Unification and Lifts, Forward Chaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unification and Lifts, Forward Chaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unification — The Heart of Logical Matching

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution θ (theta)** that makes two first-order logic (FOL) expressions syntactically identical. When such a substitution exists, the two expressions are said to **unify**, and θ is called a **unifier**.

A substitution is a mapping from variables to terms. For example:

$$\theta = \{x/\text{Riya},\ y/\text{Anand}\}$$

means replace every occurrence of `x` with `Riya` and every `y` with `Anand`.

When applied to an expression `α`, we write `αθ` (alpha-sub). The unification function `UNIFY(α, β)` returns the most general unifier (MGU) that makes `αθ = βθ`.

### Intuition: Unification is Like a "Smart Pattern Matcher"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you have two partially filled forms — Form A has "Student _X_ scored _Y_ marks" and Form B says "Student Riya scored 95 marks." Unification is the act of figuring out what values (_X_ = Riya, _Y_ = 95) make both statements talk about the **same scenario**. It's like solving a "fill-in-the-blanks" puzzle where blanks in both forms must match.

The catch: we want the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** — the one that makes the *fewest assumptions*, leaving as many variables free as possible.

## 1.2 Lifts — Promoting Propositional Rules to First-Order Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Russell & Norvig, KTU Syllabus)**
> A **lift** (or **lifting**) is the conversion of a propositional logic inference rule into its equivalent, more powerful **first-order logic (FOL)** version by introducing variables, quantifiers, and substitution.

Common lifts:

| Propositional Rule | Lifted Version |
|---|---|
| Modus Ponens | **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** |
| Modus Tollens | **Generalized Modus Tollens** |
| Resolution | **Generalized Resolution** |

The lift preserves logical soundness but massively increases expressive power.

### Intuition: From Fixed Facts to General Patterns

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Propositional logic says: *"If the bulb is OFF and the switch is ON, the bulb is BROKEN."* That's a single fact. The lifted (FOL) version says: *"For any device D — if D is OFF and the switch S is ON, then D is BROKEN."* Now the same rule works for *any* device — TV, fan, light — without rewriting. **Lifting = generalization from examples to patterns.**

## 1.3 Forward Chaining — Reasoning from Evidence to Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Forward Chaining** is a **data-driven** reasoning algorithm that begins with the **known facts** in the knowledge base and repeatedly applies inference rules whose premises are satisfied, deriving new facts until either (a) no more facts can be derived, or (b) the **goal/query** is established.

It is the implementation backbone of **Generalized Modus Ponens** as a practical inference engine.

### Intuition: Forward Chaining is Detective Work from Clues

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine Sherlock Holmes at a crime scene. He starts with what he **sees** (footprints, broken glass) and works *forwards*, deriving conclusions step by step: footprints → someone walked here → it's the suspect → motive established. He does NOT start with "who did it?" and work backwards (that would be **backward chaining**). Forward chaining is **clue-driven inference**.

### Key Metric Symbols (Bold for Emphasis)

- **KB** — Knowledge Base (set of FOL sentences)
- **θ** — Substitution (unifier)
- **MGU** — Most General Unifier
- **α, β** — Logical expressions/sentences
- **⊢** — Entailment symbol (KB ⊢ q means q is entailed)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unification Algorithm — Structured Logic Flow

The unification algorithm `UNIFY(p, q)` works recursively on the structure of two FOL expressions:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Same Predicate & Arity Check:** If `p` and `q` are *different predicates* (e.g., `Knows(x,y)` vs `Loves(x,y)`) → return **FAILURE**. They cannot unify.
2. **Predicate Decomposition:** If `p = P(s₁, s₂, …, sₙ)` and `q = P(t₁, t₂, …, tₙ)`, recurse on each pair `(sᵢ, tᵢ)`.
3. **Constant-Constant Rule:** If both `s` and `t` are identical constants → return **empty substitution** `{}`.
4. **Variable-Term Rule (Occurs Check Critical):**
   - If `s` is a variable `v` and `t` is a term:
     - If `v` occurs inside `t` → **FAILURE** (otherwise infinite recursion, e.g., unifying `x` with `Knows(x,y)`).
     - Else → add `{v/t}` to substitution.
5. **Composition:** When two parts unify with substitutions θ₁ and θ₂, compose them: `θ = θ₂ ∘ θ₁`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Occurs Check** is the most-lost-marks step in KTU exams. Always verify a variable does not appear within the term it would be substituted into.

## 2.2 Most General Unifier (MGU)

> [!NOTE]
> A unifier `θ` is a **Most General Unifier (MGU)** if no other unifier `θ'` is strictly more specific (i.e., there is no substitution `γ` such that `θ' = γ ∘ θ` where `γ` is non-empty).

For example, `θ₁ = {x/John}` is more general than `θ₂ = {x/John, y/Mary}` because `θ₂` makes an unnecessary extra commitment.

## 2.3 Forward Chaining — Detailed Operational Rules

The forward chaining algorithm maintains two sets:
- **Facts:** Known true FOL atoms
- **Rules:** Definite clauses of the form `(p₁ ∧ p₂ ∧ … ∧ pₙ) → q`

### Operational Cycle:

1. **Trigger Check:** Find a rule whose premises are ALL satisfied (by substitution θ) using **unification** against current facts.
2. **Apply GMP:** Derive the consequent `qθ` as a new fact.
3. **Add & Loop:** Add `qθ` to the Facts set.
4. **Termination:** Stop when no new facts are added OR the query is in Facts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Termination Concern:** Forward chaining in FOL is **semi-decidable** — it may run forever without reaching a goal if the goal is not entailed. This is a known theoretical limitation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| `θ` (theta) | A substitution mapping | Unification |
| `αθ` | Apply substitution θ to expression α | Unification |
| `UNIFY(α, β) = θ` | Returns MGU making αθ = βθ | Unification |
| `θ₁ ∘ θ₂` | Composition: apply θ₁ then θ₂ | Unification |
| `KB ⊢ q` | Knowledge base entails query q | Forward Chaining |
| `p₁ ∧ p₂ → q` | Definite clause (Horn) | Forward Chaining |
| `GMP` | Generalized Modus Ponens | Forward Chaining |
| `Occurs(x, t)` | True if variable x occurs in term t | Unification (Occurs Check) |
| `MGU` | Most General Unifier | Unification |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Symptoms (facts) → disease hypotheses via forward chaining rules.
- **Network Intrusion Detection:** Packet patterns trigger security rules forward.
- **Semantic Web / RDF Reasoners:** Ontological rules fire as new facts emerge.
- **Prolog & Datalog Engines:** Use lifted resolution and forward chaining internally.
- **Automated Theorem Provers (e.g., Otter, Vampire):** Rely on unification as the core matcher.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Unification with Multiple Variables

**Problem:** Find the MGU of:

$$p = Knows(John, x)$$

$$q = Knows(y, Mary)$$

### Solution Walk-through:

**Step 1 — Predicate Check:** Both are `Knows/2`. Arity matches (both 2 arguments). Proceed.

**Step 2 — Decompose into argument pairs:**
- Pair 1: `John` and `y`
- Pair 2: `x` and `Mary`

**Step 3 — Unify Pair 1 (`John`, `y`):**
- `John` is a constant, `y` is a variable.
- Apply rule: variable substitutes with constant when variable not in constant.
- Substitution θ₁ = `{y/John}`

**Step 4 — Unify Pair 2 (`x`, `Mary`):**
- After applying θ₁, `Knows(John, x)θ₁ = Knows(John, x)`.
- After applying θ₁, `Knows(y, Mary)θ₁ = Knows(John, Mary)`.
- Now unify `x` with `Mary`.
- Substitution θ₂ = `{x/Mary}`

**Step 5 — Compose substitutions:**

$$\theta = \theta_2 \circ \theta_1 = \{x/Mary,\ y/John\}$$

**Step 6 — Verify:**

$$Knows(John, x)\theta = Knows(John, Mary)$$

$$Knows(y, Mary)\theta = Knows(John, Mary) \quad \checkmark$$

> **[MGU = {x/Mary, y/John}]**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: The Occurs Check Trap

**Problem:** Unify:

$$p = Loves(x, x)$$

$$q = Loves(y, F(y))$$

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Decompose:** Same predicate `Loves/2`. Two pairs:
- Pair 1: `x` and `y`
- Pair 2: `x` and `F(y)`

**Step 2 — Unify Pair 1:** `x` is variable, `y` is variable. Substitute `x` with `y`:
- θ₁ = `{x/y}`

**Step 3 — Apply θ₁ then Unify Pair 2:**
- After θ₁: `Loves(y, y)` and `Loves(y, F(y))`.
- Unify `y` with `F(y)`.

**Step 4 — Occurs Check:** Does `y` occur inside `F(y)`? **YES.** This creates infinite recursion: `y = F(y) = F(F(y)) = …`

**Result: UNIFICATION FAILS.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This example is a **classic KTU exam question**. Always state explicitly: *"Failure due to occurs check: variable `y` occurs inside `F(y)`."*

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Forward Chaining with Multiple Rules

**Knowledge Base (KB):**

$$R_1: \quad \text{Crown}(x) \wedge \text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \text{King}(x)$$

$$R_2: \quad \text{King}(x) \rightarrow \text{Person}(x)$$

$$R_3: \quad \text{Crown}(x) \wedge \text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \text{Royal}(x)$$

**Facts:**

$$F_1: \quad \text{Crown(John)}, \quad F_2: \quad \text{Person(John)}$$

**Query:** Is `King(John)` entailed?

### Step-by-Step Forward Chaining Trace:

**Iteration 1:**
- Try R₁: Premises `Crown(x)` and `Person(x)` unify with facts using `{x/John}` (via UNIFY).
- Apply GMP: Derive `King(John)`. Add to facts.
- Try R₃: Same premises satisfied. Derive `Royal(John)`. Add to facts.

**Iteration 2:**
- Try R₂: `King(x)` unifies with `King(John)` via `{x/John}`. Derive `Person(John)` (already known — idempotent).
- No new facts emerge.

**Termination:** Query `King(John)` is in fact set. **ENTAILED.**

| Step | Rule Fired | Unifier θ | New Fact Added |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | R₁ | {x/John} | King(John) ✓ |
| 2 | R₃ | {x/John} | Royal(John) |
| 3 | R₂ | {x/John} | Person(John) (repeat) |

**Trace Table for Exam Answer:**

```
Iteration 1:
   Match R₁: Crown(x)↔Crown(John), Person(x)↔Person(John) → θ={x/John}
   Add: King(John)
   
Iteration 2:
   Match R₃: Same θ → Add Royal(John)
   
Iteration 3:
   Match R₂: King(x)↔King(John) → θ={x/John}
   Add: Person(John) (already exists, no change)
   
Stop: No new facts; King(John) ∈ Facts → SUCCESS
```

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Lifts Demonstration

**Propositional Rule:**

$$A \wedge B \rightarrow C$$

**Lifted Version (using GMP with variables):**

$$\forall x:\ P(x) \wedge Q(x) \rightarrow R(x)$$

**Given Facts:** `P(Anand)`, `Q(Anand)`.

**Apply GMP:** Unify `P(x)` with `P(Anand)` → θ = `{x/Anand}`. Then unify `Q(x)θ = Q(Anand)` with `Q(Anand)` ✓. Consequent: `R(Anand)`.

> This lift turns **1 propositional rule** into a rule working over **infinitely many entities** — the power of first-order representation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Unification | Lifts | Forward Chaining |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Role** | Pattern matcher | Rule generalizer | Inference engine |
| **Input** | Two FOL expressions | Propositional rule | KB + Facts |
| **Output** | MGU (substitution) | FOL rule with variables | Derived facts |
| **Direction** | Bidirectional | One-way upgrade | Forward (data → goal) |
| **Data Driven?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Decision** | KTU 2024: CO3 | KTU 2024: CO3 | KTU 2024: CO3 |
| **Complexity** | O(exp(n)) worst case | Linear in rule size | Semi-decidable in FOL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Unification Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: UNIFY p, q] --> B{p and q identical constants?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return empty substitution]
    B -- No --> D{One is variable v?}
    D -- Yes --> E{v occurs inside other term?}
    E -- Yes --> F[FAIL: Occurs Check Violation]
    E -- No --> G[Add v to substitution as v to t]
    D -- No --> H{Both compound terms with same predicate?}
    H -- No --> I[FAIL: Predicate mismatch]
    H -- Yes --> J[Decompose into argument pairs]
    J --> K[Recursively UNIFY each pair]
    K --> L{All pairs unified?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Return composed MGU]
    L -- No --> F
    G --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Forward Chaining Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Knowledge Base KB] --> B[Fact Set: Known Truths]
    C[Rule Set: Horn Clauses] --> D[Rule Matcher]
    B --> D
    D --> E{Unify rule premises with facts?}
    E -- Yes via MGU theta --> F[Apply Generalized Modus Ponens]
    F --> G[Derive new fact q theta]
    G --> H{New fact already in KB?}
    H -- No --> I[Add to Fact Set]
    H -- Yes --> J[Skip: No new info]
    I --> K{Goal query q in Facts?}
    K -- Yes --> L[SUCCESS: Return True]
    K -- No --> M{New facts added?}
    M -- Yes --> D
    M -- No --> N[FAIL: Cannot entail]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The Lift Transformation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Propositional Rule: A and B implies C] --> B[Introduce Variables]
    B --> C[Add Universal Quantifier: For all x]
    C --> D[Lifted Rule: For all x: P x and Q x implies R x]
    D --> E[Apply via GMP with Substitution theta]
    E --> F[Derive specific fact for each x]
    F --> G[Scalable Inference over infinite domain]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Unified Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    U[Unification] -->|provides MGU| GMP
    L[Lifts] -->|upgrade propositional rules| GMP
    GMP[Generalized Modus Ponens] -->|drives| FC[Forward Chaining]
    FC -->|data-driven inference| KB[Enriched Knowledge Base]
    KB -->|feeds back into| U
```
