# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define unification in First-Order Logic. Explain the role of the Most General Unifier (MGU).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Unification** is the process of finding a substitution **θ** that makes two first-order logic expressions syntactically identical. Formally, given two expressions α and β, unification finds θ such that αθ = βθ.

The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the unifier that makes the fewest commitments — i.e., it leaves the maximum number of variables uninstantiated. For example, unifying `P(x, y)` with `P(A, B)` gives MGU = `{x/A, y/B}` rather than the more specific `{x/A, y/B, z/C}` which unnecessarily binds an unrelated variable. The MGU is unique up to variable renaming and is fundamental for efficient inference in resolution and forward chaining systems.

*(Mark distribution: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for MGU explanation)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is forward chaining? How is it different from backward chaining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Forward Chaining** is a data-driven reasoning algorithm that starts with the known facts in the knowledge base and applies inference rules (typically in the form of Horn clauses) in the forward direction to derive new facts until the query is proven or no new inferences are possible.

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data → Goal | Goal → Data |
| **Strategy** | Data-driven | Goal-driven |
| **Best for** | Monitoring, diagnosis, planning | Query answering, theorem proving |
| **Trigger** | New facts available | Sub-goal matching |

Forward chaining implements **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** and is naturally suited for applications like real-time expert systems and reactive agents.

*(Mark distribution: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for comparison)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q. (a)** Define unification. Explain the unification algorithm with a suitable example. Discuss the significance of the **occurs check** with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Unification is the process of finding a substitution θ that, when applied to two FOL expressions, makes them syntactically equal. It returns the Most General Unifier (MGU).

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. If both arguments are identical constants → return empty substitution.
2. If one argument is a variable `v` and the other is a term `t` (with `v` NOT occurring in `t`) → return `{v/t}`.
3. If both are compound terms with the same predicate and arity → recursively unify argument pairs and compose substitutions.
4. Otherwise → FAILURE.

**Example (1 mark):** UNIFY(`Knows(John, x)`, `Knows(y, Mary)`) returns θ = `{x/Mary, y/John}`.

**Occurs Check (1 mark):** Occurs check prevents substituting a variable with a term that contains that variable itself. Example: Unifying `Loves(x, x)` with `Loves(y, F(y))` fails because `y` occurs inside `F(y)`. Without this check, infinite substitution loops occur.

**(b)** Consider the following knowledge base: **(7 marks)**

```
Facts:  Cat(Tom), Cat(Luna), Dog(Bruno), Fast(Tom), Likes(Mary, Tom)
Rules:  
  R1: Cat(x) ∧ Fast(x) → GoodPet(x)
  R2: GoodPet(x) → Likes(Mary, x)
  R3: Likes(Mary, x) ∧ Dog(y) → Walk(Mary, y, x)
```

**Apply forward chaining to determine if `Likes(Mary, Tom)` and `Walk(Mary, Bruno, Tom)` can be derived. Show each iteration clearly.**

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1: Triggering R1**
- Try unify `Cat(x)` with `Cat(Tom)` → θ = {x/Tom}
- Check `Fast(x)θ = Fast(Tom)` ∈ Facts ✓
- Apply GMP: Derive `GoodPet(Tom)`
- Add `GoodPet(Tom)` to facts
- Try `Cat(Luna)` → needs `Fast(Luna)` which is absent ✗

**Iteration 2: Triggering R2**
- Unify `GoodPet(x)` with `GoodPet(Tom)` → θ = {x/Tom}
- Derive `Likes(Mary, Tom)` ✓ (Already in KB, confirmed)
- Add to facts

**Iteration 3: Triggering R3**
- Unify `Likes(Mary, x)` with `Likes(Mary, Tom)` → θ = {x/Tom}
- Unify `Dog(y)` with `Dog(Bruno)` → θ₂ = {y/Bruno}
- Compose: θ = {x/Tom, y/Bruno}
- Derive: `Walk(Mary, Bruno, Tom)` ✓

**Final Result:** Both `Likes(Mary, Tom)` (already known) and `Walk(Mary, Bruno, Tom)` (newly derived) are entailed.

**Trace Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Iter | Rule | Substitution θ | New Fact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | R1 | {x/Tom} | GoodPet(Tom) |
| 2 | R2 | {x/Tom} | Likes(Mary, Tom) |
| 3 | R3 | {x/Tom, y/Bruno} | Walk(Mary, Bruno, Tom) |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** What are **lifts** in First-Order Logic? Explain the **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** rule with an example. Why is GMP preferred over plain Modus Ponens? **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Lifts Definition (2 marks):** A lift is the transformation of a propositional logic inference rule into its first-order logic equivalent by introducing variables and quantifiers. Common lifts include GMP (lift of Modus Ponens), Generalized Modus Tollens (lift of Modus Tollens), and Generalized Resolution (lift of Resolution).

**Generalized Modus Ponens (3 marks):**

GMP states: For atomic sentences `p₁', p₂', …, pₙ'` and a rule `p₁ ∧ p₂ ∧ … ∧ pₙ → q`, if there exists a substitution θ such that `UNIFY(pᵢ, pᵢ')` succeeds for all i, then the conclusion `qθ` can be derived.

Formal statement:

$$\frac{p_1',\ p_2',\ \ldots,\ p_n',\quad (p_1 \wedge p_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge p_n \rightarrow q)}{\therefore q\theta}$$

where θ is the MGU making all premises unify.

**Example (1 mark):**
- Rule: `King(x) ∧ Greedy(x) → Evil(x)`
- Facts: `King(John)`, `Greedy(John)`
- Unifier: θ = `{x/John}`
- Consequence: `Evil(John)`

**Why GMP Preferred (1 mark):** Unlike propositional Modus Ponens, GMP handles variables and unifies patterns in a single step. It scales to infinite domains without enumerating ground instances and is the foundation of efficient FOL inference in Prolog and production systems.

**(b)** Consider the following FOL knowledge base: **(7 marks)**

```
Facts:  Student(Ravi), Student(Priya), Knows(Ravi, Math), Knows(Priya, Science)
Rules:
  R1: Student(x) → Person(x)
  R2: Person(x) ∧ Knows(x, y) → Expert(x, y)
  R3: Expert(x, Math) → CanTeach(x, Algebra)
  R4: Expert(x, Science) → CanTeach(x, Biology)
```

**Use forward chaining to derive all possible conclusions. Show each iteration with the substitution used.**

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1: Fire R1**
- Unify `Student(x)` with `Student(Ravi)` → θ = {x/Ravi}
- Derive: `Person(Ravi)` ✓ Add to facts
- Unify `Student(x)` with `Student(Priya)` → θ = {x/Priya}
- Derive: `Person(Priya)` ✓ Add to facts

**Iteration 2: Fire R2**
- Unify `Person(x) ∧ Knows(x, y)`:
  - Match `Person(Ravi)` with `Person(x)` → θ₁ = {x/Ravi}
  - Apply: `Knows(Ravi, y)` matches `Knows(Ravi, Math)` → θ₂ = {y/Math}
  - Composed: θ = {x/Ravi, y/Math}
  - Derive: `Expert(Ravi, Math)` ✓ Add
- Match `Person(Priya)` with `Person(x)` → θ₁ = {x/Priya}
  - Apply: `Knows(Priya, y)` matches `Knows(Priya, Science)` → θ₂ = {y/Science}
  - Composed: θ = {x/Priya, y/Science}
  - Derive: `Expert(Priya, Science)` ✓ Add

**Iteration 3: Fire R3**
- Unify `Expert(x, Math)` with `Expert(Ravi, Math)` → θ = {x/Ravi}
- Derive: `CanTeach(Ravi, Algebra)` ✓

**Iteration 4: Fire R4**
- Unify `Expert(x, Science)` with `Expert(Priya, Science)` → θ = {x/Priya}
- Derive: `CanTeach(Priya, Biology)` ✓

**Termination:** No more applicable rules.

**Final Derived Facts Set:**

| Iteration | Rule | Substitution θ | New Fact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1a | R1 | {x/Ravi} | Person(Ravi) |
| 1b | R1 | {x/Priya} | Person(Priya) |
| 2a | R2 | {x/Ravi, y/Math} | Expert(Ravi, Math) |
| 2b | R2 | {x/Priya, y/Science} | Expert(Priya, Science) |
| 3 | R3 | {x/Ravi} | CanTeach(Ravi, Algebra) |
| 4 | R4 | {x/Priya} | CanTeach(Priya, Biology) |

**Total conclusions derived: 6 new facts.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the Occurs Check (–2 marks):** Many students unify `x` with `F(x)` without mentioning the occurs check failure. Always state: *"Occurs(x, F(x)) = TRUE → FAIL."*
>
> 2. **Not showing substitution composition (–1.5 marks):** KTU examiners expect explicit `θ = θ₂ ∘ θ₁` notation when multiple unification steps occur.
>
> 3. **Confusing Forward vs Backward Chaining direction (–2 marks):** Forward chaining ALWAYS starts from FACTS, not goals. Writing "we assume the goal is true and derive sub-goals" = backward chaining description. This is a major marker pitfall.
>
> 4. **Not showing MGU uniqueness check (–1 mark):** When asked for MGU, mention it is unique up to variable renaming.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to compose substitutions in chained unifications (–1 mark):** In multi-step forward chaining, when the same variable appears in multiple rules, always show the COMPOSED θ across all premises.
>
> 6. **Missing trace table (–1 mark):** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners reward a clean **iteration trace table** — it saves examiner time and earns full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Unification** = finding θ making two FOL expressions identical via pattern matching.
>
> ✅ **Substitution θ** = mapping variables → terms; written as `{x/A, y/B}`.
>
> ✅ **UNIFY(α, β)** = returns the MGU (Most General Unifier) or FAILURE.
>
> ✅ **MGU** is unique up to variable renaming — the "most general" = fewest commitments.
>
> ✅ **Occurs Check** is mandatory — prevents infinite substitution loops (e.g., unifying `x` with `Knows(x, y)`).
>
> ✅ **Lifts** convert propositional rules to first-order by introducing variables and quantifiers.
>
> ✅ **GMP (Generalized Modus Ponens)** is the lifted version of Modus Ponens using substitution θ.
>
> ✅ **Forward Chaining** = data-driven reasoning: Facts → apply rules → derive new facts → repeat.
>
> ✅ Forward Chaining terminates when no new facts are added OR the **goal query** is in the fact set.
>
> ✅ Forward Chaining in FOL is **semi-decidable** (may not halt if goal is not entailed).
>
> ✅ Algorithm complexity for unification is **exponential** in worst case — important for theoretical questions.
>
> ✅ Every KTU 14-mark answer on this topic **must include**: (i) clear problem statement, (ii) iteration-by-iteration trace, (iii) explicit θ at each step, (iv) final fact set, (v) conclusion stating entailment success/failure.
>
> ✅ Bloom's Taxonomy levels typically tested: **L3 (Apply)** for unification, **L4 (Analyze)** for forward chaining traces.
>
> ✅ Common exam verbs: *"Unify"*, *"Find the MGU"*, *"Apply forward chaining to derive"*, *"Show the substitution used"*, *"Determine if the query is entailed"*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backward Chaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backward Chaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backward Chaining** is a *goal-driven* (or *query-driven*) inference strategy used in knowledge-based systems and logic programming. The reasoning engine begins with a hypothesized **goal (conclusion)** and works *backwards* through the knowledge base (a set of **IF-THEN rules** or **Horn clauses**), attempting to verify the goal by recursively proving the premises (antecedents) of any rule whose **head (consequent)** matches the goal. The process continues until all sub-goals are either proven from **known facts** or are determined to be unprovable.

In formal logic terms, given a rule of the form:

$$
\text{IF } P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \text{ THEN } Q
$$

Backward chaining starts by trying to prove **Q** (the consequent), and to do so, it must prove all the **Pᵢ** (the antecedents). This is in stark contrast to **Forward Chaining**, which starts with known facts and derives new conclusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Backward Chaining is a core component of *Module 3: Reasoning under Uncertainty / Knowledge Representation and Reasoning*, typically studied alongside forward chaining, resolution, and unification in logic-based expert systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical detective** trying to determine if a patient has *Disease X*.

| Step | Detective's Action | Backward Chaining Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | "Could this patient have Disease X?" | Set **goal = DiseaseX** |
| 2 | "I know: IF (Fever AND Cough) THEN DiseaseX" | Find rule whose head matches goal |
| 3 | "Does the patient have Fever? Does he have Cough?" | Generate **sub-goals**: Fever, Cough |
| 4 | "Yes, he has Fever. Yes, he has Cough." | Match sub-goals to **known facts** |
| 5 | "Therefore, DiseaseX is CONFIRMED." | **Goal proven** ✓ |

**Another everyday analogy:** Think of backward chaining as **working backward from a recipe's final dish** to the ingredients. You start with the dish (goal), ask "what do I need to make it?" (premises), and recursively ask "what do I need to make *that*?" until you reach the basic ingredients (facts you already have).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following bolded terms are **standard KTU 2024 metrics** you must know:

- **Horn Clause**: A clause with *at most one positive literal*; the canonical form used in Prolog.
- **Unification**: The algorithmic process of making two logical expressions identical by finding a **substitution (θ)**.
- **Backtracking**: When a chosen rule fails to prove a sub-goal, the engine *reverses* its choice and tries an alternative rule.
- **Depth-First Search (DFS)**: Backward chaining uses DFS to traverse the rule space.
- **Goal Stack / Agenda**: A memory structure holding pending sub-goals to be resolved.
- **CLOSED-WORLD ASSUMPTION (CWA)**: Anything not provable from the knowledge base is assumed to be **FALSE**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The CWA is the *default semantic* of backward chaining systems like Prolog. Always mention it in descriptive answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The backward chaining engine executes the following structured logic:

1. **Goal Initialization:** Place the *query* (goal) on top of the **goal stack**.
2. **Rule Retrieval:** Search the rule base for every rule whose **consequent (head)** *unifies* with the current goal.
3. **Sub-Goal Generation:** For each matched rule, push the rule's *antecedents* (premises) as new sub-goals onto the stack.
4. **Fact Matching (Leaf Test):** If a sub-goal matches a *known fact* in the working memory → it is **proven** (success).
5. **Recursive Descent:** If a sub-goal does not match a fact, treat it as a *new goal* and recurse into Step 2.
6. **AND-Semantics:** All premises of a rule must be proven for the rule's conclusion to hold (logical AND).
7. **Backtracking Mechanism:** If ANY sub-goal fails → discard that rule's proof, **pop the stack**, and try the next matching rule.
8. **Termination:** The process terminates when (a) the top-level goal is proven → **YES**, or (b) all rules are exhausted → **NO**.

## 2.2 Formal Properties of Backward Chaining

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Backward"?** Because the logical flow of reasoning goes from the *right-hand side* of an implication (Q) toward the *left-hand side* (P₁ ∧ P₂ ∧ ... ∧ Pₙ), which is the *opposite* direction of the rule's written form.

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Reasoning Direction** | Goal-driven (conclusion → premises) |
| **Search Strategy** | **Depth-First Search (DFS)** with backtracking |
| **Rule Form** | **Horn Clauses** (single head) |
| **Termination** | Guaranteed for finite, acyclic knowledge bases |
| **Completeness** | Complete for Horn-clause logic (refutation-complete via SLD-resolution) |
| **Soundness** | Yes — derived goals are logically entailed |
| **Best Suited For** | *Diagnostic* tasks (medical, fault-finding, debugging) |
| **Not Suited For** | Tasks requiring many derived intermediate facts (use Forward Chaining instead) |
| **Memory Footprint** | Low (only the goal stack is maintained) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ in worst case, where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = proof depth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** Backward chaining is **NOT complete for full first-order logic** — only for the **Horn-clause subset**. This is precisely *why* Prolog restricts programs to Horn clauses.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ (theta) | A **substitution** (e.g., $\{x/Mary, y/John\}$) | Unification |
| $\sigma$ (sigma) | A *most general unifier* (MGU) | Unification |
| $\models$ | "Entails" / "logically follows from" | Logical consequence |
| $\leftarrow$ | Prolog's "if" operator (reversed implication) | Horn clauses |
| $\land$ | Logical AND (conjunction) | Rule premises |
| $\neg$ or $\lnot$ | Logical NOT (negation) | Literals |
| $\emptyset$ | Empty substitution (identity) | Unification |
| $K\!B$ | Knowledge Base | Inference |
| $\text{FAIL}$ | Top-level goal cannot be proven | Termination |

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Backward chaining is the **reasoning backbone** of several mission-critical engineering systems:

- **MYCIN & Modern CDSS (Clinical Decision Support Systems):** Backward-chains through a differential diagnosis tree.
- **Prolog Compilers (SWI-Prolog, GNU Prolog):** Entire execution model is SLD-resolution (a form of backward chaining).
- **CLIPS (NASA's expert system shell):** Used in spacecraft fault diagnosis via goal-driven rules.
- **Automated Theorem Provers (ATP):** Goal-directed proof search.
- **Legal Expert Systems:** Backward-chain over statutes to determine liability.
- **Cybersecurity Triage Tools:** Backward-chain from "is this an attack?" to indicators of compromise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Complete Backward Chaining Trace

This is a **classic KTU-style problem**. We will trace a backward-chaining proof **step by step** with no shortcuts.

### 3.1.1 The Knowledge Base (KB)

**Rules (Horn Clauses):**
1. $\text{Disease}(X) \leftarrow \text{Symptom}(X, \text{fever}) \land \text{Symptom}(X, \text{cough})$
2. $\text{Symptom}(X, \text{fever}) \leftarrow \text{Has}(X, \text{high\_temp})$
3. $\text{Has}(X, \text{high\_temp}) \leftarrow \text{Reading}(X, T) \land T > 100$

**Facts (Working Memory):**
- $\text{Reading}(\text{Alice}, 102)$
- $\text{Reading}(\text{Bob}, 98)$

### 3.1.2 The Goal (Query)

**Prove:** $\text{Disease}(\text{Alice})$ — *Does Alice have the disease?*

### 3.1.3 Detailed Trace

**Step 1 — Goal Stack Initialization**
The top-level goal is placed on the stack:

$$
\text{Goal Stack: } [\text{Disease}(\text{Alice})]
$$

**Step 2 — Match Goal to Rule Heads**

We scan the KB for any rule whose **head unifies** with $\text{Disease}(\text{Alice})$. Only **Rule 1** matches:

$$
\text{Disease}(X) \leftarrow \text{Symptom}(X, \text{fever}) \land \text{Symptom}(X, \text{cough})
$$

Unification: $\text{Disease}(\text{Alice}) \equiv \text{Disease}(X)$, yielding the substitution:

$$
\theta_1 = \{X/\text{Alice}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Generate Sub-Goals (Apply $\theta_1$ to premises)**

The rule's premises, after applying $\theta_1$, become new sub-goals:

$$
\text{Sub-goals: } \{\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{fever}), \ \text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{cough})\}
$$

Push them onto the stack:

$$
\text{Goal Stack: } [\text{Disease}(\text{Alice}), \ \text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{cough}), \ \text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{fever})]
$$

> **Processing Order:** In Prolog-style backward chaining, the **rightmost** premise is processed first (LIFO stack). So we process $\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{fever})$ first.

**Step 4 — Prove Sub-Goal $\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{fever})$**

Scan rule heads for a match. **Rule 2** matches:

$$
\text{Symptom}(X, \text{fever}) \leftarrow \text{Has}(X, \text{high\_temp})
$$

Unification gives $\theta_2 = \{X/\text{Alice}\}$, generating the new sub-goal:

$$
\text{Sub-goal: } \text{Has}(\text{Alice}, \text{high\_temp})
$$

**Step 5 — Prove Sub-Goal $\text{Has}(\text{Alice}, \text{high\_temp})$**

**Rule 3** matches:

$$
\text{Has}(X, \text{high\_temp}) \leftarrow \text{Reading}(X, T) \land T > 100
$$

Unification gives $\theta_3 = \{X/\text{Alice}\}$, generating:

$$
\text{Sub-goals: } \{\text{Reading}(\text{Alice}, T),\ T > 100\}
$$

**Step 6 — Fact Matching (Leaf Test)**

We have the fact $\text{Reading}(\text{Alice}, 102)$ in the KB. Unification yields:

$$
\theta_4 = \{T/102\}
$$

Now evaluate the numeric condition $T > 100$ with $T = 102$:

$$
102 > 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 7 — Back-Propagate Success**

The compound sub-goal succeeds. Therefore:
- $\text{Has}(\text{Alice}, \text{high\_temp})$ is **PROVEN** ✓
- $\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{fever})$ is **PROVEN** ✓
- Next, evaluate the second premise of Rule 1: $\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{cough})$

**Step 8 — Try to Prove $\text{Symptom}(\text{Alice}, \text{cough})$**

No rule in the KB has a head matching $\text{Symptom}(X, \text{cough})$. No fact matches either. → **FAIL**.

**Step 9 — Backtrack**

Per the AND-semantics of Rule 1, *both* premises must be proven. Since one failed, Rule 1 fails for Alice. No other rule has $\text{Disease}$ in its head. → **Top-level goal FAILS**.

**Step 10 — Final Answer**

$$
\text{Backward Chaining Result: } K\!B \not\models \text{Disease}(\text{Alice})
$$

The system returns **NO** (cannot prove).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl:** Many students skip **Step 9 (backtracking)** in their answer scripts. The backtrack step is *the* defining feature of backward chaining — always show it explicitly to secure full marks.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Backward vs. Forward Chaining

| Criterion | **Backward Chaining** | **Forward Chaining** |
|---|---|---|
| Starting point | **Goal / Hypothesis** | **Known Facts** |
| Direction | Consequent → Antecedents | Antecedents → Consequent |
| Also called | *Goal-driven*, *query-driven* | *Data-driven*, *antecedent-driven* |
| Search type | **Depth-First** (with backtracking) | **Breadth-First** (forward propagation) |
| Best for | **Diagnostic** problems | **Inference** / discovery problems |
| Output | A **proof tree** (or failure) | New **derived facts** added to KB |
| Typical use | Prolog, MYCIN | CLIPS, production systems |
| Triggers | Goal query | New fact insertion |
| Completeness | Horn-clause complete | Horn-clause complete |
| Cyclic KBs | May loop (requires cycle detection) | May also loop (requires fixed-point) |

### 3.3 Mathematical Foundation — Unification Algorithm

Backward chaining depends fundamentally on **unification**. The recursive unification function $\text{unify}(L_1, L_2)$ returns either a most general unifier (MGU) $\sigma$ or **FAIL**.

$$
\text{unify}(L_1, L_2) = \begin{cases} \emptyset & \text{if } L_1 = L_2 \text{ (identical)} \\ \{V/t\} \cup \theta & \text{if } V \in \text{vars}(L_1),\ V \notin \text{vars}(t) \\ \text{FAIL} & \text{otherwise (occurs check fails)} \end{cases}
$$

where $V$ is a variable in $L_1$ and $t$ is the term it must unify with in $L_2$. The **occurs check** (ensuring $V$ is not nested inside $t$) guarantees termination of unification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Standard Prolog implementations *omit the occurs check* for efficiency, which can cause infinite loops on pathological inputs. Mention this if asked about Prolog's limitations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Control Flow of Backward Chaining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User Submits Goal G] --> B{Push G onto Goal Stack}
    B --> C{Pop Top Sub-Goal S from Stack}
    C --> D{Does S match a Known Fact in Working Memory?}
    D -->|YES| E[Mark S as PROVEN]
    D -->|NO| F{Scan Rule Base: Any rule with head unifying with S?}
    F -->|NO MATCH| G[Mark S as FAILED]
    F -->|MATCH FOUND| H[Unify S with rule head, produce substitution theta]
    H --> I[Push rule premises onto Goal Stack in reverse order]
    I --> C
    E --> J{Is Stack Empty?}
    G --> J
    J -->|NO| C
    J -->|YES| K{Was Top-Level Goal Proven?}
    K -->|YES| L[Return YES with Proof Tree]
    K -->|NO| M[Return NO Backtrack Triggered]
```

## 4.2 AND-OR Proof Tree (Goal Decomposition)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["GOAL: Disease(Alice)"] --> nodeB1["Symptom(Alice, fever)"]
    nodeA --> nodeB2["Symptom(Alice, cough)"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Has(Alice, high_temp)"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1["Reading(Alice, T)"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2["T greater than 100"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1["FACT: Reading Alice 102"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeF1["EVAL: 102 greater than 100 = TRUE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeG1["NO RULE OR FACT MATCHES"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeH1["SUB-GOAL FAILS"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeI1["BACKTRACK: Rule 1 discarded for Alice"]
    nodeI1 --> nodeJ1["FINAL: Goal CANNOT be proven"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeK1["Has(Alice, high_temp) PROVEN"]
    nodeK1 --> nodeL1["Symptom(Alice, fever) PROVEN"]
```

## 4.3 Backward Chaining Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Interface Layer"]
        UI1[User Query: Is X True?]
    end
    subgraph INFERENCE["Inference Engine"]
        IE1[Goal Stack Manager]
        IE2[Unification Module]
        IE3[Backtracking Controller]
        IE4[Conflict Resolver]
    end
    subgraph KB["Knowledge Base"]
        KB1[Rule Base: IF-THEN Horn Clauses]
        KB2[Working Memory: Known Facts]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        OP1[YES with Proof Tree]
        OP2[NO with Failure Trace]
    end
    UI1 --> IE1
    IE1 --> IE2
    IE2 --> KB1
    IE2 --> KB2
    IE3 --> IE1
    IE4 --> IE2
    IE1 --> OP1
    IE1 --> OP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The four blocks inside the **Inference Engine** are the *core control components*. The **Goal Stack Manager** maintains the agenda; the **Unification Module** performs MGU computation; the **Backtracking Controller** reverses failed choices; and the **Conflict Resolver** decides which rule to fire when multiple match.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining in rule-based systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (conclusion → facts) |
| Starting point | Known facts in working memory | A goal/hypothesis to be proven |
| Search strategy | Breadth-first propagation | Depth-first with backtracking |
| Best suited to | Monitoring & inference tasks | Diagnosis & query-answering |
| Example system | CLIPS | Prolog, MYCIN |

Forward chaining derives new facts until no rule can fire, while backward chaining decomposes a goal into sub-goals until reducible to known facts.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define unification. Why is it essential for backward chaining?**

**Model Answer:**
**Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. It is *essential* for backward chaining because the engine must match each goal (or sub-goal) against the **head of a rule** in the KB. Without unification, the engine could not determine which rule to invoke, and the variable bindings needed to instantiate premises could not be propagated. The most general unifier (MGU) is the canonical substitution returned by the algorithm.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the backward chaining inference algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **goal stack** and **backtracking**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following knowledge base. Using backward chaining, prove or disprove the query: **"Is John a grandfather?"** Show the complete trace. **(7 Marks)**

**Rules:**

1. $\text{Grandfather}(X) \leftarrow \text{Father}(X, Y) \land \text{Parent}(Y, Z)$
2. $\text{Parent}(X, Y) \leftarrow \text{Father}(X, Y)$
3. $\text{Parent}(X, Y) \leftarrow \text{Mother}(X, Y)$

**Facts:**

- $\text{Father}(\text{John}, \text{Mary})$
- $\text{Father}(\text{Mary}, \text{Sam})$

#### Model Solution to (a):

The backward chaining algorithm operates as follows:

1. **Input:** A goal $G$ to be proven.
2. **Goal Stack:** A LIFO data structure. The initial goal $G$ is pushed. When a rule matches, its premises are pushed in *reverse* order so the leftmost is processed first (Prolog convention) — or rightmost first (some textbooks). The stack is dynamically updated as sub-goals succeed or fail.
3. **Backtracking:** When a chosen rule fails (any premise not provable), the engine **pops** the failed sub-goal, **undoes** variable bindings made by that sub-proof, and tries the *next* candidate rule whose head unifies with the sub-goal. This is the "depth-first search with chronological backtracking" paradigm.
4. **Termination:** Algorithm halts when the stack is empty (success) or when all rules have been exhausted for the top-level goal (failure).

The **block diagram** (see Section 4.3 of these notes) illustrates these components: Goal Stack Manager ↔ Unification Module ↔ Rule Base ↔ Working Memory, with the Backtracking Controller coordinating the reversibility.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Grandfather}(\text{John})$.

**Step 1:** Match goal to Rule 1 head: $\text{Grandfather}(X)$ unifies with $\text{Grandfather}(\text{John})$ → $\theta_1 = \{X/\text{John}\}$. Generate sub-goals: $\text{Father}(\text{John}, Y)$ and $\text{Parent}(Y, Z)$.

**Step 2:** Sub-goal $\text{Father}(\text{John}, Y)$. Try fact $\text{Father}(\text{John}, \text{Mary})$ → MATCH with $\theta_2 = \{Y/\text{Mary}\}$. **PROVEN** ✓

**Step 3:** Sub-goal $\text{Parent}(\text{Mary}, Z)$. Try Rule 2: $\text{Parent}(X, Y) \leftarrow \text{Father}(X, Y)$. Unify $\text{Parent}(\text{Mary}, Z)$ with head → $\{X/\text{Mary}\}$. New sub-goal: $\text{Father}(\text{Mary}, Z)$.

**Step 4:** Sub-goal $\text{Father}(\text{Mary}, Z)$. Try fact $\text{Father}(\text{Mary}, \text{Sam})$ → MATCH with $\theta_4 = \{Z/\text{Sam}\}$. **PROVEN** ✓

**Step 5:** All premises of Rule 1 satisfied under combined substitution $\theta_1 \cup \theta_2 \cup \theta_4 = \{X/\text{John}, Y/\text{Mary}, Z/\text{Sam}\}$.

**Step 6:** Top-level goal proven.

**Result:** $\boxed{K\!B \models \text{Grandfather}(\text{John})}$ — **YES**, John is a grandfather.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]` (Alternative)

**(a)** What are **Horn clauses**? Explain why backward chaining is restricted to Horn-clause knowledge bases, with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the KB below, use backward chaining to determine whether **"Bunny is a mammal"** holds. Provide a step-by-step trace. **(7 Marks)**

**Rules:**

1. $\text{Mammal}(X) \leftarrow \text{Has\_Fur}(X)$
2. $\text{Has\_Fur}(X) \leftarrow \text{Animal}(X) \land \text{Warm\_Blooded}(X)$
3. $\text{Warm\_Blooded}(X) \leftarrow \text{Vertebrate}(X)$

**Facts:**

- $\text{Animal}(\text{Bunny})$
- $\text{Vertebrate}(\text{Bunny})$

#### Model Solution to (a):

A **Horn clause** is a disjunction of literals containing *at most one positive literal*:

$$
\neg P_1 \lor \neg P_2 \lor \dots \lor \neg P_n \lor Q
$$

Equivalently (in Prolog form):

$$
Q \leftarrow P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n
$$

Backward chaining is restricted to Horn clauses because each rule has **exactly one head** ($Q$). This means when the engine matches a goal to a rule head, there is *no ambiguity* about what consequent to derive — the engine knows it is trying to prove $Q$, and the sub-goals are uniquely $P_1, \dots, P_n$. If rules had multiple positive literals (e.g., $P \lor Q \leftarrow R$), backward chaining could not decide *which* consequent to chain on, breaking the goal-driven paradigm.

**Example:** $\text{Bird}(X) \leftarrow \text{Animal}(X) \land \text{Feathers}(X)$ is a valid Horn clause; $\text{Bird}(X) \lor \text{Fish}(X) \leftarrow \text{Animal}(X)$ is *not* a Horn clause (two positive literals).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Mammal}(\text{Bunny})$.

**Step 1:** Match to Rule 1 head. $\theta_1 = \{X/\text{Bunny}\}$. Sub-goal: $\text{Has\_Fur}(\text{Bunny})$.

**Step 2:** Match $\text{Has\_Fur}(\text{Bunny})$ to Rule 2 head. $\theta_2 = \{X/\text{Bunny}\}$. Sub-goals: $\text{Animal}(\text{Bunny})$ and $\text{Warm\_Blooded}(\text{Bunny})$.

**Step 3:** Sub-goal $\text{Animal}(\text{Bunny})$. Fact match ✓. **PROVEN**.

**Step 4:** Sub-goal $\text{Warm\_Blooded}(\text{Bunny})$. Match Rule 3 head. Sub-goal: $\text{Vertebrate}(\text{Bunny})$.

**Step 5:** Fact match $\text{Vertebrate}(\text{Bunny})$ ✓. **PROVEN**.

**Step 6:** All premises satisfied. **Mammal(Bunny) is PROVEN** ✓.

**Result:** $\boxed{\text{YES — Bunny is a mammal.}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to show the **substitution θ** at each unification step. Examiners specifically look for $\theta$ notation. Omitting it can cost **2–3 marks per question**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students confuse *backtracking* with *forward chaining failure handling*. Backward chaining backtracks *within the proof*; forward chaining re-fires different rules on new data. Wording matters!
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In AND-OR trees, the AND arc is between *premises* of the same rule, while the OR arc is between *different rules* with the same head. Mixing these up in diagrams is a frequent marker complaint.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to state the **Closed-World Assumption** explicitly in descriptive answers — examiners allocate marks for it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Backward chaining is a **goal-driven** inference strategy — it starts from the conclusion and works backward.
- ✅ It is the foundational reasoning mechanism of **Prolog** and diagnostic expert systems like **MYCIN**.
- ✅ Rules must be in **Horn-clause form** (at most one positive literal) for backward chaining to be sound.
- ✅ The engine uses **Depth-First Search (DFS)** with chronological **backtracking**.
- ✅ The core algorithmic primitive is **unification** — always show the substitution $\theta$ in your answers.
- ✅ The **Closed-World Assumption (CWA)** applies: anything not derivable is assumed **FALSE**.
- ✅ Best suited for **diagnostic / classification** tasks; not ideal for tasks with many intermediate derivations.
- ✅ Sound and complete *for Horn-clause logic*, but **incomplete for full first-order logic**.
- ✅ Complexity is $O(b^d)$ in the worst case — proof depth $d$ matters.
- ✅ Key components: **Goal Stack**, **Unification Module**, **Backtracking Controller**, **Conflict Resolver**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Exam Mantra:** *"Backward chaining = Goal → Sub-Goals → Facts, using Horn clauses, DFS, and backtracking, with unification at every step."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning: Learning from Rewards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning: Learning from Rewards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an **intelligent agent** learns an optimal **policy** of actions by **interacting with a dynamic environment** and receiving **scalar reward signals** (positive or negative feedback) that quantify the quality of each transition, with the explicit objective of **maximizing the cumulative long-term reward** (the expected return).

This formulation is canonically expressed as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$, where:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — finite (or continuous) **state space**.
- $\mathcal{A}$ — finite **action space**.
- $P(s' \mid s,a)$ — **transition probability function** giving the probability of moving to state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$.
- $R(s,a,s')$ — **reward function** yielding the immediate reward for the transition $(s, a, s')$.
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ — **discount factor** controlling the present value of future rewards.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — *Training a Pet*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Teaching a Dog New Tricks**
> Imagine you are teaching a dog (*the agent*) to fetch a ball inside a living room (*the environment*). Every time the dog performs a desirable behavior (sits, runs toward the ball, brings it back), you hand it a treat (*positive reward*). When it chews the furniture, you say "No" (*negative reward / penalty*). The dog does **not** receive a labeled dataset telling it *exactly* what to do at every moment; it merely explores, observes the consequences, and gradually builds an internal **strategy (policy)** that maps situations ("I see the ball") to actions ("run toward it"). Over thousands of repetitions, the dog converges to a behavior that reliably produces treats. This closed-loop, trial-and-error learning driven by **reward maximization** is precisely Reinforcement Learning.

## 1.3 The Five Foundational Elements of RL

> [!NOTE]
> Any RL system is fully characterized by the following five interacting elements. KTU questions frequently test student recall of these primitives — memorize them precisely.

| # | Element | Symbol | Role |
|---|---------|--------|------|
| 1 | **Agent** | — | The decision-maker / learner |
| 2 | **Environment** | $E$ | Everything external to the agent |
| 3 | **State** | $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ | Snapshot of the world at time $t$ |
| 4 | **Action** | $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$ | Move chosen by the agent at time $t$ |
| 5 | **Reward** | $r_t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Scalar feedback signal at time $t$ |

The **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ — a probability distribution over actions given a state — is what the agent ultimately learns. A policy may be **deterministic** ($a = \pi(s)$) or **stochastic** ($\pi(a \mid s)$).

## 1.4 Distinguishing RL from Supervised and Unsupervised Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RL is the **third pillar** of Machine Learning and is fundamentally different from the other two:
> - **Supervised Learning** — the learner receives *labeled input–output pairs* from a teacher. No sequential decision-making.
> - **Unsupervised Learning** — the learner discovers *hidden structure* (clusters, manifolds) in unlabeled data. No feedback signal.
> - **Reinforcement Learning** — the learner receives *delayed, scalar feedback* on its own actions and must balance exploration with exploitation. The data distribution itself depends on the agent's behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The operational heart of RL is the **sequential decision-making loop**:

1. At time step $t$, the environment is in state $s_t$.
2. The agent observes $s_t$ and selects an action $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s_t)$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$ according to $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.
4. The environment emits a reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.
5. The agent updates its policy/value estimates and the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> This loop produces a **trajectory** (or *episode* / *rollout*) of the form:
> $$\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, s_2, \dots)$$

## 2.2 The Return — Formal Definition of Cumulative Reward

The agent's objective is to maximize the **discounted return** $G_t$:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} \, r_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$$

**Why discount?** $\gamma < 1$ ensures the infinite sum converges mathematically, and it encodes a preference for *earlier* rewards over *later* rewards — modeling realistic human/animal preference for immediate gratification and avoiding mathematical divergence in cyclical environments.

## 2.3 Value Functions — The Core Quantities RL Learns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State-Value Function** $V^\pi(s)$ — the expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:
> $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \,\right]$$
>
> **Action-Value Function** $Q^\pi(s,a)$ — the expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$:
> $$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t = s,\; a_t = a \,\right]$$

The relationship between them is:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^{\pi}(s,a)$$

## 2.4 The Bellman Equations — Recursive Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V^\pi$ expresses the value recursively:
> $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]$$
>
> Similarly for $Q^\pi$:
> $$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \,\right]$$

The **Bellman Optimality Equation** characterizes the *optimal* value function $V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$:

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \,\right]$$

And equivalently:
$$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right]$$

## 2.5 Exploration vs Exploitation — The Fundamental Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Exploitation** — Choose the action believed to yield the highest expected reward based on current knowledge (greedy w.r.t. $Q$).
> - **Exploration** — Choose a non-greedy action to gather new information about the environment.
>
> A purely greedy agent gets stuck in suboptimal policies; a purely random agent never capitalizes on learning. The **$\epsilon$-greedy** strategy is the classical remedy: with probability $\epsilon$ act randomly, otherwise act greedily. $\epsilon$ is typically decayed over training (e.g., from $0.1$ down to $0.01$).

## 2.6 Model-Free vs Model-Based RL

| Paradigm | Description | Example Algorithm |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Model-Free** | Agent learns value/policy directly from experience; no transition model required. | Q-Learning, SARSA |
| **Model-Based** | Agent first (or simultaneously) learns a model $P(s' \mid s,a)$, then plans using it. | Dyna-Q, AlphaZero |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Commit these to memory.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted return | Dimensionless; $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 2 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s]$ | State-value under $\pi$ | Same units as $r$ |
| 3 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a]$ | Action-value under $\pi$ | Same units as $r$ |
| 4 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\,Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | Value ↔ Q-value relation | Probability-weighted sum |
| 5 | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Bellman Optimality for V | Self-consistency equation |
| 6 | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')]$ | Bellman Optimality for Q | Self-consistency equation |
| 7 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\right]$ | **Q-Learning update rule** | $\alpha$ = learning rate $\in (0,1]$ |
| 8 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\right]$ | **SARSA update rule** (on-policy) | Uses action actually taken next |
| 9 | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Optimal greedy policy | Deterministic extraction |
| 10 | $\text{TD-error}\; \delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | Temporal-Difference error | Drives value updates |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reinforcement Learning has transitioned from a theoretical curiosity into a transformative engineering tool with applications in:

- **Robotics** — locomotion, manipulation, dexterous grasping (e.g., OpenAI's Rubik's cube robot hand).
- **Autonomous Systems** — self-driving cars, drone navigation, warehouse logistics (Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Game Playing** — AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five (Dota 2), Deep Q-Networks on Atari.
- **Industrial Control** — HVAC optimization, chemical process control, semiconductor fabrication.
- **Finance** — portfolio allocation, algorithmic trading under transaction-cost constraints.
- **Recommender Systems** — sequential recommendation where user engagement is the reward.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi(s)$

**Goal:** Express $V^\pi(s)$ recursively in terms of $V^\pi(s')$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of $V^\pi(s)$.** By definition,
$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \,\right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the return using the Markov property.** The return decomposes as
$$G_t \;=\; r_{t+1} + \gamma\, r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}\, r_{t+3} + \dots \;=\; r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the first action and next state.** Summing over all actions $a$ weighted by $\pi(a \mid s)$ and all next states $s'$ weighted by $p(s' \mid s, a)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s,\; a_t = a,\; s_{t+1} = s' \,\right] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\!\left[\, \mathbb{E}[r_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'] + \gamma\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s'] \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Markov property to factor the expectation.** Because the future $G_{t+1}$ depends on the past only through $s_{t+1} = s'$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s'] \;=\; V^{\pi}(s')$$

And defining $\mathbb{E}[r_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'] = r$ as the reward function:

$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V^\pi$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^{*}(s,a)$

**Step 1 — Begin with the optimal action-value.** By definition,
$$Q^{*}(s,a) \;=\; \max_{\pi}\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s,\; a_t = a \,\right]$$

**Step 2 — Condition on the next state $s'$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s,a) &= \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_{t+1} = s' \,\right] \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — At the optimal policy, the future return is maximized.** The agent will choose the best action $a'$ in $s'$:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_{t+1} = s' \,\right]_{\text{optimal}} \;=\; \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and conclude.**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s,a) &= \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for $Q^*$. It is the contraction mapping that fixed-point iteration algorithms (e.g., **Value Iteration**) repeatedly apply until convergence. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Q-Learning Update Rule

**Step 1 — Recall the Bellman Optimality target.** The "true" target value of the $(s,a)$ pair should be:
$$Q^{\text{target}}(s,a) \;=\; r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$$

**Step 2 — Current estimate.** Our current estimate is $Q(s,a)$.

**Step 3 — Define the Temporal-Difference (TD) error.** The discrepancy between target and estimate is:
$$\delta_t \;=\; r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)$$

**Step 4 — Apply a stochastic gradient step.** Move the current estimate toward the target by a fraction $\alpha$:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q(s,a) &\leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\, \delta_t \\
&\leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a) \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convergence guarantee.** By the Robbins-Monro conditions ($\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$) and provided every $(s,a)$ pair is visited infinitely often, $Q(s,a) \to Q^{*}(s,a)$ as $t \to \infty$ (Watkins & Dayan, 1992). $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A 3-State Grid World

Consider a deterministic $3$-state MDP with discount $\gamma = 0.9$:

| State | Reward | Possible Next States |
|-------|--------|----------------------|
| $s_1$ | $+5$ (terminal) | — |
| $s_2$ | $0$ | $s_1$ or $s_3$ |
| $s_3$ | $+10$ (terminal) | — |

The agent can choose actions $\{$left, right$\}$. From $s_2$: left → $s_1$, right → $s_3$.

**Compute $Q^{*}(s_2, \text{right})$ in one shot:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s_2, \text{right}) &= r(s_2, \text{right}) + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_3, a') \\
&= 10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

(We treat $Q^{*}(s_{\text{terminal}}, \cdot) = 0$.)

**Compute $Q^{*}(s_2, \text{left})$:**

$$Q^{*}(s_2, \text{left}) = 5 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 5$$

**Optimal policy at $s_2$:** $\pi^{*}(s_2) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s_2, a) = $ **right** (since $10 > 5$).

This tiny example illustrates the entire RL inference pipeline: compute optimal Q-values using Bellman recursion, then extract the policy by greedy selection.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reinforcement Learning Feedback Loop (Agent–Environment Cycle)

This diagram captures the closed-loop interaction that defines every RL system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AGENT"] -- "Action a_t" --> B["ENVIRONMENT"]
    B -- "Reward r_t+1" --> A
    B -- "New State s_t+1" --> A
    A -- "Policy pi(a|s)" --> A
    A -- "Value V or Q updated" --> A

    classDef agentBox fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B45F06,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef envBox fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A agentBox
    class B envBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **AGENT** (orange box) consults its current policy to choose action $a_t$.
- The **ENVIRONMENT** (blue box) responds with both a new state $s_{t+1}$ and a scalar reward $r_{t+1}$.
- The agent uses this new $(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1})$ to update its internal value estimates $V$ or $Q$, which refines the policy. The cycle continues.

## 4.2 Markov Decision Process — Decision Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["State s_t"] -- "Action a_1 / pi" --> N1["Next State s_t+1_a1"]
    S1 -- "Action a_2 / pi" --> N2["Next State s_t+1_a2"]
    S1 -- "Action a_3 / pi" --> N3["Next State s_t+1_a3"]
    N1 -- "Reward r_a1" --> S1
    N2 -- "Reward r_a2" --> S1
    N3 -- "Reward r_a3" --> S1

    classDef stateBox fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nextBox fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S1 stateBox
    class N1 nextBox
    class N2 nextBox
    class N3 nextBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The current state $s_t$ (green) branches into the possible next states reachable by each action $a_i$.
- Each transition yields an associated reward $r_{a_i}$ that the agent experiences.
- This captures the **stochastic** nature of MDP transitions — even from the same $(s, a)$, multiple $s'$ are possible.

## 4.3 Q-Learning Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["STEP 1: Initialize Q table with zeros"] --> P2["STEP 2: Observe current state s"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: Choose action a using epsilon greedy"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: Execute action a in environment"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: Observe reward r and next state s prime"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6: Compute TD target r + gamma max Q s prime a prime"]
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7: Update Q s a using Q learning rule with learning rate alpha"]
    P7 --> P8{"STEP 8: Episode complete?"}
    P8 -- "No" --> P2
    P8 -- "Yes" --> P9["STEP 9: Decay epsilon and repeat for next episode"]
    P9 --> P2

    classDef procBox fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef checkBox fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P9 procBox
    class P8 checkBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The Q-Learning algorithm proceeds in **per-step updates inside per-episode loops**.
- After every transition $(s, a, r, s')$, the algorithm computes the **TD target** $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ and applies the update rule.
- $\epsilon$ is decayed once per episode to shift from exploration toward exploitation as training progresses.

## 4.4 Exploration vs Exploitation — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1{"STEP A: Generate random number u in 0 to 1"}
    D1 -- "u less than epsilon" --> D2["STEP B: EXPLORE - choose random action a"]
    D1 -- "u greater or equal epsilon" --> D3["STEP C: EXPLOIT - choose argmax Q s a"]
    D2 --> D4["STEP D: Execute chosen action and observe reward"]
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5["STEP E: Update Q table using observed transition"]
    E1["Decay epsilon over time"] -.-> D1

    classDef decBox fill:#F9CB9C,stroke:#CC4125,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actBox fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class D1 decBox
    class D2,D3,D4,D5,E1 actBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- At every state, a Bernoulli draw against $\epsilon$ decides whether to **explore** (random action) or **exploit** (greedy w.r.t. current $Q$).
- Over training, $\epsilon$ is annealed downward (dashed arrow), biasing the policy toward exploitation in the limit.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A.1 — 3 Marks
**Define Reinforcement Learning. List and briefly explain any four key elements of an RL system.**

**Model Answer:**
Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a machine learning paradigm in which an *agent* learns an optimal *policy* by *interacting* with an *environment* and receiving *reward* signals, with the goal of maximizing cumulative long-term reward.

**Four Key Elements:**
1. **Agent** — the learner / decision-maker that selects actions.
2. **Environment** — the external system with which the agent interacts.
3. **Reward Signal ($r$)** — scalar numerical feedback defining the goal.
4. **Policy ($\pi$)** — the agent's behavior mapping states to actions; what RL ultimately learns.

*(A fifth element — **Value Function** — may also be cited for full credit.)*

---

### Question A.2 — 3 Marks
**What is the exploration–exploitation trade-off in RL? How does the $\epsilon$-greedy strategy resolve it?**

**Model Answer:**
The **exploration–exploitation trade-off** is the dilemma an RL agent faces between:
- **Exploitation** — choosing the best-known action based on current knowledge (maximizes immediate reward), and
- **Exploration** — trying novel actions to discover potentially better long-term strategies (gains information).

Pure exploitation traps the agent in suboptimal policies; pure exploration wastes already-gathered information. The **$\epsilon$-greedy strategy** resolves this by selecting a random action with probability $\epsilon$ (exploration) and the greedy action (highest $Q$-value) with probability $1 - \epsilon$ (exploitation). Typically $\epsilon$ is annealed from a high initial value (e.g., $0.1$) toward near zero to gradually shift the agent toward exploitation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A — 14 Marks
**(a)** Explain the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework with its formal components. Clearly state the Bellman Optimality Equation for the state-value function $V^{*}(s)$ and derive it from first principles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Q-Learning algorithm in detail. Write down its update rule and explain each term. How does Q-Learning differ from SARSA in terms of on-policy vs off-policy behavior? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**MDP Framework:** An MDP is the canonical mathematical formalism for RL, defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $\mathcal{S}$ — finite set of states.
- $\mathcal{A}$ — finite set of actions.
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — transition probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via action $a$.
- $R(s, a, s')$ — expected immediate reward.
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ — discount factor.

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^{*}(s)$:**

By definition, the optimal state-value is the maximum expected return achievable from $s$:
$$V^{*}(s) \;=\; \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q^{*}(s,a)$$

Substituting the definition of $Q^{*}(s,a)$ and applying the one-step look-ahead decomposition:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s,a) &= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\pi^{*}}[\,G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s'\,] \,\right] \\
&= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \cdot V^{*}(s') \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Choosing the maximizing action:

$$V^{*}(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{*}(s') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$

This is a system of $|\mathcal{S}|$ non-linear equations that admits a unique fixed-point solution under the contraction mapping property.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Q-Learning Algorithm Description (off-policy TD control):**
- Initialize $Q(s,a) = 0$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}, a \in \mathcal{A}$.
- For each episode:
  - Observe initial state $s$.
  - Repeat for each step:
    - Choose $a$ from $s$ using $\epsilon$-greedy policy derived from $Q$.
    - Execute $a$, observe reward $r$ and next state $s'$.
    - Apply the **Q-Learning update rule**:
      $$Q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s, a) + \alpha\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a) \,\right]$$
    - Set $s \leftarrow s'$.
  - Until $s$ is terminal.

**Meaning of Each Term:**
| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $Q(s,a)$ | Current estimate of action-value |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate $\in (0, 1]$ |
| $r$ | Observed immediate reward |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor |
| $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ | Best future value estimate from $s'$ |
| $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)$ | Temporal-Difference error |

**Q-Learning vs SARSA:**

| Aspect | Q-Learning | SARSA |
|--------|------------|-------|
| Update target | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ | $r + \gamma Q(s', a')$ *(uses actually taken next action)* |
| Policy type | **Off-policy** (learns optimal policy while following exploratory one) | **On-policy** (learns the value of the policy actually being followed) |
| Convergence | $Q \to Q^{*}$ under standard conditions | $Q \to Q^{\pi}$ for the current behavior policy |

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question B — 14 Marks
**(a)** Explain the agent–environment interaction loop in Reinforcement Learning. Define the policy, return, state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$, and action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ with mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the Q-Learning algorithm. Apply **one iteration** of the Q-Learning update to the following MDP and compute the new $Q(s_1, a_2)$:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Current $Q(s_1, a_2)$ | $0.4$ |
| Reward $r$ observed | $1.0$ |
| Next state $s_2$ values: $Q(s_2, a_1) = 0.8$, $Q(s_2, a_2) = 0.5$, $Q(s_2, a_3) = 0.6$ |
| Learning rate $\alpha$ | $0.5$ |
| Discount factor $\gamma$ | $0.9$ |

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Agent–Environment Loop:**
The agent at time $t$ observes state $s_t$ from the environment, selects an action $a_t$ via its policy $\pi$, executes it, and receives reward $r_{t+1}$ along with next state $s_{t+1}$. This loop repeats indefinitely (or until a terminal state).

**Formal Definitions:**

1. **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$** — probability of choosing action $a$ in state $s$:
$$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \mathbb{P}[a_t = a \mid s_t = s]$$

2. **Return $G_t$** — cumulative discounted future reward:
$$G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, r_{t+k+1}, \qquad \gamma \in [0, 1)$$

3. **State-Value Function $V^{\pi}(s)$** — expected return starting from $s$ and following $\pi$:
$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \mid s_t = s \,\right]$$

4. **Action-Value Function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$** — expected return after taking action $a$ from $s$ and following $\pi$:
$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \mid s_t = s,\; a_t = a \,\right]$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Q-Learning Algorithm Diagram** — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the complete Mermaid flowchart.

**Numerical Computation — One Q-Learning Update:**

The Q-Learning update rule:
$$Q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s, a) + \alpha \!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \,\right]$$

**Step 1 — Identify next-state max Q-value.**
$$\max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = \max\{0.8,\; 0.5,\; 0.6\} = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Compute the TD target.**
$$\text{TD target} = r + \gamma \cdot \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = 1.0 + 0.9 \times 0.8 = 1.0 + 0.72 = 1.72$$

**Step 3 — Compute the TD error.**
$$\delta_t = 1.72 - Q(s_1, a_2) = 1.72 - 0.4 = 1.32$$

**Step 4 — Apply the update.**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) &= Q_{\text{old}}(s_1, a_2) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t \\
&= 0.4 + 0.5 \times 1.32 \\
&= 0.4 + 0.66 \\
&= \mathbf{1.06}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final answer:** $Q(s_1, a_2) = \boxed{1.06}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing on-policy vs off-policy** — Q-Learning is *off-policy* (uses $\max$), SARSA is *on-policy* (uses actual action). Examiners *explicitly* test this distinction. Memorize it cold.
> 2. **Forgetting to write the $\max$ operator** in the Q-Learning update — writing $Q(s', a')$ without $\max$ is a SARSA rule, not Q-Learning. This is the #1 mark-deduction error.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps in Bellman equation questions** — Show the one-step look-ahead decomposition $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ explicitly. Don't jump to the final form.
> 4. **Mixing up $V^{\pi}$ and $Q^{\pi}$ definitions** — $V$ is state-only; $Q$ is state-and-action. Examiners penalize this.
> 5. **Using $\gamma = 1$ without justification** — Always state that $\gamma \in [0,1)$ to ensure convergence.
> 6. **Numerical mistakes in Q-Learning updates** — Compute the TD target *first*, then subtract $Q_{\text{old}}$, then multiply by $\alpha$, then add. Mixing the order loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Not labeling units / dimensions** in numerical problems — $r$ and $Q$ are dimensionless reward magnitudes; $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ are unit-less fractions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reinforcement Learning: Learning from Rewards**

- ✅ RL is the **third paradigm** of ML — distinct from supervised and unsupervised.
- ✅ The core loop is: **Agent → Action → Environment → Reward + New State → Agent**.
- ✅ An **MDP** is the formal tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- ✅ **Return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, r_{t+k+1}$; $\gamma < 1$ ensures convergence.
- ✅ **State-Value** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s]$.
- ✅ **Action-Value** $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a]$.
- ✅ **Bellman Expectation Equation** expresses values recursively under a fixed policy.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality Equation** characterizes $V^{*}$ and $Q^{*}$; has a unique fixed-point solution.
- ✅ **Q-Learning update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **SARSA update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ Q-Learning = **off-policy**, SARSA = **on-policy** (HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams).
- ✅ **$\epsilon$-greedy** balances exploration vs exploitation; $\epsilon$ is annealed downward.
- ✅ **TD-error** $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ drives every temporal-difference update.
- ✅ Convergence requires all $(s, a)$ pairs to be visited infinitely often (and Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha$).
- ✅ RL applications span robotics, autonomous vehicles, game AI, recommender systems, and industrial control.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Q-Learning asks 'what's the BEST I could do?' SARSA asks 'what WILL I do next?'"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passive Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST522 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Passive Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Passive Reinforcement Learning (Passive RL)** is a category of *Reinforcement Learning* in which the agent's **policy $\pi$ is fixed** (given) and the agent's only task is to **evaluate** how good that policy is, i.e., to learn the **utility function $U^{\pi}(s)$** (or the state-value function) for every state $s$ under policy $\pi$. The agent **does not change** its action-selection strategy while learning.

The agent interacts with a stochastic environment modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP) $\langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$** and observes sequences of (state, reward, next state) tuples generated by following $\pi$.

Formally, the agent must learn to approximate:

$$U^{\pi}(s) = E\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t}\, R(S_{t}) \,\Big|\, \pi,\, S_{0}=s \right]$$

where:
- $S$ is the set of states,
- $A$ is the set of actions,
- $P(s'\vert s,a)$ is the transition probability,
- $R(s)$ is the reward function,
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):** *Passive* = policy is fixed (only **evaluation**). *Active* = policy is improved (evaluation + **control**). KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **junior doctor assigned to a senior doctor's ward**. The junior doctor is told: *"Watch and learn, do not change the treatment protocol."* As patients come in, the junior doctor observes:
- The patient's state (symptoms)
- The treatment the senior chose (policy $\pi$)
- The patient's outcome/recovery (reward)
- The next state (improvement, deterioration, or discharge)

After many patients, the junior doctor forms a mental model: *"When the protocol is followed, patients in state A usually recover fast, but in state B the recovery is slow."* This is exactly **passive RL** — the agent is **evaluating an existing policy** rather than inventing a new one.

## 1.3 Three Standard Approaches to Passive RL (Russell & Norvig Framework)

| # | Approach | Model Required? | Core Idea |
|---|----------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Direct Utility Estimation (DUE)** | No | Treat each state as independent; average observed total rewards from that state. |
| 2 | **Adaptive Dynamic Programming (ADP)** | Yes (learns $P$ and $R$) | Use the learned MDP model + Bellman equations to compute $U^{\pi}$. |
| 3 | **Temporal Difference (TD) Learning** | No (model-free) | Combine sampling with bootstrapping — update utility estimate from successor state's estimate. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists these three methods. Students should be prepared to **compare** them and write the **update equations** for each.

## 1.4 Notation & Standard Metrics

- **Discount factor $\gamma$** — usually a constant such as **0.9** or **0.99** (a bold assumption: future rewards are worth less than immediate ones).
- **Learning rate $\alpha$** — controls the step size in incremental updates, e.g., **0.1** to **0.5**.
- **Convergence criterion** — typically $\vert \Delta U \vert < 10^{-4}$ across all states.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the bolded constants: $\gamma$, $\alpha$, $\varepsilon$ (for active learning's exploration). They appear verbatim in KTU numericals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Passive RL Problem Setup

The agent observes a sequence of *trials* (or episodes) generated by following fixed $\pi$:

$$s_{0} \xrightarrow{r_{0}} s_{1} \xrightarrow{r_{1}} s_{2} \xrightarrow{r_{2}} \cdots$$

where $r_t = R(s_t)$ is the immediate reward received after visiting $s_t$. The observed **return-to-go** from $s_t$ is:

$$u_{t} = r_{t} + \gamma r_{t+1} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+2} + \cdots$$

The true utility is the expectation: $U^{\pi}(s_t) = E[u_t]$.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Approach

### A. Direct Utility Estimation (DUE) — "Naive Averaging"

- Treats each state visit as an **independent regression problem**.
- For each state $s$, maintains a running average of the **observed return** starting from $s$.
- **Why this fails partially:** It **ignores** the Markov property — it does not use the fact that utilities of neighboring states are related (Bellman consistency).

### B. Adaptive Dynamic Programming (ADP) — "Model-Based"

- The agent first **learns the transition model** $\hat{P}(s'\vert s,a)$ and reward model $\hat{R}(s)$ from samples.
- Then **solves the Bellman equation** for the given $\pi$:

$$U^{\pi}(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s, \pi(s))\, U^{\pi}(s')$$

- Typically solved by **iterative policy evaluation** (Gauss-Seidel or Jacobi sweeps) until convergence.
- **Strength:** Exploits Bellman constraints → **fastest convergence**.
- **Weakness:** Computationally expensive; needs to learn a model of size $O(\vert S\vert^{2} \vert A\vert)$.

### C. Temporal Difference (TD) Learning — "Model-Free Bootstrapping"

- The signature update rule (used at every transition $s \to s'$ with reward $r$):

$$U^{\pi}(s) \leftarrow U^{\pi}(s) + \alpha \left( r + \gamma\, U^{\pi}(s') - U^{\pi}(s) \right)$$

- The term $\left( r + \gamma U^{\pi}(s') - U^{\pi}(s) \right)$ is called the **TD error** (denoted $\delta_t$).
- **Why it works:** Combines *sampling* (uses actual observed $r, s'$) with *bootstrapping* (uses current estimate $U^{\pi}(s')$).
- **Convergence:** Guaranteed to converge to $U^{\pi}$ if $\alpha$ decays appropriately (e.g., $\alpha_t = 1/(1 + \text{visits}(s))$) and every state is visited infinitely often.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Use Case | Notes |
|---|---------|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | True utility under $\pi$ | $U^{\pi}(s) = E[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R(S_t) \vert \pi, S_0=s]$ | Definition | Infinite-horizon discounted return |
| 2 | Bellman equation for fixed $\pi$ | $U^{\pi}(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s,\pi(s))\, U^{\pi}(s')$ | ADP convergence target | Linear in $U$ for fixed $\pi$ |
| 3 | Sample-average estimate | $\widehat{U}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{k=1}^{N(s)} u^{(k)}(s)$ | DUE | $N(s)$ = number of trials starting at $s$ |
| 4 | Incremental DUE update | $\widehat{U}_{n}(s) = \widehat{U}_{n-1}(s) + \frac{1}{n}\bigl(u_n(s) - \widehat{U}_{n-1}(s)\bigr)$ | DUE online | Equivalent to running mean |
| 5 | Model estimate (transitions) | $\widehat{P}(s'\vert s,a) = \frac{N(s,a,s')}{N(s,a)}$ | ADP | Maximum-likelihood estimate |
| 6 | Model estimate (rewards) | $\widehat{R}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{k} r^{(k)}$ | ADP | Empirical mean reward |
| 7 | **TD(0) update rule** | $U(s) \leftarrow U(s) + \alpha\bigl(r + \gamma U(s') - U(s)\bigr)$ | TD learning | **Most-tested equation in KTU** |
| 8 | TD error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma U(s_{t+1}) - U(s_t)$ | TD analysis | Target minus prediction |
| 9 | $\alpha$ decay schedule | $\alpha_t(s) = \frac{1}{1 + N_t(s)}$ | Convergence proof | Robbins-Monro conditions |
| 10 | Passive RL objective | $\min \sum_s \bigl(\widehat{U}(s) - U^{\pi}(s)\bigr)^{2}$ | Evaluation | Not solved directly — sample-based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU board exams, equation **(7)** (TD update) is the single most frequently asked formula. Always state that $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** and $0 < \gamma < 1$ is the **discount factor**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics (locomotion):** A quadruped robot's policy is fixed by a controller; passive RL evaluates which states lead to falls.
- **Recommender systems:** A baseline recommendation policy is in production; passive RL estimates long-term user engagement.
- **Autonomous driving simulation:** Evaluating the safety of a pre-trained driving policy using logged trajectories.
- **Healthcare:** Estimating the long-term outcome of an existing treatment regimen (analogous to the junior doctor analogy in §1.2).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Optimality to Passive Bellman Equation

We start from the general Bellman equation for any stationary policy $\pi$:

$$U^{\pi}(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s,\pi(s))\, U^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 1 — Expand the expectation.** The utility of $s$ equals the immediate reward plus the discounted expected utility of the next state reached by following $\pi$.

**Step 2 — Recognize linearity.** Since $\pi$ is fixed, $\pi(s)$ is a single deterministic (or fixed-probability) action. Thus the sum reduces to a single weighted sum over $s'$.

**Step 3 — Exploit Markov property.** The future depends only on the current state $s$ — history before $s$ is irrelevant. This allows the recursive structure above.

**Step 4 — In vector form.** Letting $\mathbf{U}$ be the column vector of utilities and $\mathbf{P}^{\pi}$ the $\vert S\vert \times \vert S\vert$ transition matrix under $\pi$:

$$\mathbf{U}^{\pi} = \mathbf{R} + \gamma \mathbf{P}^{\pi} \mathbf{U}^{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically.** Rearranging:

$$(\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P}^{\pi}) \mathbf{U}^{\pi} = \mathbf{R}$$

$$\mathbf{U}^{\pi} = (\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P}^{\pi})^{-1} \mathbf{R}$$

This is the **closed-form solution** used by ADP when the model is known exactly.

## 3.2 Derivation: TD(0) Update Rule from Sample-Based Bellman Approximation

**Step 1 — Recall the Bellman target for $s$:**

$$U^{\pi}(s) \approx R(s) + \gamma\, E[U^{\pi}(s')]$$

**Step 2 — Replace the expectation with a single sample.** On observing transition $s \xrightarrow{r} s'$:

$$U^{\pi}(s) \approx r + \gamma\, U^{\pi}(s')$$

This is the **TD target**.

**Step 3 — Formulate a stochastic gradient-descent update.** The squared error is:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(U(s) - (r + \gamma U(s'))\bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $U(s)$.** The gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial U(s)} = U(s) - (r + \gamma U(s'))$$

**Step 5 — Apply gradient descent with step size $\alpha$:**

$$U(s) \leftarrow U(s) - \alpha \cdot \bigl(U(s) - (r + \gamma U(s'))\bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to the canonical TD form:**

$$U(s) \leftarrow U(s) + \alpha \bigl(r + \gamma U(s') - U(s)\bigr)$$

This is the famous **TD(0) update rule**. The bracketed term is the **TD error** $\delta_t$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Incremental Sample-Average Update (DUE)

Let $u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n$ be the observed returns starting from $s$. The sample average is:

$$\widehat{U}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} u_k$$

Separate the last term:

$$\widehat{U}_n = \frac{1}{n}\left( u_n + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} u_k \right) = \frac{1}{n}\left( u_n + (n-1)\widehat{U}_{n-1} \right)$$

Expanding:

$$\widehat{U}_n = \frac{n-1}{n}\widehat{U}_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n} u_n = \widehat{U}_{n-1} - \frac{1}{n}\widehat{U}_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n} u_n$$

$$\boxed{\widehat{U}_n = \widehat{U}_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n}\bigl( u_n - \widehat{U}_{n-1} \bigr)}$$

This is the **incremental sample-average** update — a special case of TD where the learning rate $\alpha = 1/n$ and we use the *actual* total return $u_n$ rather than a one-step bootstrap.

## 3.4 Convergence & Sample-Complexity Comparison

| Method | Convergence Rate | Sample Efficiency | Computational Cost | Model Needed? |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| DUE | $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ (slow, high variance) | Low (each state learned independently) | $O(1)$ per update | No |
| ADP | $O(\beta^{\,k})$ where $\beta=\gamma<1$ over sweeps | High (Bellman constraints) | $O(\vert S\vert^{2}\vert A\vert)$ per sweep | Yes (learned) |
| TD(0) | $O(1/n)$ with appropriate $\alpha$ decay | Medium | $O(1)$ per update | No |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU viva voce, students are often asked: *"Why is ADP faster than TD(0)?"* The answer: **ADP uses the full Bellman constraint set** at every iteration (global update), while TD(0) performs **local, sample-by-sample updates**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 2-state MDP with $S=\{s_1, s_2\}$, fixed policy $\pi$, $\gamma = 0.5$. Suppose we observe the following sequence of rewards from $s_1$: $r=10$ followed by $s_2$ with $r=4$ (terminal). Compute the observed return $u$ and show one DUE update step.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the return-to-go from $s_1$:**

$$u = r_0 + \gamma r_1 = 10 + 0.5 \times 4 = 12$$

**Step 2 — Suppose the current DUE estimate is $\widehat{U}(s_1) = 8$, and this is the $n = 4$-th visit to $s_1$.** Apply the incremental update:

$$\widehat{U}_{4}(s_1) = 8 + \frac{1}{4}\bigl( 12 - 8 \bigr) = 8 + 1 = 9$$

**Step 3 — TD(0) alternative.** With the same transition $(s_1 \xrightarrow{10} s_2)$ and $\widehat{U}(s_2) = 6$, $\alpha = 0.2$:

$$U(s_1) \leftarrow 8 + 0.2 \cdot (10 + 0.5 \cdot 6 - 8) = 8 + 0.2 \cdot 5 = 9$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Passive RL Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment MDP] -->|Provides s, r| B[Sensor / Perception Module]
    B --> C[Fixed Policy pi]
    C -->|Selects action a| D[Action Actuator]
    D -->|Executes a| A
    A -->|Next state s prime| B
    B --> E[Experience Buffer]
    E --> F1[Direct Utility Estimation Module]
    E --> F2[Adaptive Dynamic Programming Module]
    E --> F3[Temporal Difference Learning Module]
    F1 --> G[Utility Estimates U pi s]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Performance Monitor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The key architectural fact to highlight: the **policy block is upstream and unchanged**; only the **learning modules** are active. The agent never alters the action selected at block `C`.

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart of the Three Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Start: Observe s] --> decide{Approach?}
    decide -->|DUE| due[Average observed returns per state]
    decide -->|ADP| adp[Learn P and R model]
    decide -->|TD0| td[Bootstrap from successor estimate]
    due --> dueUpdate[Update U with sample mean]
    adp --> adpStep1[Estimate transition probabilities]
    adpStep1 --> adpStep2[Estimate reward function]
    adpStep2 --> adpStep3[Solve Bellman equations iteratively]
```
